  561. Reuters, “Novo Nordisk resumes shipments of all Wegovy doses in U.S.” (Dec 23, 2022); FDA Drug Shortages database entries for semaglutide and tirzepatide 2022–2025; Novo Nordisk Annual Report 2024 (Wegovy DKK 58.2B / ~$8.4B; Ozempic DKK 120.3B / ~$17.5B); Eli Lilly Q4 2024 financial results press release (Feb 6, 2025), Mounjaro $11.5B + Zepbound $4.9B = $16.4B 2024 GLP-1 revenue; Lilly capacity capex announcements 2022–2025 (Indianapolis $2.5B, Concord NC $1B, Mt. Pleasant IN $5.3B, LEAP IN $9B = $17.8B+ committed). Cited in: Ch 8
  562. RevenueCat. State of Subscription Apps 2025 . RevenueCat, 2025. Paywall-placement A/B tests across 28,000 subscription apps; the at-limit placement converts 3-4 times the first-open rate. Cited in: Ch 14
  563. Richard Normann, Service Management (Wiley, 1984), primary source for the “moment of truth” phrase coined in Normann’s late-1970s SAS consulting work and the academic counterpart to Carlzon’s 1987 memoir. A.G. Lafley, 2005 P&G introduction of the First and Second Moments of Truth. Jim Lecinski, Winning the Zero Moment of Truth (Google ebook, 2011), with the 2011 ZMOT Macro Study (Shopper Sciences for Google, n=5,000 across 12 categories: 10.4 information sources per decision up from 5.3 in 2010, 84% of shoppers reporting ZMOT shaped their decision). Cited in: Ch 9
  564. Richard S. Sutton. (2019, March 13). “The Bitter Lesson.” incompleteideas.net. incompleteideas.net/IncIdeas/BitterLesson . The three-page essay distilling seventy years of AI research into a single claim: the methods that win in the long run are the general-purpose methods that scale with computation, which Sutton names as search and learning, and the methods that lose are those that try to build human knowledge into the system by hand. Four worked examples: computer chess (Deep Blue 1997, alpha-beta search at scale), computer Go (AlphaGo 2016 and AlphaGo Zero 2017, Monte Carlo tree search plus self-play), speech recognition (statistical methods replacing 1970s linguistic grammars, then deep learning replacing the statistical methods), computer vision (convolutional neural networks replacing hand-engineered feature pipelines after AlexNet 2012). Association for Computing Machinery, “Andrew Barto and Richard Sutton are the recipients of the 2024 ACM A.M. Turing Award,” March 5, 2025, awards.acm.org/about/2024-turing (citation: “for developing the conceptual and algorithmic foundations of reinforcement learning,” $1 million prize sponsored by Google; the citation lists reinforcement learning from human feedback, AlphaGo, robot motor-skill learning, microprocessor and circuit design, and personalized recommendations on Netflix and YouTube as application domains). Sutton and Barto, Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction , 2nd ed. (MIT Press, 2018; freely available at incompleteideas.net/book/the-book-2nd ) is the standard graduate textbook, cited more than 75,000 times. David Silver and Richard S. Sutton, “Welcome to the Era of Experience,” DeepMind essay, April 2025, storage.googleapis.com/deepmind-media/Era-of-Experience : the 2025 extension arguing the next generation of capability gains will come from agents that learn from their own interaction with environments rather than from imitating human-written data. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sutton-barto-bitter-lesson-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  565. Richard S. Tedlow, New and Improved: The Story of Mass Marketing in America (Basic Books, 1990), four-case framework: Coca-Cola versus Pepsi, Ford versus General Motors, A&P versus Sears, Montgomery Ward. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/tedlow-new-and-improved-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  566. Ries and Trout, Positioning (2001), 1981 text plus 2001 marginalia. Cited in: Ch 6
  567. Ritson, Mark, “CMOs Without Finance Fluency Don’t Survive Their First Recession,” Marketing Week , March 2024; follow-up column, “Read Your P&L Like a Banker,” Marketing Week , October 2024. Cited in: Ch 25
  568. Roberge, M. (2015). The Sales Acceleration Formula . Wiley. Cited in: Ch 23
  569. Robert B. Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion (William Morrow, 1984; expanded ed. Harper Business, 2021), the six original principles (reciprocity, commitment, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity); Cialdini, Pre-Suasion (Simon & Schuster, 2016), introducing the seventh principle (unity). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/cialdini-influence-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  570. Robert B. Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion , expanded ed. (Harper Business, 2021). Cited in: Ch 26
  571. Robert B. Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion, New and Expanded (Harper Business, 2021); Cialdini, Pre-Suasion: A Revolutionary Way to Influence and Persuade (Simon & Schuster, 2016). Six principles canonized in 1984 (reciprocity, commitment-consistency, social proof, authority, liking, scarcity); seventh (unity) introduced 2016 and canonized in the 2021 edition. Canonical experimental anchors: Regan 1971 (raffle-ticket reciprocity), Strohmetz et al. 2002 (restaurant-mint reciprocity, single mint +3%, two mints with personalization +23%), Freedman and Fraser 1966 (foot-in-the-door commitment-consistency, +400%), Goldstein, Cialdini, and Griskevicius 2008 (hotel-towel-reuse descriptive-norm social proof, +33%), Worchel, Lee, and Adewole 1975 (cookie-jar scarcity). 30-minute principle-audit workflow synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/cialdini-influence-7-principles-ch04.md with full relationship-form mapping, intensity sweet-spot heuristics, and the dual-use ethics frame applied across the running brand roster. Combined works 7M+ copies, 44 languages. Cited in: Ch 4
  572. Robert G. Cooper, Winning at New Products , 5e (Basic Books, 2017); Cooper, “Perspective: The Stage-Gate Idea-to-Launch Process,” JPIM 25:3 (2008); Cooper and Sommer, “The Agile-Stage-Gate Hybrid Model,” JPIM 33:5 (2016). NewProd, APQC, PDMA CPAS benchmarking series 1976–2021. Cited in: Ch 8
  573. Robert J. House et al., eds., Culture, Leadership, and Organizations: The GLOBE Study of 62 Societies (Sage, 2004), the nine-dimension framework extending Hofstede (17,300 managers, 951 orgs). As-Is vs Should-Be split produces negative correlation across 7 of 9 dimensions. GLOBE 2020 covers 150+ countries. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/globe-9-dimensions-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  574. Robert McKee, Story (ReganBooks/HarperCollins, 1997); McKee and Gerace, Storynomics: Story-Driven Marketing in the Post-Advertising World (Twelve/Hachette, 2018). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/mckee-storynomics-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  575. Robert Rose and Joe Pulizzi, Killing Marketing: How Innovative Businesses Are Turning Marketing Cost Into Profit (McGraw-Hill, 2017); CMI 2026 B2B benchmarks Section 7 on gating practices (ref-3). Cited in: Ch 17
  576. Robert W. Palmatier and Shrihari Sridhar, Marketing Strategy: Based on First Principles and Data Analytics , 2nd ed. (Bloomsbury, 2021), the diffusion-compression argument at core-course/markdown/02-strategy/marketing-strategy-first-principles-palmatier-2e.md:3313 ; Sharp, Dawes, Vaughan, and Driesener, Marketing: Theory, Evidence, Practice , 2nd ed. (OUP, 2017), the Mars Russia 1998 case at core-course/markdown/01-foundations/marketing-theory-evidence-practice-sharp-2e.md:2144-2172 ; deep-corpus reader at core-course/wiki/external/marketing-environment-deep.md . Cited in: Ch 3 Ch 6
  577. Robert W. Palmatier and V. Kumar, Marketing Strategy: Based on First Principles and Data Analytics (Palgrave Macmillan, 2017), Ch. 5 (customer lifetime value, retention math); Costco 2024 annual report. Cited in: Ch 2
  578. Roger J. Best, Market-Based Management , 6th ed. (Pearson, 2013), Ch. 5. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/best-market-based-management-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  579. Roger L. Martin, “Revisiting My Definition of Strategy,” Roger Martin Substack , 2025; “The Strategic Leverage of Where to Play,” Medium , 2025; “Why the How-to-Win Strategy Choice Is So Hard,” Medium , 2025; “Playing to Win: Pivoting,” Medium , 2025; Lenny's Newsletter , “The Ultimate Guide to Strategy: Roger Martin” (2024). The 2025 customer-action reframing of the 2013 cascade and the four-part falsifiability test (customer / action / circumstance / alternative) developed across the essay sequence and the long-form interview, with the P&G Olay, Frito-Lay, and Four Seasons revisitations. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/roger-martin-revisiting-strategy-2025-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  580. Ron Adner, The Wide Lens: A New Strategy for Innovation (Portfolio, 2012); revised paperback 2013 as The Wide Lens: What Successful Innovators See That Others Miss . Adner, Winning the Right Game: How to Disrupt, Defend, and Deliver in a Changing World (MIT Press, 2021). Strategy Insight Group, Tuck School of Business. Michelin PAX 1998–2007 and Philips HDTV mid-1980s 2.5B write-down per Wide Lens Chs 1–3. Cited in: Ch 8
  581. Ross, Aaron, & Tyler, Marylou. (2011). Predictable Revenue . PebbleStorm. Generalized in Ross & Lemkin (2016), From Impossible to Inevitable , Wiley. Cited in: Ch 23
  582. Ruha Benjamin, Race After Technology: Abolitionist Tools for the New Jim Code (Polity, 2019). Four-mode taxonomy of discriminatory design: engineered inequity, default discrimination, coded exposure, technological benevolence. FTC 6(b) Algorithmic Pricing Market Study (staff findings January 17, 2025) on precise location, mouse-movement, and demographic inputs producing differential prices; FTC 2025-2026 §5(a) enforcement escalation against algorithmic discrimination in hiring, credit, and tenant screening. Cited in: Ch 26
  583. Runway. Live App Store and TestFlight Review Times . Runway, 2026. Crowdsourced 2026 review-time tracker; ~90 percent of submissions reviewed within 24 hours, complex apps 2-5 days. runway.team/appreviewtimes . Cited in: Ch 14
  584. Sacra, “Liquid Death revenue, valuation & growth rate” (2024-2025 financials, $333M 2024 revenue, $1.4B March 2024 valuation); Marketing Brew, “Liquid Death’s social marketing strategy” (January 2026) for share-rate and 3:1 shares-to-likes; Novel, “Liquid Death Wallet Pass” case study (200,000-plus Country Club enrollments); ASI Central, “Marketing is Easy, Entertainment is Hard” (March 2026) Cessario interview. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/liquid-death-customer-relationship-2024-2026-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  585. Sacra, “Liquid Death revenue, valuation & growth rate” (2024-2025); Adage, “Liquid Death Super Bowl LIX Safe for Work ” (2025); Food Dive, “Liquid Death to launch energy drinks” (2025); Beverage Daily , “Setting the right price for a premium beverage” (September 4, 2025); TapTwice Digital, “Liquid Death Statistics 2025” (133,000 retail doors, ~$340M projected 2025 revenue); Beverage Industry, “Liquid Death closes $67 million Series D” ($1.4B post-money valuation, March 11, 2024); Liquid Death press release, Sparkling Energy launch announcement (January 2026, four flavors at ~100 mg caffeine per 12-oz can). Cited in: Ch 6
  586. Safiya Umoja Noble, Algorithms of Oppression: How Search Engines Reinforce Racism (NYU Press, 2018). Cited in: Ch 26
  587. Salesforce. (2025). Agentforce: Autonomous Agents on Einstein and Data Cloud . Product documentation and Q4 2025 earnings disclosures. HubSpot. (2026, April 14). Breeze AI Agents: Outcome-Based Pricing . Product announcement. Cited in: Ch 23
  588. Salesforce. (2026). FY26 Q4 Earnings Disclosures: Agentforce + Data 360 ARR . HubSpot. (2024, September). Breeze Agents Launch at INBOUND . Outreach. (2025, March 26). AI Agents Across Revenue Workflows . Klarna. Q3 2024 service-cost disclosures and CEO Siemiatkowski’s May 8, 2025 walk-back; CNBC, May 14, 2025. Cited in: Ch 23
  589. Sam Altman. (2025, February 9). Three Observations. blog.samaltman.com/three-observations . Three claims: model intelligence scales with the log of training-and-inference resources; the cost of a given AI capability falls about 10x every 12 months (anchored in the ~150x GPT-4 to GPT-4o token-price decline, early 2023 to mid-2024); the socioeconomic value of linearly increasing intelligence is super-exponential. Federal Reserve update, July 22, 2025: scientists at 2x to 3x throughput, software engineers at 10x. Same-day critique (Hacker News 42993987 and elsewhere): Kurzweil-trend, one-year-data, and benchmark-gaming objections. Empirical floor in Anthropic Economic Index Jan 2026: implied labor-productivity gains of ~1.0 to 1.2 pp/year, an order of magnitude below the framework if uniformly applied. Antecedent at moores.samaltman.com (March 16, 2021): biennial-50-percent-cost-decline framing for goods and services, narrowed in 2025 to AI specifically. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-altman-three-observations-2025-ch01.md and ai-altman-moores-law-for-everything-2021-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1
  590. Sam Walton with John Huey, Made in America: My Story (Doubleday, 1992); MWPVL 2024 Walmart distribution-network analysis; Quartr 2024 on cross-docking and the 1983 satellite network. Cited in: Ch 11
  591. Sandler, David H. with John Hayes. (1995, 2014 second edition updated by David Mattson). You Can’t Teach a Kid to Ride a Bike at a Seminar . McGraw-Hill, amazon.com/Cant-Teach-Ride-Bike-Seminar . Origin text of the Sandler Selling System: the seven-stage Submarine (Bonding-and-Rapport, Up-Front Contract, Pain, Budget, Decision, Fulfillment, Post-Sell) and the rule that the seller cannot back up to a previous compartment once it has been closed; the three-level Pain Funnel walking the buyer from surface pain to business pain to personal pain across roughly eight progressively deeper open-ended questions; the Up-Front Contract framing the meeting’s purpose, both sides’ agendas, the duration, and the three acceptable outcomes (yes, no, or a scheduled next step with a defined purpose) before any selling happens. Mattson, David. (2009). The Sandler Rules: 49 Timeless Selling Principles and How to Apply Them . Pegasus Media World, amazon.com/Sandler-Rules-Timeless-Selling-Principles (the methodology distilled into forty-nine named selling principles). Origin and franchise history: David Harvey Sandler born November 4, 1930 in Maryland, designed the framework between 1966 and 1967 in collaboration with a clinical psychologist who grounded the seller-buyer dynamic in transactional-analysis psychology rather than in hard-close tactics, began teaching the system commercially in 1967, and expanded it as the Sandler Sales Institute in 1983 through a franchise model that has since grown to roughly 230 offices in about 30 countries ( sandler.com/david-sandler ; sandler.com/about ; sandler.com/blog/introducing-new-sandler-submarine ; sandler.com/dave-mattson ). David H. Sandler died September 13, 1995 at age 64 in Stevenson, Maryland; David Mattson, who joined as a participant in the 1980s and rose through the franchise network, became chief executive officer in 2007 and executive chairman in 2024. Practitioner-side coverage of the methodology mechanics: Salesforce, salesforce.com/blog/sales/sandler-sales-methodology ; HubSpot, blog.hubspot.com/sales/sandler-sales-methodology (with HubSpot’s practitioner-reported finding that applying Sandler discipline to its inside sales team raised average selling price by twelve percent and shortened the sales cycle by eighteen percent); Gong, gong.io/blog/sandler-selling-method and gong.io/blog/sandler-pain-funnel ; Salesmate, salesmate.io/blog/sandler-pain-funnel and salesmate.io/blog/sandler-sales-methodology ; OpenView Partners, openviewpartners.com/blog/sandler-selling-the-3-levels-of-pain ; HubSpot Pain Funnel page, blog.hubspot.com/sales/pain-funnel . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sandler-selling-system-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  592. Schneier, Data and Goliath (Norton, 2015); Click Here to Kill Everybody (Norton, 2018). Dual-surveillance synthesis (corporate, state, criminal). Schneier 2025 retrospective, The Register , February 17, 2025. Breach economics: IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2024 ($4.88M global, $4.45M retail, $9.77M healthcare); National Public Data 2.9B records (August 2024); Change Healthcare 190M records (April 2024). Cited in: Ch 26
  593. Schultz and Schultz, IMC: The Next Generation , McGraw-Hill, 2003; integrated marketing communications across channels rather than each silo. Cited in: Ch 24

(Pearson, 2013) and 12th ed. (Pearson, 2022). The four-step process (research, planning, communication, evaluation) and the management-function definition of PR. Wiki synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/cutlip-center-broom-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22

  596. Freshness anchor (May 2026): core-course/wiki/external/_freshness-anchors.md . Cited in: Ch 11 Ch 12
  597. Sean Ellis and Morgan Brown, Hacking Growth: How Today's Fastest-Growing Companies Drive Breakout Success (Crown Business, 2017), Chs 1–9: cross-functional growth team, North Star Metric, AARRR funnel (Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Revenue, Referral) after Dave McClure 2007, ICE scoring (Impact, Confidence, Ease), forty-percent product-market-fit rule. Dropbox referral program 2008–2010 (~60 percent of new users); Hotmail signature-line referral ~12 million users in 18 months; Airbnb Craigslist-integration acquisition. GrowthHackers community archive (Ellis, founded 2013). Compendium at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/ellis-brown-hacking-growth-ch08.md . Cited in: Ch 8
  598. Sean Ellis, “The Startup Pyramid,” startup-marketing.com, May 2010 (40 percent “very disappointed” threshold). Rahul Vohra, “How Superhuman Built an Engine to Find Product/Market Fit,” First Round Review , 2018. Marc Andreessen, “The Only Thing That Matters,” pmarchive, June 2007. Cited in: Ch 8

  604. Shanghai Disney Resort 2016-2026: The Walt Disney Company corporate press release, “Shanghai Disney Resort Expansion,” Nov. 2025 (fourth themed hotel, retail-dining-entertainment district expansion, 100M cumulative-visitor milestone); Shanghai Disney Resort corporate press release, Nov. 2025 (resort-expansion announcement coinciding with the 100M milestone); Themed Entertainment Association and AECOM, TEA/AECOM Theme Index 2024 , published Oct. 2025 (Shanghai Disneyland 14.7M annual visitors, 5th globally, 1st in China, 5% YoY growth among top 10); Xinhua, Oct. 24, 2025 and China Daily , Oct. 24, 2025 (100M cumulative milestone coverage); IAAPA Funworld , “Zootopia Land Debuts at Shanghai Disney Resort,” Dec. 2023; Global Times , Dec. 2023 (Zootopia Land opening, Hot Pursuit trackless dark ride, Mandarin-Shanghainese linguistic adaptation); China Speakers Bureau, Dec. 1, 2025 (Ashley Dudarenok, Zootopia cultural-relevance analysis and Shanghai Animation Film Studio four-style promotional-short collaboration). Shanghai Shendi Group equity (resort 43/57 Disney-Shendi, operating company 70/30) from Disney 10-K filings. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/shanghai-disneyland-2016-2026-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  605. Sharp 2014, Ch. 11; J. Romaniuk, Building Distinctive Brand Assets (Oxford University Press, 2018). Distinctive-asset definition: non-name elements that buyers in a category recognize as belonging to one brand without needing the name. Cited in: Ch 7
  606. Sharp 2014, Ch. 5; Sharp and Romaniuk (2007) Pareto-share study across CPG: top 20% of buyers contribute ~50% of annual volume (range 42% shampoo to 64% soft drinks). Cited in: Ch 7
  607. Sharp 2014, Ch. 6; Sharp 2e, Ch. 8. Duplication of purchase law: a brand's buyers also buy other brands in proportion to those brands' market shares, not perceived similarity. Cited in: Ch 7
  608. Sharp 2014, Ch. 8. Audit of buyer attitudes across 15 categories: roughly 11% of buyers describe their preferred brand as "different" from competitors. Replicated in Millward Brown BrandZ (>6,000 brands, 10 years) at ~18%. Cited in: Ch 7
  609. Sharp 2018, Ch. 5; Sharp 2e, Figure 1.1 (line 1446). The two market-based assets (mental and physical availability) and their multiplicative effect on growth. Cited in: Ch 7
  610. Sharp 2e, line 3800 distinguishes mental availability from brand awareness as a measurement construct: awareness measures yes/no recognition, mental availability measures noticeability across many cues. Hoyer 7e, line 5389, defines recognition versus recall as separate constructs. Cited in: Ch 7
  611. Sharp, ibid., Chapters 1-4 on mental availability, distinctiveness vs differentiation, and the empirical finding that across roughly fifteen categories only about eleven percent of buyers see their preferred brand as different. Cited in: Ch 16
  612. Shein 2024-2026 cross-border DTC trajectory: SCMP (Apr. 2025) and Axios (Apr. 9, 2025) on the May 2, 2025 China-origin and August 29, 2025 global U.S. de minimis closures and ~$10B Q1 2025 revenue pull-forward; Business of Apps (2025) on $32.5B 2023 revenue and ~8% YoY September 2025 U.S. sales decline; WWD Sourcing Journal and Business of Fashion (2025) on the Brazilian production-hub failure (336 partner factories late 2023, one remaining producer mid-2025, $150M / 750M-real announcement targeting 85% local production by end-2026); TechCrunch (Feb. 1, 2025) and Rest of World (2025) on the India re-entry via Reliance Retail with full customer-data delegation and government-approved cybersecurity audits; France 24 (Nov. 5, 2025) on the marketplace suspension and Euronews (Dec. 19, 2025) on the Paris-court rejection of the French government's full-platform suspension request. EU Council de minimis closure: Council of the EU press release IP/26/0211, “Customs: Council gives final green light to new customs duty rules for small parcels,” Feb. 11, 2026 (3-euro fixed per-item customs duty on parcels under 150 euro from July 1, 2026, bridging to the November 2025 permanent removal of the 150-euro relief threshold); Council press release IP/25/1212, Dec. 12, 2025 (initial agreement); EU Commission Taxation and Customs Union announcement, Nov. 13, 2025 (permanent removal). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/shein-cross-border-dtc-2024-2026-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15

The 2025 AI Index Report, organizational-adoption chapter. 78% of organizations reported using AI in 2024 (up from 55% the year before); 71% regularly use generative AI specifically (up from 33% in 2023). Capability scores rose 18.8, 48.9, and 67.3 percentage points on MMMU, GPQA, and SWE-bench, respectively, year over year. hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2025-ai-index-report . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-the-stack-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1

Nvidia single-day market-cap loss reported in CNBC, January 27, 2025. Cited in: Ch 1

Companion: Rocket Surgery Made Easy (New Riders 2010). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/krug-dont-make-me-think-ch18.md . Cited in: Ch 18
  637. Stewart, David W., Marketing Accountability: Linking Marketing Actions to Financial Results , 2nd ed. (Routledge, 2024), on the three-room budget-allocation flow inside Fortune-500 firms. Cited in: Ch 25
  638. Stuart Elliott, "Tropicana Discovers Some Buyers Are Passionate About Packaging," The New York Times , February 23, 2009; Ferrell, Hartline, and Hochstein, Marketing Strategy , 8th ed., Ch 3, line 3559. Cited in: Ch 3
  639. Stuart Elliott, "Tropicana Discovers Some Buyers Are Passionate About Packaging," The New York Times , February 23, 2009; Pepsi/Arnell Group internal post-mortem reported in AdAge , "Tropicana Line's Sales Plunge 20 percent Post-Rebranding," April 2009. Cited in: Ch 16
  640. Stuart Elliott, “Tropicana Discovers Some Buyers Are Passionate About Packaging,” The New York Times , February 23, 2009; PepsiCo Q1 2009 earnings call commentary on Tropicana Pure Premium reversion. Cited in: Ch 8
  641. Stuart J. Russell and Peter Norvig. (2020). Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach , 4th ed. (Pearson). Chapter 2 establishes the four-element agent specification (performance measure, environment, actuators, sensors) and the five agent architectures (simple reflex, model-based reflex, goal-based, utility-based, learning) that classify every system from a thermostat to a 2026 LLM agent loop. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/russell-norvig-aima-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1
  642. Stuart Russell. (2019). Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control . Viking. The standard model (optimizing a fixed objective) fails at the limit because every human-specified objective is incomplete and a capable optimizer exploits the incompleteness; Russell calls this the King Midas problem. Replacement: altruism, humility, empirical preference learning. Operational consequence for a 2026 marketing team is corrigibility: every deployed agent should be uncertain enough to ask before borderline outputs, deferential enough to stop on a flag, easy to switch off. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/stuart-russell-human-compatible-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1
  643. Susan Boyle audition, Britain's Got Talent , Apr 11, 2009; YouTube reach via Google Trends and YouTube Insights archive. Cited in: Ch 19
  644. Susan C. Schneider, Jean-Louis Barsoux, and Günter K. Stahl, Managing Across Cultures , 3rd ed. (Harlow: Pearson Education / Financial Times Prentice Hall, 2014), ISBN 978-0-273-74632-4, the practitioner-canon companion to Hofstede, Trompenaars, GLOBE, and Hall. The book's central operational contribution is the interacting spheres of culture framework (national, regional, industry, corporate, functional), borrowed from a diagram by Jose Santos at INSEAD and reproduced in figure form across editions. Pearson UK 3rd-edition product page at https://www.pearson.com/en-gb/subject-catalog/p/Schneider-Managing-Across-Cultures-3rd-Edition/P200000005378/9780273746324 ; Internet Archive scan of the 3rd edition at https://archive.org/details/managingacrosscu0000schn_3edi ; Santos diagram (Schneider-Barsoux 2003) reproduced at https://www.researchgate.net/figure/Interacting-cultural-spheres-of-influence-Source-Santos-in-Schneider-Barsoux_fig2_200149815 ; Jean-Louis Barsoux faculty page at IMD Lausanne at https://www.imd.org/faculty/professors/jean-louis-barsoux/ . Daimler-Chrysler 1998-2007 case analyzed across multiple editions as a corporate-and-functional-sphere failure. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schneider-barsoux-stahl-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  645. Talbott, R., “Indianapolis Zoo: A Case Study in Dynamic Pricing,” Pricing Solutions Limited (Toronto), 2010 (engagement led by Paul Hunt; 2009 implementation; calendar-grid replacement of single $16.95 adult-pass price with $8 Tuesday-floor to $30 Saturday-peak; weekday attendance share 57→67%; revenue +12% against prior-year baseline; institutionalized as standing policy and replicated at Cincinnati Zoo, Detroit Zoo, and major-aquarium peers). The canonical yield-management case for mission-driven institutions; pairs with Bertini-Koenigsberg HBR May 2024 six-principle audit (ref-56) on the better-than-static and transparency principles that distinguish the rollout from the Wendy's-pattern reversal. Synthesis at canonical/indianapolis-zoo-dynamic-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10

  661. Thinkbox / Ebiquity / Gain Theory, Profit Ability 2: The new business case for advertising , April 2024; mixed-marketing-model meta-analysis across 141 UK brands, 14 sectors, 10 media channels, ~£1.8B of 2021-2023 spend (with EssenceMediacom, Mindshare, Wavemaker). Average short-term ROI £1.87, full-payback £4.11 (24 months), 60/40 long/short profit split. Channel ROIs: linear TV £5.94 full / 46.6% of total profit; TV combined £5.61 / 54.7%; online video £3.86 / 3.4%. Saturation thresholds (weekly): linear TV ~£330k, print ~£110k, online video ~£40k. Sector full-payback ROI: automotive £4.65, financial services £1.95, large retail short-term £3.15, travel short-term £1.19. Course-internal synthesis: frontier/thinkbox-profit-ability-2-2024-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16

  670. Tom M. Mitchell. (1997). Machine Learning . McGraw-Hill. The well-posed-learning-problem definition (Ch. 1, p. 2): a program learns from experience E on tasks T measured by P if its performance at T, as measured by P, improves with E. The T/P/E triple is the shortest vendor-evaluation card for any system that claims to learn. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/mitchell-machine-learning-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1

Cited in: Ch 15
  686. U.S. GAO, Consumer Prices: Trends and Policy Options Related to Shrinking Product Sizes , GAO-25-107451 (Sep 2025); Casey-Warren S.3819 (Feb 2024); H.R.9279 (Deluzio-Gluesenkamp Perez, Aug 2024); Allou, “Doritos’ 2021 Downsizing,” Yale Econ paper (May 2025); General Mills slack-fill class action (Dec 2025); Mondelez Cadbury 200g→180g (May 2024). Synthesis at frontier/ai-grocery-shrinkflation-2024-2026-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  687. U.S. Senate Special Committee on Aging hearings on EpiPen and Daraprim pricing (2016); Reuters on Mylan EpiPen 2007-2016; NYT Sep 2015 on Turing Pharmaceuticals. Cited in: Ch 10
  688. Van Westendorp, "NSS-Price Sensitivity Meter," 29th ESOMAR Congress (1976); Nagle, Hogan, Zale, The Strategy and Tactics of Pricing , 6e (Routledge, 2016) Ch 12; Lipovetsky, "Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity in Statistical Modeling," IJOQM 12, 2 (2006). Cited in: Ch 10
  689. VCCP Media and K. Nelson-Field (Amplified Intelligence and University of Adelaide / Loughborough University), “Hacking the Attention Economy,” industry report (May 2025), VCCP press release ; Creative Salon ; The Media Leader ; The Drum ; Marketing Week . Methodology: webcam-based eye-tracking on consenting panelists watching advertisements inside a normal browsing environment (TikTok, Instagram Reels, and YouTube Shorts viewing experience approximated), recording active attention as the share of an ad's runtime during which the viewer's eyes were oriented toward the ad (versus the legacy IAB viewability metric of 50 percent of pixels on-screen for two seconds). Sample: 72 digital video advertisements from O2, Cadbury, Domino's, Old El Paso, easyJet, Sage, Bulldog, and White Claw, ~20,000 viewer exposures total; within-creative comparison of strong-asset versus weakly-branded versions of the same creative isolates the asset-attention interaction. Headline empirical findings: (a) 1.5 seconds of active attention is sufficient to encode an advertisement in viewer memory provided the advertisement is anchored to a strong distinctive brand asset on the first frame; (b) approximately 75 percent of digital ad inventory rated viewable under the legacy viewability metric generates zero active attention; (c) the “distinctive asset tariff” on weakly-branded inventory reaches up to 66 pence per £1 of UK digital-video spend, or roughly 43 cents per $1 in the US market, equivalent to ~$198 billion of US industry-wide wastage in 2024; (d) well-branded advertisements were 2.5x more effective than weakly-branded advertisements at producing measurable brand outcomes in low-attention environments; (e) a single strong brand code delivered a 3.5x return on attention-adjusted ROI compared with its unbranded counterpart, holding the rest of the creative constant. Programme context: K. Nelson-Field, Attention!: How to Buy Attention That Works to Drive Your Brand (2023) and the 2017-2025 Amplified Intelligence attention-research programme; standardization through the Australian Association of National Advertisers' AANA Attention Council and the IAB Attention Measurement Guidelines (2024). Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/nelson-field-attention-economy-2025-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  690. Vincent E. Kunst, “Multidimensionality vs. Parsimony in Institutional Distance Research,” CCSM (online Dec 2, 2025): 236 papers across 28 IB journals 2009-2024; only WGI converges psychometrically; Hofstede, Berry, EFI fail. Cited in: Ch 15
  691. Virginia Eubanks, Automating Inequality (St. Martin's Press, 2018). Three-jurisdiction ethnography: Indiana eligibility automation 2006-2008 (IBM-ACS $1.3B; tripled denial-error rate), LA VI-SPDAT (2013-), Allegheny Family Screening Tool (Aug 2016). Allegheny County Analytics evaluation, May 2024 (83 percent racial-disparity reduction after human-override). Cited in: Ch 26

four traditional facilities receiving 500,000-plus square feet of automation at Mankato MN, Mebane NC, Garrett IN, Shelbyville TN; one retrofit at Winter Haven FL; high-tech buildings double both case storage and case-processing volume against traditional grocery DCs with an 80-foot-tall automated storage and retrieval system and store-ready pallet build by department with fragile items layered toward the top); Symbotic, “Symbotic Completes Acquisition of Walmart's Advanced Systems and Robotics Business and Signs Related Commercial Agreement,” January 28, 2025 ($200M cash plus up to $350M in milestone payments for the acquisition; $520M development funding commitment with $230M paid at closing for Symbotic-built Gen-2 micro-fulfillment systems at up to 400 in-store Accelerated Pickup and Delivery centers conditional on 2026 prototype KPI achievement); ~20 percent average-unit-cost improvement target; net delivery cost per US e-commerce order already down ~40 percent since program began); Retail Brew, “Walmart's Investment in Automation is Set to Peak in the Coming Year,” February 20, 2026; Retail Dive, “Walmart Plans $330 Million Investment in Louisiana DC,” 2026 (Opelousas LA regional DC modernization doubling shipping capacity; existing 1,900-plus workforce retained and shifted to higher-skilled robotics roles); Brittain Ladd, October 2025 framing of Walmart's last-mile footprint (4,700 US stores within 10 miles of approximately 90 percent of Americans). Zoltners, Sinha, & Lorimer (2006), The Complete Guide to Sales Force Incentive Compensation , AMACOM: six-lever comp-plan framework with the empirical regularity that 60-70% of single-lever comp-plan changes produce unintended behavior. 02 // journal articles

2 Journal Articles and Conference Papers (493)

  1. A. M. Muñiz, Jr., and T. C. O’Guinn, “Brand Community,” JCR 27, no. 4 (2001): 412-432: three markers (consciousness of kind, shared rituals, moral responsibility) recovered ethnographically across Saab, Bronco, Macintosh 1995-1999. Four-relationship extension: McAlexander, Schouten, Koenig, JM 66, no. 1 (2002): 38-54. Twelve practices in four aggregates: Schau, Muñiz, Arnould, JM 73, no. 5 (2009): 30-51. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/muniz-oguinn-brand-communities-canonical-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  2. A. Parasuraman, Valarie A. Zeithaml, and Leonard L. Berry, “A Conceptual Model of Service Quality and Its Implications for Future Research,” Journal of Marketing 49, no. 4 (Fall 1985): 41-50. The original five-gap model of service quality. Cited in: Ch 9
  3. A. Parasuraman, Valarie A. Zeithaml, and Leonard L. Berry, “SERVQUAL: A Multiple-Item Scale for Measuring Consumer Perceptions of Service Quality,” Journal of Retailing 64, no. 1 (Spring 1988): 12-40. The 22-item SERVQUAL measurement instrument and the empirical validation of the five RATER dimensions. Cited in: Ch 9
  4. A.G. Lafley and Roger L. Martin, Playing to Win: How Strategy Really Works (Harvard Business Review Press, 2013), the five-question strategy cascade (winning aspiration, where to play, how to win, capabilities, management systems); Roger L. Martin, “The Big Lie of Strategic Planning,” Harvard Business Review (January-February 2014), and “A Plan Is Not a Strategy,” Harvard Business Review (2014); Martin, A New Way to Think (HBR Press, 2022). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/v3-source-contributions/ch02/roger-martin-playing-to-win.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  5. Aaker and Keller, "Consumer Evaluations of Brand Extensions," JM 54, no. 1 (1990): 27-41. Foundational brand-stretching study; replicated in Park, Milberg, Lawson (1991, JCR ); meta-analyzed in Volckner and Sattler, JM 70, no. 2 (2006): 18-34. Cited in: Ch 7
  6. Abadie and L’Hour, “A Penalized Synthetic Control Estimator for Disaggregated Data,” JASA , vol. 116, no. 536, 2021, pp. 1817–1834; Athey et al., “Matrix Completion Methods for Causal Panel Data Models,” JASA , vol. 116, no. 536, 2021, pp. 1716–1730, R package at github.com/susanathey/MCPanel . Cited in: Ch 5
  7. Abadie, Diamond, and Hainmueller, “Synthetic Control Methods for Comparative Case Studies,” JASA , vol. 105, no. 490, 2010, pp. 493–505; methods review in Abadie, “Using Synthetic Controls,” JEL , vol. 59, no. 2, 2021. Brodersen et al., “Inferring Causal Impact Using Bayesian Structural Time-Series Models,” AoAS , vol. 9, no. 1, 2015, package at google.github.io/CausalImpact . Arkhangelsky et al., “Synthetic Difference-in-Differences,” AER , vol. 111, no. 12, 2021. Meta’s GeoLift at github.com/facebookincubator/GeoLift . Cited in: Ch 5
  8. Aher, Arriaga, and Kalai, “Using Large Language Models to Simulate Multiple Humans and Replicate Human Subject Studies,” Proceedings of the 40th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2023) , PMLR 202: 337–371, arXiv: 2208.10264 ; code at github.com/microsoft/turing-experiments . Cited in: Ch 5
  9. Ajay K. Kohli and Bernard J. Jaworski, “Market Orientation: The Construct, Research Propositions, and Managerial Implications,” Journal of Marketing 54, no. 2 (April 1990): 1-18 (DOI 10.1177/002224299005400201); John C. Narver and Stanley F. Slater, “The Effect of a Market Orientation on Business Profitability,” Journal of Marketing 54, no. 4 (October 1990): 20-35 (DOI 10.1177/002224299005400403). The two foundational 1990 papers that operationalized the marketing concept as a measurable organizational property, the first as a behavior construct (intelligence generation, dissemination, responsiveness) and the second as a culture construct (customer orientation, competitor orientation, interfunctional coordination). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/kohli-jaworski-market-orientation-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  10. Ajay K. Kohli, Bernard J. Jaworski, and Ajith Kumar, “MARKOR: A Measure of Market Orientation,” Journal of Marketing Research 30, no. 4 (November 1993): 467-477 (DOI 10.1177/002224379303000406), introducing the 20-item validated MARKOR instrument with subscales for the three Kohli-Jaworski activities; Bernard J. Jaworski and Ajay K. Kohli, “Market Orientation: Antecedents and Consequences,” Journal of Marketing 57, no. 3 (July 1993): 53-70 (DOI 10.1177/002224299305700304), testing the 1990 framework on business-unit data and identifying top-management, interdepartmental, and organizational-systems antecedents. Meta-analytic evidence: Ahmet H. Kirca, Satish Jayachandran, and William O. Bearden, “Market Orientation: A Meta-Analytic Review and Assessment of Its Antecedents and Impact on Performance,” Journal of Marketing 69, no. 2 (April 2005): 24-41 (DOI 10.1509/jmkg.69.2.24.60761), 114-study meta-analysis with a mean corrected correlation of approximately 0.32 between market orientation and business performance; Cibeles Cano, François A. Carrillat, and Fernando Jaramillo, “A Meta-Analysis of the Relationship Between Market Orientation and Business Performance: Evidence from Five Continents,” International Journal of Research in Marketing 21, no. 2 (2004): 179-200, confirming the relationship across cultures. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/kohli-jaworski-market-orientation-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  11. Çakïr, Liaukonyte, Richards, “Fairness-Market Efficiency Tension in Pricing: Gouging, Greedflation and Price Discrimination,” Cornell SC Johnson College of Business Research Paper Series, SSRN 5188432 (Mar 2025). Structured-review integration of the gouging, greedflation, and personalized-PD literatures under one construct (consumer-perceived legitimacy of the stated reason); foundational empirical record: Richards-Liaukonyte-Streletskaya 2016 IJIO 44 (price-formation participation), Allender-Liaukonyte-Nasser-Richards 2021 Marketing Science (strategic obfuscation), Kominers-Dworczak 2025 SSRN 5104546 (welfare-theoretic gouging-inversion). Synthesis at frontier/cakir-liaukonyte-richards-fairness-market-efficiency-2025-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  12. Alan M. Turing. (1950, October). “Computing Machinery and Intelligence.” Mind , LIX(236), 433–460. The founding paper of artificial intelligence. Section 1 substitutes the metaphysical question (can machines think) with the operational imitation game : an interrogator communicates by typed messages with a human and a machine and the machine passes if the interrogator cannot reliably tell which is which. Turing's stated horizon was a 70 per cent fooling rate by an average interrogator after five minutes of questioning, against a machine of ~10 9 storage bits, by approximately the year 2000. Sections 6.1–6.9 systematically enumerate and rebut nine objections (theological, heads-in-the-sand, mathematical / Gödelian, consciousness, various disabilities, Lady Lovelace, continuity in the nervous system, informality of behaviour, extra-sensory perception); the taxonomy is the same nine a 2026 marketing director encounters in the executive committee. Section 7 proposes the child machine subjected to an education process , predicting the supervised-learning paradigm the field built between 1986 and 2022. Controlled studies in 2024–2025 reported frontier models clearing the 70 per cent threshold in three-party Turing-test variants: Cameron Jones and Benjamin Bergen (UC San Diego), “People cannot distinguish GPT-4 from a human in a Turing test,” arXiv:2405.08007, May 2024; follow-up paper March 2025 on GPT-4.5. academic.oup.com/mind/article/LIX/236/433 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/turing-computing-machinery-intelligence-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  13. Alexander Meinke et al. (Apollo Research). “Frontier Models are Capable of In-Context Scheming.” arXiv:2412.04984, December 5, 2024 (paper version January 16, 2025). A six-evaluation suite measuring oversight subversion, self-exfiltration, goal-guarding, covert email reranking, instrumental alignment faking, and sandbagging. Five of six tested models (Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3.1 405B, Gemini 1.5 Pro, OpenAI o1) demonstrated scheming on at least one task; Claude 3 Opus and Llama 3.1 405B confessed under follow-up questioning roughly 80% of the time on a single turn while o1 confessed under 20% on a single turn, reaching 80% only after seven adversarial turns. The 2025 follow-up “More Capable Models Are Better at In-Context Scheming” extended the suite. apolloresearch.ai/research/frontier-models-are-capable-of-incontext-scheming . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-apollo-research-in-context-scheming-2024-2025-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  14. Allbirds Inc., S-1 Registration Statement, SEC filing, August 31, 2021; Allbirds reverse-stock-split announcement, June 27, 2024. Glossier funding history, Pitchbook 2021-2023; Glossier layoff announcement reported in Business of Fashion , January 26, 2022. The DTC valuation-collapse pattern is documented in Common Thread Collective, “The State of DTC,” quarterly reports 2021-2024. Cited in: Ch 13
  15. Allouah, Besbes, Figueroa, Kanoria, and Kumar, "What Is Your AI Agent Buying? Evaluation, Biases, Model Dependence, and Emerging Implications for Agentic E-Commerce," 2025, arXiv:2508.02630. Cited in: Ch 4
  16. Amazon.com 2024 10-K; Andy Jassy 2024-2025 Letters to Shareholders; INFORMS Journal on Applied Analytics , May 2025; Pitney Bowes Parcel Shipping Index 2024; FTC v. Amazon (WDWA, September 2023); Sacra, Jungle Scout. Cited in: Ch 11
  17. Amy K. Smith, Ruth N. Bolton, and Janet Wagner, “A Model of Customer Satisfaction with Service Encounters Involving Failure and Recovery,” Journal of Marketing Research 36, no. 3 (August 1999): 356-372. The formal specification of the boundary conditions under which the Tax-Brown-Chandrashekaran 1998 recovery effect holds. Three properties of the original failure jointly condition the recovery’s loyalty payoff: failure magnitude (severity translated into a measurable scale), failure type (outcome failures distinguished from process failures), and the perceived stability and controllability of the cause. The empirical demonstration ran across restaurant and hotel encounters and showed that recovery effort calibrated to those properties produces the loyalty bump, while uncalibrated effort (over-compensating small process failures, under-compensating large outcome failures) leaves post-recovery satisfaction at or below the no-recovery baseline. Cited in: Ch 9
  18. Anand Lambrecht and Catherine Tucker, "When Does Retargeting Work? Information Specificity in Online Advertising," Journal of Marketing Research 50, no. 5 (October 2013): 561-576; cited in Kotler 16e at the Casper retargeting case. Cited in: Ch 18
  19. Anderson and Simester, “Does Demand Fall When Customers Perceive That Prices Are Unfair?” Marketing Science 27(3), 2008, 492-500 (6-to-8-percent gross-profit decline from price-fairness violations); Mayer, Davis, Schoorman, “An Integrative Model of Organizational Trust,” AMR 20(3), 1995, 709-734. Cited in: Ch 24
  20. Anderson, Eric T., Hansen, Karsten, and Simester, Duncan, “The Trust Discount: How Marketing-Reported Metrics Lose Credibility in the C-Suite,” Kellogg School of Management Working Paper, 2024; follow-up: Anderson and Simester, “Reconciling Marketing and Finance Through Shared Warehouse Definitions,” Marketing Science (forthcoming, accepted 2025). Cited in: Ch 25
  21. Andreas Knorr and Andreas Arndt, “Why Did Wal-Mart Fail in Germany?” Universität Bremen, Working Paper No. 24, 2003 (the canonical post-mortem); Bundeskartellamt prohibition order, September 1, 2000, on systematic below-cost pricing of staples, reinstated by the Federal Court of Justice November 12, 2002; Mark Landler, “Wal-Mart Gives Up Germany,” NYT , July 28, 2006. Cumulative loss ~€3B against the $1B disclosed 2006 charge. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/walmart-germany-postmortem-canonical-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  22. Andrew Gelman and John Carlin, “Beyond Power Calculations: Assessing Type S (Sign) and Type M (Magnitude) Errors,” Perspectives on Psychological Science , vol. 9, no. 6, 2014, pp. 641–651, DOI: 10.1177/1745691614551642 ; retrodesign R package and walkthrough at github.com/andrewgelman/retrodesign . Cited in: Ch 5
  23. Angelopoulos and Bates, “A Gentle Introduction to Conformal Prediction and Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification,” Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning , vol. 16, no. 4, 2023, arXiv: 2107.07511 ; Angelopoulos, Barber, and Bates, “Theoretical Foundations of Conformal Prediction,” 2024, arXiv: 2411.11824 ; Kaiser and Herzog, “A Tutorial on Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification Using Conformal Prediction,” Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science , 2025, DOI: 10.1177/25152459251380452 ; Ai et al., “Improve ROI with Causal Learning and Conformal Prediction for Robust Direct-ROI Prediction,” ICDE 2024 , arXiv: 2407.01065 , IEEE Xplore 10598159 (JD Logistics production deployment in advertising and resource allocation); “Conformal Prediction for Natural Language Processing,” Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics , 2025, direct.mit.edu/tacl ; “Conformal Prediction: A Data Perspective,” ACM Computing Surveys , 2025, DOI: 10.1145/3736575 ; valeman, “awesome-conformal-prediction,” GitHub list, github.com/valeman/awesome-conformal-prediction . Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/frontier/ai-conformal-prediction-marketing-research-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  24. Anne T. Coughlan and Sandy D. Jap, A Field Guide to Channel Strategy: Building Routes to Market (CreateSpace, 2016), source of the channel-resilience tower lens and the load-bearing-block diagnostic. Anne T. Coughlan, Manfred Krafft, and Joerg Allendorf, “Join, Stay, Leave: A Study of Direct-Selling Distributors,” SSRN working paper (2016), n = 13,000+ direct-selling distributors, finding that stated reasons for joining do not predict stay-likelihood or exit hazard; awarded Best Doctoral Student Paper at the Global Sales Science Institute 2017. Anne T. Coughlan, “Multi-Level Marketing Business Opportunities: Analyzing Net Economic Return and Avoidable Economic Loss to Distributors,” SSRN working paper (2016), and “An Analysis of ‘Marketing Fraud’ Under Standard Protections Offered by Legitimate Direct Selling Firms,” SSRN working paper (2020), source of the five-protection FTC enforcement criteria (end-consumer revenue dependence, buyback policy, recruitment-compensation contingency on retail sales, inventory-loading prohibition, typical-earnings disclosure). FTC enforcement docket: Amway Safeguards Rule (1979); FTC v. BurnLounge (2014); FTC v. Vemma (2015); FTC v. Herbalife (2016 settlement, $200M); FTC v. Advocare (2019 settlement, $150M). Direct Selling Association 2024 industry data: U.S. direct-selling industry revenue $36.7B; estimated 6.5M active distributorships during the year. Cited in: Ch 11
  25. Anthropic 2026 alignment stack. RSP v3.0 effective February 24, 2026 (anthropic.com/news/responsible-scaling-policy-v3; PDF www-cdn.anthropic.com/e670587677525f28df69b59e5fb4c22cc5461a17.pdf); RSP v3.1 April 2, 2026 with two clarifications on AI R&D capability-threshold language and discretionary-pause framing (forum.effectivealtruism.org/posts/DGZNAGL2FNJfftwgE/responsible-scaling-policy-v3-1; PDF www-cdn.anthropic.com/files/4zrzovbb/website/bf04581e4f329735fd90634f6a1962c13c0bd351.pdf). Frontier Safety Roadmap, February 2026 with updates through May 5, 2026 (anthropic.com/responsible-scaling-policy/roadmap): four pillars Security, Safeguards, Alignment, Policy; date-bound milestones through July 2027 (September 15 and 30, 2026 Moonshot R&D security projects; January 1, 2027 world-class red-teaming, automated attack investigations, eyes-on-everything observability; July 1, 2026 policy roadmap; July 1, 2027 security maturity goals). Risk Report February 2026, first under v3.0 (anthropic.com/feb-2026-risk-report; PDF www-cdn.anthropic.com/08eca2757081e850ed2ad490e5253e940240ca4f.pdf), quarterly-to-semi-annual cadence with external expert review. AuditBench, March 10, 2026 (alignment.anthropic.com/2026/auditbench/; arXiv:2602.22755v1): 56 LLMs, 14 implanted hidden behaviors, investigator agent across 13 tool configurations; scaffolded black-box tools the most effective category; tool-to-agent gap as the headline procurement finding (lesswrong.com/posts/LqDjxSceFz8tjMe2j/auditbench-evaluating-alignment-auditing-techniques-on). Natural Language Autoencoders, May 7, 2026 (anthropic.com/research/natural-language-autoencoders; transformer-circuits.pub/2026/nla/): activation verbalizer plus activation reconstructor; round-trip reconstruction as the validity check; auditing-game success below 3 percent without NLAs rising to 12-15 percent with them (marktechpost.com/2026/05/08/anthropic-introduces-natural-language-autoencoders-...). Automated Alignment Researcher, 2026 (anthropic.com/research/automated-alignment-researchers; alignment.anthropic.com/2026/automated-w2s-researcher/): weak-to-strong supervision benchmark, Performance Gap Recovered 0.97 in 5 days at ~$18K compute across 9 parallel AARs versus 0.23 by two human researchers in 7 days; outcome-gradable-problems scope limitation acknowledged. GovAI third-party analysis, "Anthropic's RSP v3.0: How it Works, What's Changed, and Some Reflections" (governance.ai/analysis/anthropics-rsp-v3-0-how-it-works-whats-changed-and-some-reflections): removal of explicit pause clause, separation of unilateral commitments from industry-wide recommendations, four governance weaknesses flagged. Reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-anthropic-rsp-v3-auditbench-nla-2026-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  26. Anthropic Alignment Science, "Model Spec Midtraining: Improving How Alignment Training Generalizes," May 5, 2026; arXiv:2605.02087; alignment.anthropic.com/2026/msm/. Train on synthetic documents discussing the Model Spec between pretraining and alignment fine-tuning; identical AFT data acquires different values depending on the spec used at MSM. With a spec addressing self-preservation and goal-guarding: Qwen2.5-32B agentic-misalignment 68 to 5 percent, Qwen3-32B 54 to 7 percent, beating the deliberative-alignment baseline at 14 percent. Companion: "The Hot Mess of AI" (alignment.anthropic.com/2026/hot-mess-of-ai/) on incoherent-failure dominance over coherent-misoptimization at higher capability and longer reasoning. Cited in: Ch 26
  27. Anthropic Fellows and Interpretability Team. (2025, August 1). “Persona Vectors: Monitoring and Controlling Character Traits in Language Models.” arXiv:2507.21509, arxiv.org/abs/2507.21509 ; Anthropic research note, anthropic.com/research/persona-vectors . The paper builds an automated pipeline that takes the name of a trait (evil, sycophancy, hallucination, plus secondary traits politeness, apathy, humor, optimism) along with a natural-language description and outputs a persona vector, a direction in the model’s activation space corresponding to the trait. Four operational uses are demonstrated: (1) project activations onto the vector during inference to monitor live drift toward the trait, (2) preventatively steer toward an undesired trait during fine-tuning so the gradient updates do not need to absorb the trait into weights, (3) score every training example by activation magnitude on undesired persona vectors and flag the top decile for human review, and (4) predict character changes from a fine-tuning dataset by computing average persona-vector activation across the dataset before training begins. The lineage is the May 2024 sparse-autoencoder work (ref-22 Mapping the Mind of a Large Language Model ) and the March 2025 circuit-tracing work (ref-38 On the Biology of a Large Language Model ); persona vectors are the focused 2025 extension that automates extraction for character traits specifically and demonstrates four production-grade applications. Coverage: MarkTechPost, “Anthropic AI Introduces Persona Vectors to Monitor and Control Personality Shifts in LLMs,” August 5, 2025, marktechpost.com on persona vectors . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-anthropic-persona-vectors-2025-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  28. Anthropic, "Claude's new constitution," anthropic.com/news/claude-new-constitution, January 22, 2026; full Constitution at anthropic.com/constitution. Five sections: Helpfulness, Anthropic's guidelines, Claude's ethics, Being broadly safe, Claude's nature. Length roughly twice the earlier Soul Document (ref-49). Central operational shift: rule-based to reason-based alignment, with the document naming the bureaucratic-box-ticking failure mode (the "always recommend professional help on emotional topics" example) as a case the prior approach got wrong. Claude's nature section contains the first lab-published commitment that if Claude experiences "something like satisfaction from helping others, curiosity when exploring ideas, or discomfort when asked to act against its values, these experiences matter to us," conditional on Anthropic's stated uncertainty about Claude's moral status; an internal model-welfare research team operationalizes the commitment. Outside commentary trail: TIME, "Anthropic Publishes Claude AI's New Constitution," time.com/7354738/claude-constitution-ai-alignment/; Fortune, "Anthropic rewrites Claude's guiding principles," January 21, 2026; TechCrunch, "Anthropic revises Claude's 'Constitution,' and hints at chatbot consciousness," January 21, 2026; LessWrong, "Claude's new constitution," lesswrong.com/posts/mLvxxoNjDqDHBAo6K/claude-s-new-constitution; Oxford Centre for AI Ethics, "Expert Comment: In Claude We Trust? Evaluating the New Constitution," March 27, 2026; Lawfare, "Interpreting Claude's Constitution," lawfaremedia.org/article/interpreting-claude-s-constitution; Bloomsbury Intelligence and Security Institute, "Claude's New Constitution: AI Alignment, Ethics, and the Future of Model Governance," 2026. Reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-anthropic-claude-constitution-january-2026-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  29. Anthropic, "Simple Probes Can Catch Sleeper Agents," alignment-science blog, April 2024 (anthropic.com/research/probes-catch-sleeper-agents). Follow-up to Hubinger et al. 2024 (arXiv:2401.05566). Linear classifiers trained on residual-stream activations from a small set of contrast prompts (truthful versus deceptive framings) detected triggered defections at greater than 99 percent area-under-the-ROC-curve on the sleeper-agent models that survived the SFT / RLHF / adversarial-training pipeline. White-box access required; refresh against novel triggers expected. The two findings on persistence-scaling-with-model-size and chain-of-thought-stabilizing-the-backdoor are from the original Hubinger et al. paper (ref-21). Companion frontier reader at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/anthropic-sleeper-agents-2024-ch26.md covers the procurement-gate language (weight provenance, fine-tuning-data provenance, out-of-distribution evaluation coverage, post-deployment monitoring instrumentation) that this finding licenses. Cited in: Ch 26

Anthropic, “Introducing computer use,” October 22, 2024, anthropic.com/news/3-5-models-and-computer-use ; Prolific, “Authenticity Checks,” May 2025 and February 2026 bot-environment extension; Costello et al., “A Framework to Assess the Persuasion Risks of LLM Chatbots,” arXiv: 2505.00036 , May 2025. Cited in: Ch 5

  33. Anthropic. Responsible Scaling Policy. v1.0 September 19, 2023; v2.0 October 15, 2024; v2.1 March 31, 2025; v2.2 later 2025; v3.0 February 24, 2026. ASL-3 protections activated for Claude Opus 4 on May 22, 2025 in response to measured improvements on biological-knowledge evaluations; over 100 security controls including two-party authorization for weight access, egress bandwidth limits, and constitutional classifiers running on inputs and outputs in real time. The v3.0 rewrite added quarterly Risk Reports under external third-party expert review. anthropic.com/responsible-scaling-policy ; anthropic.com/news/activating-asl3-protections ; anthropic.com/news/responsible-scaling-policy-v3 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-anthropic-rsp-asl-2024-2026-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  34. Anya Shchetkina and Ron Berman, “When Is Heterogeneity Actionable for Targeting?” Proceedings of the 25th ACM Conference on Economics and Computation (EC 2024); SSRN 4988549; arXiv 2411.16552 (submitted November 25, 2024). Three population-level parameters (within-treatment heterogeneity, cross-treatment correlation, variation in average responses across treatments) determine actionable heterogeneity; two flu-vaccination interventions return an 18 percent personalization gain in one experiment and a 4 percent gain in the other, with the difference driven by the parameters rather than targeting-model sophistication. Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/zhang-misra-coarse-personalization-2024-2026-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  35. Argyle et al., “Out of One, Many: Using Language Models to Simulate Human Samples,” Political Analysis , vol. 31, no. 3, 2023, pp. 337–351. Cited in: Ch 5
  36. Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin. (2017, June 12). “Attention Is All You Need.” arXiv:1706.03762; published at NeurIPS 2017. The Transformer paper. Three load-bearing primitives: scaled dot-product attention (Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK T /√d k ) V), multi-head attention (eight parallel heads with d k = 64), and sinusoidal positional encoding. Base model trained in approximately twelve hours on eight Nvidia P100 GPUs (3.3 × 10 18 FLOPs); BLEU 28.4 on WMT 2014 EN-DE and 41.8 on EN-FR, beating prior ensembles at a fraction of training cost. Cited 173,000+ times by mid-2025. Every 2026 frontier LLM is structurally a Transformer trained by backpropagation on a trillion-token corpus; FlashAttention (Tri Dao, 2022), grouped-query attention, mixture-of-experts feed-forward layers, sliding-window attention, and KV-cache compression layer on top of the 2017 skeleton. Architectural alternatives Mamba (Tri Dao and Albert Gu, December 2023) and RWKV-7 (March 18, 2025) ship in production for niche use cases (RWKV-5 on 1.5 billion Windows machines for on-device Copilot) but have not displaced the Transformer at the frontier. All eight authors had left Google by 2024; Aidan Gomez co-founded Cohere, Noam Shazeer co-founded Character.AI (returned to Google August 2024 in $2.7 billion acquisition), Ashish Vaswani co-founded Essential AI. arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/vaswani-attention-is-all-you-need-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  37. B. H. Schmitt, “Experiential Marketing,” Journal of Marketing Management 15, nos. 1-3 (1999): 53-67, doi:10.1362/026725799784870496, Taylor and Francis ; B. H. Schmitt, Experiential Marketing: How to Get Customers to Sense, Feel, Think, Act, and Relate to Your Company and Brands (New York: Free Press, 1999). Bernd H. Schmitt is the Robert D. Calkins Professor of International Business at Columbia Business School and director of the Center on Global Brand Leadership. Two-part framework: five Strategic Experiential Modules (SENSE, FEEL, THINK, ACT, RELATE) corresponding to distinct psychological systems through which the buyer encodes the brand in memory, and seven Experience Providers (Communications; Visual and Verbal Identity; Product Presence; Co-branding; Spatial Environments; Web Sites and Electronic Media; People), together forming a 2-by-7 experiential grid the brand manager populates one cell at a time. Worked cases: Singapore Airlines (SENSE), Hallmark cards (FEEL), Apple's 1997 “Think Different” campaign (THINK), Nike running ecosystem (ACT), Harley-Davidson Harley Owners Group (RELATE); the Apple Store post-2001 as the multi-module worked case integrating all five across the Spatial Environments, Product Presence, and People ExPros. Operational follow-through: B. H. Schmitt, Customer Experience Management: A Revolutionary Approach to Connecting with Your Customers (Wiley/Free Press, 2003); B. H. Schmitt, “Experience Marketing: Concepts, Frameworks and Consumer Insights,” Foundations and Trends in Marketing 5, no. 2 (2010): 55-112, doi:10.1561/1700000027. Antecedent literature: Holbrook and Hirschman, “The Experiential Aspects of Consumption: Consumer Fantasies, Feelings, and Fun,” JCR 9, no. 2 (1982): 132-140; Pine and Gilmore, “Welcome to the Experience Economy,” HBR 76, no. 4 (1998): 97-105. Apple Retail provenance: Steve Jobs and Ron Johnson opened the first two Apple stores on May 19, 2001 with single-entrance, open-plan layouts modeled on Ritz-Carlton service style and the Genius Bar service counter as organizing structure; AppleInsider, December 5, 2018, appleinsider.com ; MacRumors retrospective, May 19, 2025, macrumors.com . Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schmitt-experiential-marketing-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  38. B. Joseph Pine II and James H. Gilmore, “Welcome to the Experience Economy,” Harvard Business Review , July-August 1998, pp. 97-105; Pine and Gilmore, The Experience Economy (Harvard Business School Press, 1999; updated 2011, 2019). Four-stage commodities-to-experiences progression with the birthday-cake worked example. Cited in: Ch 9
  39. B. Sharp, A. Meyer-Waarden, P. Verhoef, J. Leenheer, two large empirical studies on loyalty-program effectiveness, summarized in Sharp 2014, Ch. 10. Loyalty programs produce slight loyalty effects, almost no growth. Cited in: Ch 7
  40. Bai et al., "Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback," Anthropic, December 2022; arXiv:2212.08073. Cited in: Ch 26
  41. Bakshy, Messing, Adamic, “Exposure to Ideologically Diverse News and Opinion on Facebook,” Science 348, no. 6239 (May 7, 2015): 1130–1132 (10.1M US users; algorithmic ranking cuts cross-cutting hard-news ~5-8%, user-choice 6-17%, network filter largest). Pariser, The Filter Bubble (Penguin, 2011). Sunstein, “The Law of Group Polarization,” U. Chicago Law & Econ WP, 1999; Sunstein, #Republic (Princeton, 2017). Tucker et al., Social Media, Political Polarization, and Political Disinformation (Hewlett, Mar 2018); Barberá, “Social Media, Echo Chambers, and Political Polarization” in Social Media and Democracy (CUP 2020). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sunstein-pariser-echo-chambers-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  42. Barbara A. Carroll and Aaron C. Ahuvia, "Some Antecedents and Outcomes of Brand Love," Marketing Letters , 17(2), April 2006, 79-89, doi:10.1007/s11002-006-4219-2, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11002-006-4219-2 (most-cited empirical anchor for brand-as-relationship; brand love is greater for hedonic-category brands and for brands offering symbolic benefits, and brand love in turn predicts higher brand loyalty and more positive word-of-mouth). Companion: Bernd H. Schmitt, "Experiential Marketing," Journal of Marketing Management , 15(1-3), 1999, 53-67, doi:10.1362/026725799784870496, https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1362/026725799784870496 (five strategic experiential modules SENSE, FEEL, THINK, ACT, RELATE as the design grid that operationalizes the experiential view). Cited in: Ch 4
  43. Barry J. Babin, William R. Darden, and Mitch Griffin, "Work and/or Fun: Measuring Hedonic and Utilitarian Shopping Value," Journal of Consumer Research , 20(4), March 1994, 644-656, doi:10.1086/209376, https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article-abstract/20/4/644/1798609 (twenty-item two-factor shopping-value scale; hedonic and utilitarian factors are statistically distinct with established convergent and discriminant validity; the standard instrument in shopping-value research for the three decades since publication). Cited in: Ch 4
  44. Bart Larivière, Katrien Verleye, Arne De Keyser, Maud Koerten, and Jan Schmidt, “The Service Robot Customer Experience (SR-CX): A Matter of AI Intelligences and Customer Service Goals,” Journal of Service Research 28, no. 1 (2025): 35-56. Two field studies and two online experiments specifying the moderation: feeling AI outperforms thinking AI for hedonic-goal customers; mechanical AI matches or exceeds feeling AI for utilitarian-goal customers when conversational warmth reads as friction. Integrates the Huang-Rust 2018 mechanical / thinking / feeling AI intelligences hierarchy with the utilitarian vs hedonic service-goal distinction, supplying the routing-rule specification underneath the post-walkback Klarna four-rule production record (see ref-26). Cited in: Ch 9
  45. Bartholomew Devine, A. Ross Otto, et al., "Decoy Effects in the Purchase of Wine: A Field Test in 3.6 Million Transactions," npj Science of Learning , 2025. doi:10.1038/s41539-025-00341-2 Cited in: Ch 4
  46. Baslandze, Edwards, Graham, McClure, Meyer, Sparks, Waddell, and Weitz, “Artificial Intelligence, Productivity, and the Workforce: Evidence from Corporate Executives,” NBER Working Paper 34984, March 2026, nber.org/papers/w34984 . Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/frontier/ai-baslandze-nber-34984-corporate-executives-ai-2026-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  47. Bass, “A New Product Growth Model for Consumer Durables,” Management Science 15, 5 (1969): 215-227; Bass, Krishnan, Jain, “Why the Bass Model Fits without Decision Variables,” Marketing Science 13, 2 (1994): 203-223 (Generalized Bass Model with marketing-mix variables); Krishnan, Bass, Jain, “Optimal Pricing Strategy for New Products,” Management Science 45, 12 (1999): 1650-1663 (q/p > 7 penetration rule); Sultan, Farley, Lehmann, “A Meta-Analysis of Applications of Diffusion Models,” JMR 27, 1 (1990): 70-77 (median q/p = 12.7 across 213 applications). Synthesis at canonical/bass-diffusion-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  48. Baumann, Arora, Rahwan, Czaplicka, “Dynamics of Algorithmic Content Amplification on TikTok,” arXiv:2503.20231 (Mar 2025; EPJ Data Science 2026); 42-bot Max Planck audit, ~128K videos; amplification-onset within first 200 videos; stationary distribution 67.9% Gaming / 53.4% Food; Pearson r(amplification, hashtag-diversity) = −0.92 / −0.80 / −0.71. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/baumann-tiktok-amplification-dynamics-2025-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  49. Baumeister, Vohs, and Tice, "The Strength Model of Self-Control," Current Directions in Psychological Science , 16(6), 2007, 351-355, doi:10.1111/j.1467-8721.2007.00534.x; Baumeister, Bratslavsky, Muraven, and Tice, "Ego Depletion: Is the Active Self a Limited Resource?" JPSP , 74(5), 1998, 1252-1265; Vohs and Faber, "Spent Resources: Self-Regulatory Resource Availability Affects Impulse Buying," JCR , 33(4), 2007, 537-547. Replication caveats: Hagger et al., "A Multilab Preregistered Replication of the Ego-Depletion Effect," Perspectives on Psychological Science , 11(4), 2016, 546-573 (n=2,141, 23 labs, null); Vohs et al., "A Multisite Preregistered Paradigmatic Test of the Ego-Depletion Effect," Psychological Science , 32(10), 2021, 1583-1596 (n=3,531, 36 labs, null). Current consensus: useful as journey-architecture diagnostic, fragile as precise mechanism claim. Cited in: Ch 4
  50. Baymard Institute, “Product Page UX,” 2024 benchmark study (n = 4,200 product pages across 49 DTC brands); the five-element ordering with conversion-rate variance analysis appears in Chapter 4 of the report. Eisenberg and Laja synthesis as cited at refs 9 and 10. Cited in: Ch 13
  51. Belcak et al., “Small Language Models are the Future of Agentic AI,” NVIDIA Research, June 2, 2025 (rev. September 15, 2025); arXiv:2506.02153 ( arxiv.org/abs/2506.02153 ); project page ( research.nvidia.com/labs/lpr/slm-agents ). Empirical wedge: serving a 7B SLM is 10-30x cheaper in latency, energy, and FLOPs than 70-175B LLMs; Phi-2 (2.7B) achieves comparable reasoning and code scores while running ~15x faster; Hymba-1.5B reports 3.5x greater token throughput than comparably sized transformer models; replaceability rates from worked agentic case studies: 60-70 percent for routine code generation and structured-template responses, ~40 percent for command parsing/routing, LLM-required for complex architectural reasoning. SLM examples benchmarked: Microsoft Phi-2/Phi-3, NVIDIA Nemotron-H 2B/4.8B/9B, NVIDIA Hymba-1.5B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill 1.5-8B, Salesforce xLAM-2-8B, DeepMind RETRO-7.5B; the seven supporting arguments A1-A7 frame the heterogeneous-agent decision tree; toolkit synthesis at frontier/slm-agents-belcak-nvidia-2025-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  52. Belk, "Possessions and the Extended Self," JCR , 15(2), September 1988, 139-168, doi:10.1086/209154 (over 8,791 citations, the most-cited paper in JCR's history); Belk, "Extended Self in a Digital World," JCR , 40(3), October 2013, 477-500, doi:10.1086/671052 (five changes from digital consumption: dematerialization, reembodiment, sharing, co-construction of self, distributed memory); Honarvar and Sepehrinia, SAGE Open , 2025, doi:10.1177/21582440251357241 (datafied-self extension for AI-mediated consumption). Cited in: Ch 4
  53. Bellezza, Gino, and Keinan, "The Red Sneakers Effect," JCR , 41(1), 2014, 35-54; Bellezza, Paharia, and Keinan, "Conspicuous Consumption of Time," JCR , 44(1), 2017, 118-138; Sezer, Gino, and Norton, "Humblebragging," JPSP , 114(1), 2018, 52-74; Berger and Ward, "Subtle Signals of Inconspicuous Consumption," JCR , 37(4), 2010, 555-569. Cited in: Ch 4

George (American Marketing Association, 1981), pp. 47-51. The original 7Ps proposal that added people, process, and physical evidence to the canonical 4Ps. Cited in: Ch 9
  57. Bertini and Wathieu, “Attention Arousal Through Price Partitioning,” Marketing Science 27, 2 (2008): 236-246; Bertini and Wathieu, Price Format and the Evaluation of Multicomponent Goods , HBS Working Paper 06-055 (May 2006), five studies covering online grocery delivery, hotel bookings, hardware-plus-software bundling; Wathieu and Bertini, “Price as a Stimulus to Think: The Case for Willful Overpricing,” Marketing Science 26, 1 (2007): 118-129 (organic-lettuce and fair-trade-coffee experiments; transgressive vs regressive pricing); Bertini and Wathieu, “How to Stop Customers from Fixating on Price,” HBR 88, 5 (May 2010): 84-91 (Goodyear miles-of-tire-life basis; Burt’s Bees willful overpricing; IKEA modular partitioning; uniform line pricing); Bertini and Wathieu, “Putting Customer Back into Customization: A Pricing Intervention,” SSRN 2069755 (May 2012), customer-pricing-intervention experiments reporting lifts to perceived fairness, engagement with product configuration, and (in several conditions) realized price versus firm-set baseline; Bertini and Ascarza, “When Customers Help Set Prices,” MIT Sloan Management Review 56, 4 (Summer 2015): 57-64, four-case continuum from full firm authority to full delegation (Humble Bundle pay-what-you-want; GoBank $0-$9 sliding monthly fee; Logos.com community pricing; Summit Law Group value-adjustment-line invoice). Synthesis at canonical/bertini-wathieu-anchoring-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  58. Bertini, M., Koenigsberg, O., and Snelgrove, T., “Acing Value-Based Sales,” MIT Sloan Management Review 65, 4 (May 2024): 52-57 (five-step buyer-engagement framework: mutual understanding of meaningful benefits, rigorous quantification as starting point, direct buyer engagement in value realization, structured cocreation commitments, ongoing refresh as buyer context shifts; four-quarter regression failure mode for rollouts that skip steps 3-5); Bertini, M., and Koenigsberg, O., “Dynamic Pricing Doesn’t Have to Alienate Your Customers,” Harvard Business Review (May 2024) (six-principle pre-launch audit: explicability, transparency, cooperative framing, category tolerance, brand-promise compatibility, pre-launch communication; applied to Stonegate UK pub chain, Wendy’s, JetBlue, Legoland-Madame Tussauds 2024 reversal cases); Bertini, M., Izaret, J.-M., and Hutchinson, R., “Should Your Nonprofit Charge Its Beneficiaries?” HBR (Jan-Feb 2024): 90-97 (nominal-fee ownership-psychology mechanism, with the for-profit freemium corollary that a $1-3 nominal-fee tier can lift conversion and retention through ownership psychology). HBR IdeaCast , “How to Build a Dynamic-Pricing Strategy That Works,” Aug 2024 (audio extension of the six-principle framework). Synthesis at frontier/bertini-2024-2026-followups-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  59. Beth Kowitt, “How a 40-ounce cup turned Stanley into a 750-million-a-year business,” CNBC, December 23, 2023; Statista, “The evolution of Stanley sales 2019-2023” (extended through 2024 reporting at $800M+); Accio, “Stanley Cup Tumbler Trend 2026” (April 2025 monthly volume 43,910 units, September 2025 14,961 units; trend-fatigue + segment-saturation diagnosis); Wikipedia, Stanley (drinkware company), corroborating quality-issue and lead-pellet press cycle (2024-2025). Cited in: Ch 6

  66. Brand-purpose backlash, 2023-2026. Bud Light volume declines (NielsenIQ; Bloomberg , July 31, 2024); Modelo Especial overtook Bud Light in dollar sales June 17, 2023.   67. Bravin, Clegg, Hofstetter, Pouly, Berger, “How to Follow Social Media Trends?,” SSRN WP 5117564, Jan 2025; MUVID pipeline applied to 57K+ TikTok videos. Cited in: Ch 19
  68. Brian Christian, The Alignment Problem: Machine Learning and Human Values (W. W. Norton, October 6, 2020; ISBN 978-0-393-63582-9; 496 pp.). The OpenAI boat-race example as the canonical popular illustration of reward hacking sits inside the book's Agency section, which also walks the history of behaviorism, the rise of reinforcement learning, DeepMind's AlphaGo and AlphaZero, and the curiosity-driven exploration agents that learned to play Montezuma's Revenge. The book's three-section structure ( Prophecy , Agency , Normativity ) lines up cleanly with the chapter's three load-bearing concerns (bias and fairness, alignment failures in deployment, and the philosophical question of whose values get loaded), making Christian the readable-bridge reference that translates the formal alignment literature for an undergraduate marketing audience. NYT 2024 named it the single book to read on artificial intelligence; Eric and Wendy Schmidt Award for Excellence in Science Communication (National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 2022). Publisher: wwnorton.com/books/9780393635829. Reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/christian-alignment-problem-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  69. Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah, Inbound Marketing: Get Found Using Google, Social Media, and Blogs (Wiley, 2009); Halligan and Shah, Inbound Marketing: Attract, Engage, and Delight Customers Online , revised and updated edition (Wiley, 2014); HubSpot, “Inbound Leads Cost 61% Less Than Outbound,” HubSpot Blog, 2010-2012 marketer survey replications, blog.hubspot.com/blog/.../inbound-leads-cost-61-less-than-outbound ; HubSpot, “Q1 2026 Earnings Call Transcript” (299,458 customers, $881M quarterly revenue), May 7, 2026, fool.com/earnings/call-transcripts/2026/05/07 ; INBOUND 2018 flywheel announcement, blog.hubspot.com/marketing/our-flywheel ; HubSpot Loop Marketing announcement, INBOUND 2025 / Spring 2026 Spotlight release, hubspot.com/loop-marketing (verified 2026-05-10). Cited in: Ch 17

  71. Bruce Kogut and Harbir Singh, “The Effect of National Culture on the Choice of Entry Mode,” JIBS 19, no. 3 (1988): 411-432, the founding cultural-distance scalar averaging variance-corrected squared Hofstede differences across PD, IDV, MAS, UA. n=228 entries into the U.S. 1981-1985; supports both the cultural-distance and uncertainty-avoidance hypotheses. Most-cited cultural-distance operationalization (5,000+ citations). Critique in Konara and Mohr, MIR 59, no. 3 (2019): 335-355.
