  81. C. K. Prahalad and Yves L. Doz, The Multinational Mission (Free Press, 1987), the founding integration-responsiveness grid; empirical test in Roth and Morrison, JIBS 21, no. 4 (1990): 541-564. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/prahalad-doz-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  82. C. K. Prahalad, The Fortune at the Bottom of the Pyramid: Eradicating Poverty Through Profits , 1st ed. (Wharton School Publishing, 2004; revised 2010), product page amazon.com/Fortune-Bottom-Pyramid ; conceptual origin in C. K. Prahalad and Stuart L. Hart, “The Fortune at the Bottom of the Pyramid,” Strategy + Business 26 (2002), with Wikipedia synthesis at en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bottom_of_the_pyramid ; the four-pillar product-development logic (single-serve packaging, distribution invented for the geography, credit access designed for daily cash cycles, cost-driven product invention rather than feature-stripping) walked across the book's worked cases (Hindustan Unilever Project Shakti sachets, Casas Bahia installment credit in Brazil, Aravind Eye Care $25 cataract surgery, ITC e-Choupal). Aneel Karnani, “The Mirage of Marketing to the Bottom of the Pyramid: How the Private Sector Can Help Alleviate Poverty,” California Management Review 49, no. 4 (Summer 2007): 90-111, abstract at cmr.berkeley.edu/2007/08/49-4 ; SSRN working-paper version with full text at papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=914518 . Prahalad's response at NextBillion: nextbillion.net/prahalad-responds-to-mirage ; Stuart Hart's BOP 2.0 reframing at CKGSB Knowledge: english.ckgsb.edu.cn/knowledge/article/bottom-of-the-pyramid-and-beyond . 2024-2026 update sources: Hindustan Unilever, “A New Strategy for a New India,” 2024, at hul.co.in/news/2024/new-strategy-for-a-new-india ; Harvard Business School Project Shakti case page at hbs.edu/faculty/Pages/item.aspx?num=32078 ; Reliance Jio 2025 marketing-strategy synthesis at latterly.org/reliance-jio-marketing-strategy . The 2024-2025 academic consensus is that Karnani won the producer-vs-consumer framing, which is why practitioner-grade BOP today pairs consumer-good revenue with sourced suppliers, formal-economy employment, and microfinance rather than selling additional shampoo as a standalone poverty-alleviation claim. Reader synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/prahalad-bottom-of-pyramid-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  83. C.K. Prahalad and Gary Hamel, "The Core Competence of the Corporation," HBR 68, no. 3 (May-June 1990), the three-test definition (market access, customer benefit, inimitability); Hamel and Prahalad, Competing for the Future (HBSP, 1994); Jay B. Barney, "Firm Resources and Sustained Competitive Advantage," JoM 17, no. 1 (1991), the VRIN criteria. Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hamel-prahalad-core-competence-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3

Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-hinton-google-departure-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1

  87. Castelo et al., "Perceived Corruption Reduces Algorithm Aversion," JCP , 34(2), 2024, 326-333, doi:10.1002/jcpy.1373. Cited in: Ch 4

  89. Castelo, Bos, and Lehmann, "Task-Dependent Algorithm Aversion," JMR , 56(5), Oct 2019, 809-825. Cited in: Ch 4
  90. Cathy O'Neil, Weapons of Math Destruction (Crown, 2016). Three-trait WMD diagnostic. O'Neil, Sargeant, Appel, "Explainable Fairness in Regulatory Algorithmic Auditing," SSRN 4756637 (2024). Cited in: Ch 26
  91. Cespedes, F. V., Peterson, S., & Weinfurter, D. (2024, September 24). 4 Steps That Can Optimize Your Sales Process. Harvard Business Review . Gupta, S., & Cespedes, F. V. (2025, March 27). How One Company Used AI to Broaden Its Customer Base. HBR . Cespedes, F. V. (2024, July). AI, ROI, and Sales Productivity. Empirical anchor for the 12-20% productivity ceiling: 30-35% of the rep’s week is in customer contact. Cited in: Ch 23
  92. Chen et al., “Persona Vectors,” arXiv 2507.21509 , July 29, 2025, arxiv.org/abs/2507.21509 . Cited in: Ch 5
  93. Chen et al., “xbench: Tracking Agents Productivity Scaling with Profession-Aligned Real-World Evaluations,” June 16, 2025 ( arXiv:2506.13651 ); xbench leaderboard surface ( xbench.org ); inaugural Marketing benchmark (50 advertiser briefs, 836-candidate influencer pool, scored by alignment with advertiser's actual selection); the framework's central architectural claim is that profession-aligned evaluation predicts Technology-Market Fit better than ARC, MMLU, or function-calling micro-benchmarks; toolkit synthesis at frontier/xbench-marketing-agent-eval-2025-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  94. Chen, Arditi, Sleight, Evans, Lindsey, "Persona Vectors: Monitoring and Controlling Character Traits in Language Models," Anthropic, July 29, 2025; arXiv:2507.21509; companion blog August 1, 2025. Cloud, Le, Chua, Betley, Sztyber-Betley, Hilton, Marks, Evans, "Subliminal Learning: Language Models Transmit Behavioral Traits via Hidden Signals in Data," Anthropic Alignment Science, July 22, 2025. Cited in: Ch 26
  95. Cherep, Ma, Xu, Shaked, Maes, and Singh, "A Framework for Studying AI Agent Behavior: Evidence from Consumer Choice Experiments," ICLR 2026 / arXiv:2509.25609. Cited in: Ch 4
  96. Chiara Drolsbach and Nicolas Pröllochs, “Characterizing AI-Generated Misinformation on Social Media,” arXiv:2505.10266, May 15, 2025; 91,452 misleading posts via X Community Notes Jan 2023-Jan 2025; AI-generated subset shows higher virality despite smaller-account origin, more positive sentiment, entertainment-topic concentration, slightly lower believability/harmfulness vs conventional misinformation. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/drolsbach-prollochs-ai-misinfo-2025-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  97. Chip Heath and Dan Heath, Made to Stick: Why Some Ideas Survive and Others Die (Random House, 2007). SUCCES framework pp. 14-18; Curse of Knowledge and 1990 Newton tappers-and-listeners study pp. 19-22; CSPI movie-popcorn case pp. 3-4. Cited in: Ch 16
  98. Christakis and Fowler, Connected (Little, Brown, 2009); “Spread of Obesity,” NEJM 357 (2007): 370–379 (12,067-person Framingham re-analysis; influence at one/two/three degrees, gone at four); Fowler-Christakis, BMJ 337: a2338 (2008) (25/9.8/5.6% lift); “Cooperative behavior cascades,” PNAS 107(12) (2010): 5334–5338 (random-assignment public-goods); Shalizi-Thomas, “Homophily and Contagion Are Generically Confounded,” Sociological Methods & Research 40, no. 2 (2011): 211–239 (magnitudes are upper bounds). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/christakis-fowler-connected-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  99. Christensen, Hall, Dillon, and Duncan, Competing Against Luck (HarperBusiness, 2016); “Know Your Customers’ Jobs to Be Done,” Harvard Business Review , September 2016 (the milkshake case and the four-step JTBD process). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/christensen-jtbd-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  100. Christian Grönroos, “A Service Quality Model and Its Marketing Implications,” European Journal of Marketing 18, no. 4 (1984): 36-44, on the Nordic-school technical-functional decomposition of perceived service quality; Grönroos, “Defining Marketing,” EJM 23, no. 1 (1989): 52-60, the Nordic redefinition of marketing as relationship establishment, maintenance, and enhancement; Evert Gummesson, Total Relationship Marketing (Routledge, 2008), 30R framework. Christopher H. Lovelock, “Classifying Services to Gain Strategic Marketing Insights,” Journal of Marketing 47, no. 3 (1983): 9-20, the five 2x2 matrices that anchor the cross-industry classification toolkit. Cited in: Ch 9
  101. Christoph H. Loch, Arnoud De Meyer, Michael T. Pich, Managing the Unknown (Wiley, 2006); Sommer and Loch, Management Science 50:10 (2004). Airbus A380 wing-stress case from the Loch case base. Cited in: Ch 8
  102. Christopher A. Bartlett and Sumantra Ghoshal, Managing Across Borders: The Transnational Solution , 2nd ed. (HBS Press, 1998); empirical confirmation in Leong and Tan, JIBS 24, no. 3 (1993): 449-464 (n=131, four-cell typology held). The transnational form (high integration + high responsiveness) is the fourth cell EPRG implicitly conflates with geocentric; fewer than 5% of firms studied had built it. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/bartlett-ghoshal-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  103. Christopher H. Lovelock, “Classifying Services to Gain Strategic Marketing Insights,” Journal of Marketing 47, no. 3 (Summer 1983): 9-20. Five 2x2 classification matrices: nature-of-act-and-recipient (people-, possession-, mental-stimulus-, and information-processing), customer-relationship type, customization-and-judgment, demand-and-supply, and service-delivery method. Cited in: Ch 9
  104. Christopher K. Hsee, "The Evaluability Hypothesis: An Explanation for Preference Reversals between Joint and Separate Evaluations of Alternatives," Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes , 67(3), September 1996, 247-257, doi:10.1006/obhd.1996.0077, https://pages.ucsd.edu/~cmckenzie/Hsee1996OBHDP.pdf (the music-dictionary willingness-to-pay reversal); Hsee, "Less Is Better: When Low-Value Options Are Valued More Highly than High-Value Options," Journal of Behavioral Decision Making , 11(2), June 1998, 107-121, doi:10.1002/(SICI)1099-0771(199806)11:2<107::AID-BDM292>3.0.CO;2-Y (the scarf-coat, ice-cream-overflow, and dinnerware-with-broken-pieces reversals in three product categories); Hsee, Loewenstein, Blount, and Bazerman, "Preference Reversals Between Joint and Separate Evaluations of Options: A Review and Theoretical Analysis," Psychological Bulletin , 125(5), September 1999, 576-590, https://www.cmu.edu/dietrich/sds/docs/loewenstein/PrefRevJoint.pdf (the foundational review extending the framework to job-candidate selection, salary judgments, and public-goods valuation); Hsee and Zhang, "General Evaluability Theory," Perspectives on Psychological Science , 5(4), 2010, 343-355, doi:10.1177/1745691610374586 (the distribution-based account of when an attribute is evaluable in separate mode). Replication: Vonasch, Hung, and Sanchez, "Less Is Better in Separate Evaluations Versus More Is Better in Joint Evaluations: Mostly Successful Close Replication and Extension of Hsee 1998," Collabra: Psychology , 9(1), 2023, 77859, https://online.ucpress.edu/collabra/article/9/1/77859/196807 (preregistered confirmation of the core reversal). E-commerce extension: Tafti, Tafti, and Hsieh, "Did I Buy the Wrong Gadget? How the Evaluability of Technology Features Influences Online Information Search and Choice," MIS Quarterly , 42(2), June 2018, 633-657, https://misq.umn.edu/misq/article-abstract/42/2/633/1697 (hard-to-evaluate-alone but easy-to-compare specs gain weight on comparison pages). 2026 frontier anchor: "The New Face of ChatGPT Shopping: AI Product Comparisons," EverTune AI, March 2026, https://www.evertune.ai/resources/insights-on-ai/the-new-face-of-chatgpt-shopping-ai-product-comparisons (OpenAI shipped side-by-side product comparison tables inside ChatGPT shopping in March 2026, with 88% of responses including a "Best for" superlative that imposes joint evaluation by default). Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hsee-evaluability-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  105. Christopher Lovelock and Jochen Wirtz, Services Marketing: People, Technology, Strategy , 8th ed. (World Scientific, 2016): the 7Ps, service blueprint with three lines and fail-points, service-marketing triangle, SERVQUAL gap diagnostic, and tiered-service design as the structural alternative to commodity-versus-boutique positioning. A. Parasuraman, Leonard L. Berry, and Valarie A. Zeithaml, “Refinement and Reassessment of the SERVQUAL Scale,” Journal of Retailing 67, no. 4 (1991): 420-450, the zone-of-tolerance two-threshold formulation. Disney Tourist Blog, “New Record High Price for Lightning Lane Premier Pass,” October 1-2, 2025; WDWNT, “Lightning Lane Passes Increase in Price for Christmas and New Year Weeks,” December 2025 (Premier Pass and per-park tier pricing); disneyworld.disney.go.com . Cited in: Ch 9
  106. Churchill, Gilbert A., Jr., “A Paradigm for Developing Better Measures of Marketing Constructs,” Journal of Marketing Research , vol. 16, no. 1, 1979, pp. 64–73, DOI 10.1177/002224377901600110 ; Iacobucci, Dawn, and Gilbert A. Churchill, Jr., Marketing Research: Methodological Foundations , 12th ed., CreateSpace, 2018, ISBN 9781983654466; Campbell, Donald T., and Donald W. Fiske, “Convergent and Discriminant Validation by the Multitrait-Multimethod Matrix,” Psychological Bulletin , vol. 56, no. 2, 1959, pp. 81–105; Cronbach, Lee J., “Coefficient Alpha and the Internal Structure of Tests,” Psychometrika , vol. 16, no. 3, 1951, pp. 297–334; Nunnally, Jum C., Psychometric Theory , 2nd ed., McGraw-Hill, 1978 (the 0.70 alpha threshold for early-stage scales and 0.80 for established scales); Fornell, Claes, and David F. Larcker, “Evaluating Structural Equation Models with Unobservable Variables and Measurement Error,” Journal of Marketing Research , vol. 18, no. 1, 1981, pp. 39–50 (AVE-greater-than-squared-inter-construct-correlation criterion for discriminant validity); Hussey, Ian, Adam Hussain, and Sean Hughes, “Reliability Generalization Meta-Analysis Demonstrates that Existing Self-Report Measures Are Less Reliable than Researchers Assume,” Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science , 2025, DOI 10.1177/25152459241287123 (anomalous clustering of reported alphas just above the 0.70 cutoff across the published literature, suggesting selective reporting). Canonical methodology source for the construct → scale → alpha → validity pipeline that anchors brand-tracking, customer-satisfaction, service-quality, and customer-experience measurement instruments in marketing research; cited in the chapter's measurement-foundations subsection at §5.3. Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/canonical/churchill-iacobucci-marketing-research-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  107. CITAP (UNC), “TikTok and the 2024 Election” (Oct 2024; 800-account audit); NCRI, “A Tilted Playing Field” (Dec 2023, upd Jun 2024; 8:1 hashtag ratio); Lim-Han et al., “Algorithmic Persistence on TikTok,” AAAI ICWSM 2024 (360-account; 50% dis-engagement floor); UW Center for an Informed Public (Apr 2024; New Media & Society 2025; 1,000-video onboarding fingerprint); Liu-Hsu-Wang et al., arXiv:2410.13124 (Oct 2024); ADL, “Short-Form, Real Harm” (May 2024; 8-sec cue, >8% similar-share within 72h); NYU Stern (Apr 2024; 12-18pp removal lag). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/tiktok-algorithmic-amplification-audits-2024-2026-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  108. Claude Hopkins, Scientific Advertising (Crown Publishers, 1923); keyed-coupon attribution and the small-town test method at pp. 35-38, 60-62; sixteenfold cost-per-reply spread on a single five-dollar mail-order article (lines 250-252 of the converted markdown); two-thousand-headline keyed panel at lines 374-378; Pepsodent film habit-loop case at lines 652-712 with the cost-per-converter calculation that ruled out direct habit-change. David Ogilvy, Ogilvy on Advertising (Crown Publishers, 1983), pp. 1602-1606 attributing the Schlitz five-page-of-text revival (fifth-to-first in months) to Hopkins. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/v3-source-contributions/ch16/ogilvy-hopkins-classics.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  109. Clegg, Hofstetter, de Bellis, and Schmitt, "Unveiling the Mind of the Machine," JCR , 51(2), August 2024, 342-361, doi:10.1093/jcr/ucad075. Cited in: Ch 4
  110. Cohen, “The Earth Is Round (p < .05),” American Psychologist , vol. 49, no. 12, 1994, pp. 997–1003. Cited in: Ch 5
  111. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2016). Consent Order: In the Matter of Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. File No. 2016-CFPB-0015, September 8, 2016. Cited in: Ch 23
  112. Costco Wholesale Corporation, FY2025 Operating Results (September 25, 2025), Annual Report 10-K (October 2025), Q1 FY2026 Operating Results (December 11, 2025) reporting 145.9M cardholders, 81.4M paid memberships, 39.7M paid Executive memberships (+9.1 percent YoY), and $1.329B fee income; Travis Hoium, “How Costco Makes Money, Despite $1.50 Hot Dog Combos,” Motley Fool , December 5, 2025; AInvest, “How Costco Turned Kirkland Signature Into an $86 Billion Brand,” May 2025; Worldly Partners Costco analysis 2024-2025; Thirteenth Amendment to the 2015 Co-Branded Credit Card Program Agreement among Costco, Citibank, and Visa (September 11, 2024 effective date), filed via Justia Contracts, extending the initial term to June 19, 2029 and committing the parties to a February 2025 Loyalty Program enhancement. Cited in: Ch 11
  113. Costco. Annual Report 2024 . Costco Wholesale Corporation, 2024. Membership-database deterministic-identity layer in the Costco mobile app. Cited in: Ch 14
  114. Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and Human Rights, Democracy and the Rule of Law (CETS 225), opened for signature in Vilnius September 5, 2024 (coe.int/en/web/artificial-intelligence/the-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence; full text rm.coe.int/1680afae3c). Initial signatories September 5, 2024: Andorra, Georgia, Iceland, Norway, the Republic of Moldova, San Marino, the United Kingdom, the United States, Israel, and the European Union; subsequent signatures by Canada, Japan, Liechtenstein, Montenegro, Switzerland, Ukraine, and Uruguay. As of May 2026 no state has ratified; entry into force requires five ratifications including three Council of Europe member states. European Parliament report A10-0007/2026 (europarl.europa.eu/doceo/document/A-10-2026-0007_EN.html) backed EU conclusion of the Convention March 11, 2026 (Texts Adopted TA-10-2026-0071). UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence, adopted by the 41st General Conference on November 23, 2021 (unesco.org/en/articles/recommendation-ethics-artificial-intelligence; full text unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000381137). Readiness Assessment Methodology and Ethical Impact Assessment portal at unesco.org/ethics-ai/en/ram; 60-country deployment count by 2026 in advance of the 4th UNESCO Global Forum on the Ethics of AI (Riyadh, September 14-17, 2026). OECD Council Recommendation OECD-LEGAL-0449 on AI, May 22, 2019; revised May 3, 2024 (oecd.ai/en/ai-principles; legalinstruments.oecd.org/en/instruments/OECD-LEGAL-0449); endorsed by 47 countries representing approximately 80 percent of global GDP. G7 Hiroshima AI Process, International Guiding Principles and International Code of Conduct, October 30, 2023 (digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/g7-leaders-statement-hiroshima-ai-process); commitments absorbed into the EU AI Act Article 56 GPAI Code of Practice. UN Advisory Body on AI, "Governing AI for Humanity" final report, September 19, 2024 (un.org/sites/un2.un.org/files/governing_ai_for_humanity_final_report_en.pdf); seven institutional functions including International Scientific Panel on AI and Global Fund for AI. Brookings, "The COE's Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence and the future of international AI treaties" (brookings.edu/articles/the-coes-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence-and-the-future-of-international-ai-treaties/). Carnegie Endowment, "Council of Europe Framework Convention on Artificial Intelligence," September 19, 2024 (carnegieendowment.org/2024/09/19/council-of-europe-framework-convention-on-artificial-intelligence-pub-95130). CSIS, "G7 Hiroshima AI Process: Toward International Framework for Advanced AI Systems" (csis.org/analysis/g7-hiroshima-ai-process-toward-international-framework-advanced-ai-systems). Reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/global-ai-governance-frameworks-2024-2026-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  115. Cultural-appropriation case file: Dolce & Gabbana China crisis, Nov. 2018 (Stefano Gabbana DM leak, Shanghai show cancellation, Tmall/Lane Crawford/Net-a-Porter delistings) covered in The New York Times , South China Morning Post , Business of Fashion ; Pepsi & Kendall Jenner, “Live for Now Moments Anthem,” April 4-5, 2017, pulled within 24 hours, in The New York Times , The Atlantic , and analysis at Adweek ; H&M “Coolest Monkey in the Jungle” hoodie, Jan. 2018, in BBC , The Guardian ; Burberry noose-drawcord hoodie, Feb. 2019, in The Independent ; Bud Light + Dylan Mulvaney boycott, April-July 2023, sales-impact data from Bump Williams Consulting and Anheuser-Busch InBev Q2 2023 earnings (~28% U.S. volume decline year-over-year); Gap back-to-school Spanish-language ad, Aug. 2017, in Adweek and Latina ; Black Tuesday / blackout-square critique, June 2-3, 2020, summarized in The Atlantic and The New York Times . Aggregated case-study set at wiki/external/canonical/inclusive-marketing-cultural-appropriation-cases-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  116. D. A. Aaker, Building Strong Brands (Free Press, 1996), Chs. 3-4 (Brand Identity Planning Model); Aaker, Brand Portfolio Strategy (Free Press, 2004), Ch. 2 (Brand-Identity-System refinement). The four-perspective identity-system framework (brand-as-product, brand-as-organization, brand-as-person, brand-as-symbol) with the core/extended identity split (3-5 core elements; 4-7 extended) and the three-layer value proposition (functional, emotional, self-expressive benefits). Personality-dimension instrument: J. L. Aaker, “Dimensions of Brand Personality,” JMR 34, no. 3 (1997): 347-356 (sincerity, excitement, competence, sophistication, ruggedness; 631 respondents, 37 U.S. brands, 114 traits factor-analyzed to five stable dimensions). Practitioner reaffirmation: D. A. Aaker, “Story of Two Books: Managing Brand Equity and Building Strong Brands,” LinkedIn / Prophet, 2023, linkedin.com/pulse/story-two-books-managing-brand-equity . Cross-perspective overlap argument and core-vs-extended discipline elaborated in Aaker, Aaker on Branding: 20 Principles That Drive Success (Morgan James, 2014), principles 1-7. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/aaker-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  117. D. A. Aaker, Building Strong Brands (Free Press, 1996), Chs. 3-4: brand identity system, four perspectives, value-proposition trio. Personality: J. L. Aaker, JMR 34, no. 3 (1997): 347-356. Antecedents: Park, Jaworski, MacInnis, JM 50, no. 4 (1986): 135-145; Holbrook and Hirschman, JCR 9, no. 2 (1982): 132-140 (experiential consumption: fantasies, feelings, fun as consumption outcomes). Signaling: Erdem and Swait, JCP 7, no. 2 (1998): 131-157. Empirical brand-tracking factor structure: D. R. Lehmann, K. L. Keller, J. U. Farley, “The Structure of Survey-Based Brand Metrics,” Journal of International Marketing 16, no. 4 (2008): 29-56 (six recurring factors across U.S. and China samples). Canonical readers: park-jaworski-macinnis-ch07.md , erdem-swait-ch07.md , holbrook-hirschman-ch07.md , lehmann-keller-farley-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  118. Damon Centola and Michael Macy, “Complex Contagions and the Weakness of Long Ties,” AJS 113, no. 3 (Nov 2007): 702–734 (agent-based simulation showing the non-monotonic relationship between long-tie density and threshold-two diffusion); Damon Centola, “The Spread of Behavior in an Online Social Network Experiment,” Science 329, no. 5996 (Sep 3, 2010): 1194–1197 (Healthy Lifestyle Network; 1,528 randomized participants; 54% clustered vs 38% random-tie adoption at six-buddy average degree; ~4× faster diffusion clustered; dose response 15/31/42% at one/two/three already-adopting buddies); Centola, How Behavior Spreads: The Science of Complex Contagions (Princeton, 2018); Sune Lehmann et al., “Transition from simple to complex contagion in collective decision-making,” Nature Communications (2022; high-stakes decisions exhibit clustering advantage, low-stakes weak-tie advantage). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/centola-complex-contagion-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  119. Daniel Yankelovich, “New Criteria for Market Segmentation,” Harvard Business Review 42, no. 2 (1964): 83–90; Daniel Yankelovich and David Meer, “Rediscovering Market Segmentation,” HBR 84, no. 2 (2006): 122–131. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/yankelovich-1964-segmentation-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6

EU AI Act Article 50 enforcement Aug 2, 2026. FTC 16 CFR Part 465 (effective Oct 21, 2024; $51,744 per-violation). Liu et al., "Self-Information Rewrite Attacks on Text Watermarking," arXiv:2505.05190 (2025). Cited in: Ch 26
  121. David A. Aaker and Erich Joachimsthaler, “The Brand Relationship Spectrum,” California Management Review 42, no. 4 (2000): 8-23; Aaker, Brand Portfolio Strategy (Free Press, 2004); Hsu, Fournier, and Srinivasan, Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science 44, no. 2 (2016): 261-280. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/aaker-brand-portfolio-strategy-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  122. David Court, Dave Elzinga, Susan Mulder, and Ole Jørgen Vetvik, “The Consumer Decision Journey,” McKinsey Quarterly , June 2009, mckinsey.com/capabilities/growth-marketing-and-sales/our-insights/the-consumer-decision-journey (subsequent McKinsey work expanded the original 20,000-buyer sample to ~200,000 journeys across 90 industries); Mark W. Schaefer, Marketing Rebellion: The Most Human Company Wins (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2019), businessesgrow.com/rebellion ; Mark W. Schaefer, The Content Code: Six Essential Strategies to Ignite Your Content, Your Marketing, and Your Business (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2015), archive.org/details/contentcodesixes0000scha ; Mark W. Schaefer, Belonging to the Brand: Why Community is the Last Great Marketing Strategy (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2023); Mark W. Schaefer, Audacious: How Humans Win in an AI Marketing World (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2024), businessesgrow.com/audacious (verified 2026-05-10). Cited in: Ch 17
  123. David E. Bowen and Edward E. Lawler III, “The Empowerment of Service Workers: What, Why, How, and When,” Sloan Management Review 33, no. 3 (Spring 1992): 31-39. The four-level empowerment framework (production-line, suggestion involvement, job involvement, high involvement) descended from the Carlzon SAS program; the canonical conceptual paper for the late-1980s through early-2000s academic literature on service-employee empowerment. Cited in: Ch 9
  124. David H. Maister, “The Psychology of Waiting Lines,” in The Service Encounter: Managing Employee/Customer Interaction in Service Businesses , ed. John A. Czepiel, Michael R. Solomon, and Carol F. Surprenant (Lexington Books, 1985), pp. 113-123. Eight propositions on perceived wait time as a service-quality variable distinct from clock time, foundational to queue-design practice across hospitality, healthcare, and theme-park operations. Cited in: Ch 9
  125. David L. Sturges, "Communicating through Crisis: A Strategy for Organizational Survival," Management Communication Quarterly , 7(3), 1994, 297-316. The three-information-types taxonomy (instructing, adjusting, internalizing). Wiki synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sturges-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22
  126. David Ogilvy, Ogilvy on Advertising (Crown Publishers, 1983), pp. 108-115 on Hathaway and the eyepatch; trade-press accounts of the C.F. Hathaway Company campaign with Hewitt, Ogilvy, Benson & Mather; Ogilvy's recession-spend observation in the same volume's cumulative-value chapter. Cited in: Ch 16
  127. David R. Bell, Location is (Still) Everything (New Harvest / Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2014); Bell, Bonfrer, and Chintagunta, “Recovering SKU-Level Preferences and Response Sensitivities,” JMR 42, no. 2 (2005): 169-182. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/bell-location-is-everything-ch12.md . Cited in: Ch 12

  131. De Freitas, Oğuz-Uğuralp, Uğuralp, and Puntoni, "Ideation with Generative AI in Consumer Research and Beyond," JCR 52(1):18, 2025; Hermann and Puntoni, "Generative AI in Marketing," JPP&M , 2025. Course-internal synthesis: frontier/ai-puntoni-ideation-homogenization-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  132. De Freitas, Uğuralp, Oğuz-Uğuralp, and Puntoni, "Chatbots and Mental Health: Insights into the Safety of Generative AI," JCP , 34(3), July 2024, 481-491, doi:10.1002/jcpy.1393. Cited in: Ch 4
  133. Dean, Managerial Economics (Prentice-Hall, 1951), Chs 4 (Pricing Policies) and 7 (Price Differentials); “Pricing Policies for New Products,” HBR 28 (Nov 1950): 45-53 (skimming-vs-penetration taxonomy, HBR Classic 1976); “Problems of Product-Line Pricing,” JM 14, 4 (1950): 518-528. Joel Dean Associates founded 1940. Synthesis at canonical/dean-managerial-economics-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  134. DeepSeek-AI. (2025, January 22). DeepSeek-R1. arXiv:2501.12948; peer-reviewed in Nature , 645, 633–638 (Sep 17, 2025). MIT-licensed weights with six distilled checkpoints (1.5B–70B). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-deepseek-r1-v3-cost-shock-2025-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  135. DeepSeek-AI. (December 26, 2024). DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report. arXiv:2412.19437. 671-billion-parameter mixture-of-experts base model with 37 billion active parameters per token; pre-trained on 14.8 trillion tokens at 2.788 million H800 GPU-hours, roughly $5.58 million at $2 per hour. arxiv.org/abs/2412.19437 . DeepSeek-AI. (January 22, 2025). DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning. arXiv:2501.12948; Nature , 645, 633–638 (Sep 17, 2025); nature.com/articles/s41586-025-09422-z . R1-Zero variant: pure reinforcement-learning training on verifiable rewards (math with known answers, code with passing tests), no supervised reasoning examples; the model spontaneously developed planning, self-checking, and revision behaviors. Additional training cost on top of V3 reported in the Nature paper at roughly $294,000. Distillation: 800,000 reasoning samples generated by R1 used to fine-tune Qwen 1.5B / 7B / 14B / 32B and Llama 8B / 70B bases; the resulting R1-Distill-Qwen-7B scores 55.5% Pass@1 on AIME 2024, surpassing the prior state-of-the-art 32-billion-parameter open model. Local-laptop path via Ollama at ollama.ai : ollama pull deepseek-r1:7b downloads roughly 4.7 GB and runs on a 16 GB consumer laptop. The January 27, 2025 market reaction (Nvidia −$589 billion in one session) is reported in CNBC, January 27, 2025. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-deepseek-r1-v3-cost-shock-2025-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  136. DellaVigna and Linos, "RCTs to Scale: Comprehensive Evidence from Two Nudge Units," Econometrica 90, no. 1 (2022): 81-116. Cited in: Ch 26
  137. Demis Hassabis. (2024, December 8). Nobel Lecture, Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences (Chemistry 2024, jointly with John M. Jumper). DeepMind's single-algorithm thesis: a small set of general learning algorithms can produce general intelligence across domains, tested through DQN on Atari (Nature 2015), AlphaGo (Nature 2016), AlphaFold 2 (Nature 2021), Project Astra (Google I/O 2024). Implication for a marketing team: tasks tempting to wrap in custom 2026 engineering become commodity frontier-model capabilities by 2027. nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2024/hassabis/lecture . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hassabis-deepmind-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1
  138. Diana I. Tamir and Jason P. Mitchell, “Disclosing Information About the Self Is Intrinsically Rewarding,” PNAS 109, no. 21 (May 2012): 8038–8043. Cited in: Ch 19
  139. Diephuis, R., Hillebrand, B., et al. (2025). There is more to sales than selling: a literature review of the roles of sales in new product development. Journal of Personal Selling & Sales Management , DOI 10.1080/08853134.2025.2551955, pp. 427-445 (October 6, 2025). Systematic review of approximately 141 peer-reviewed papers across the 2000-2024 window; four-activity sales-in-NPD framework (idea generation, market intelligence contribution, new-product selling, customer-interaction management); five-antecedent / three-outcome chain (rep-product-knowledge, rep-customer-knowledge, rep-cross-functional-network, rep-time-allocation, rep-incentive-alignment; NPD-success-rate, NPD-cycle-time, NPD-customer-fit); contingency-variable map (NPD-project-newness, NPD-market-newness, firm-size, NPD-team-structure); vertical-specialist or industry-expert fifth-tier rep role as the structural complement to the BDR/AE/CSM ladder; mapping onto Cooper’s stage-gate model with idea-generation heaviest at Stages 1-2, market-intelligence at Stages 2-3, new-product selling at Stages 5-6, customer-interaction management at Stages 6-7. Cited in: Ch 23
  140. Dillion et al., “Can AI language models replace human participants?” Trends in Cognitive Sciences , vol. 27, no. 7, 2023, pp. 597–600. Cited in: Ch 5
  141. Dolan and Simon, Power Pricing (Free Press, 1996), 371 pp. Four-dimensional power-pricer framework (viewpoint, fact base, tools/scope, determination/implementation) and five price-customization mechanisms (segmentation, nonlinear, bundling, product-line, time-based), Chs 1-9. Simon founded Simon-Kucher (1985). Synthesis at canonical/dolan-simon-power-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  142. Dollinger, Mike. (2023, October). The Liquid Death playbook. Harvard Business Review case discussion. Cited in: Ch 23
  143. Don E. Schultz, Stanley I. Tannenbaum, and Robert F. Lauterborn, Integrated Marketing Communications: Putting It Together and Making It Work (NTC Business Books, 1993); Robert F. Lauterborn, "New Marketing Litany: Four Ps Passé: C-Words Take Over," Advertising Age , Oct 1, 1990; Schultz and Kitchen, Communicating Globally (NTC, 2000), four-stage IMC adoption model (Stage 1 tactical coordination, Stage 2 redefined scope across all brand contacts, Stage 3 application of information technology to track contact-level effects, Stage 4 financial and strategic integration with the firm P&L). Cornelissen and Lock, "Theoretical Concept or Management Fashion? Examining the Significance of IMC," Journal of Advertising Research 40(5), 7-15, 2000, and Schultz and Kitchen reply in the same issue, 17-21. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schultz-imc-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  144. Donald E. Knuth, The Art of Computer Programming , Volumes 1–4B, Addison-Wesley, 1968–2022 (Vol. 1, 3rd ed., 1997, 650pp; Vol. 2, 3rd ed., 1997, 762pp; Vol. 3, 2nd ed., 1998, 780pp; Vol. 4A, 2011, 883pp; Vol. 4B, 2022, 714pp; Vol. 4 Fascicle 7 on constraint satisfaction, February 5, 2025). The standing reference on the analysis of algorithms; the source from which computer science took its asymptotic-cost vocabulary (Big-O, Big-Θ, Big-Ω) and its working definition of when an algorithm is good. www-cs-faculty.stanford.edu/~knuth/taocp.html . The 97/3 reservation appears in Knuth's 1974 ACM Turing Award lecture, “Computer Programming as an Art,” Communications of the ACM 17(12), December 1974, p. 671. dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/361604.361612 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/knuth-art-of-computer-programming-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  145. Donald E. Knuth, “Literate Programming,” The Computer Journal 27(2), 1984, pp. 97–111. Expanded as the book Literate Programming , CSLI Publications, 1992. The argument that programs are best thought of as explanations to humans of what we want the computer to do, with the executable code as a secondary product. The 2026 instance is the SKILL.md whose top half is plain English and whose bottom half is model instructions, and Andrej Karpathy's June 2025 Software 3.0 keynote, in which the natural-language prompt becomes the program. www-cs-faculty.stanford.edu/~knuth/lp.html . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/knuth-art-of-computer-programming-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  146. Donald Horton and R. Richard Wohl, “Mass Communication and Para-Social Interaction,” Psychiatry 19, no. 3 (1956): 215-229, foundational citation; updated empirical work in Journal of Consumer Research 2018-2024 on creator-audience parasocial bonds. Cited in: Ch 20
  147. Douglas B. Holt, John A. Quelch, and Earl L. Taylor, “How Global Brands Compete,” HBR 82, no. 9 (Sept. 2004): 68-75, the canonical empirical answer to Levitt-Douglas-Wind. 41-country qualitative + 12-nation survey of 1,800 consumers; four-segment typology (global citizens 55%, global dreamers 23%, antiglobals 13%, global agnostics 8%); American values turned negative inside antiglobals. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/holt-quelch-taylor-global-brands-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  148. Dubé and Misra, “Personalized Pricing and Consumer Welfare,” JPE 131, 1 (2023): 131-189; NBER WP 23775. ZipRecruiter RCT, 10 price points, ML demand estimation; uniform monopoly +55% over status quo, personalized +19% over uniform; consumer surplus −23% aggregate but >60% get a lower price than uniform; inequity-averse welfare can rise. Synthesis at frontier/dube-misra-personalized-pricing-welfare-2023-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  149. Dunivin, “Scalable Qualitative Coding with LLMs,” arXiv 2401.15170 , 2024 (chain-of-thought lifting mean kappa from 0.59 to 0.68); NLP4RE 2024, “From Inductive to Deductive,” ceur-ws Vol-3959 paper 2 (few-shot kappa 0.738 / 0.734); “Empirical Investigation of LLM-as-Judge Design Choices,” arXiv 2506.13639 , June 2025 (prompt strategy and rubric clarity dominate inter-rater variance). Cited in: Ch 5
  150. E. Joachimsthaler and D. A. Aaker, “Building Brands Without Mass Media,” Harvard Business Review 75, no. 1 (January-February 1997): 39-50, hbr.org/1997/01/building-brands-without-mass-media . Five operational principles for brand-building without an advertising-led budget: coordinated touchpoint architecture across package, store, and service; values-aligned event sponsorship; experiential retail, sampling, and live-event channels; public-relations and publicity stunts for earned amplification; and a visible founder or chief executive as a brand personality. Worked European cases: Cadbury Schweppes (Cadbury World theme park, opened 1990 in Bournville at roughly £6M build cost, ~500,000 annual visitors by the mid-1990s); Hugo Boss (BOSS / HUGO / BALDESSARINI three-brand showroom-and-licensing architecture, 1993); Nestlé Nespresso (the Nespresso Club direct-membership model, launched 1989, first physical boutique Paris 2000); Swatch (Swatch Art Special program launched 1985 with Christian Chapiron, expanded through Keith Haring 1986, Mimmo Paladino 1989, Sam Francis 1992, Yoko Ono 1996); Häagen-Dazs (late-1980s European parlor-and-sampling rollout). U.S. CPG vs European CPG advertising-to-revenue ratios at the time of writing: 12-20 percent (U.S.) vs 3-5 percent (Europe). Contemporary D2C extensions: Liquid Death (Mike Cessario, 2017 launch, $1,500 viral video, ~3M views in three months, $263M revenue and $1.4B valuation by 2024); Stanley Quencher (~168,000 social mentions Jan 2023-Jan 2024, $73M revenue 2019 to $750M 2023 to $800M+ 2024); Glossier (Emily Weiss, Into the Gloss blog 2010, brand launch 2014, ~$134M Glossier.com revenue 2024, ~70 percent of online sales driven by peer referral). Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/joachimsthaler-aaker-building-brands-without-mass-media-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  151. Edward L. Bernays, Crystallizing Public Opinion (Boni and Liveright, 1923), available via Project Gutenberg ; Propaganda (Horace Liveright, 1928); The Engineering of Consent (University of Oklahoma Press, 1955); Biography of an Idea: Memoirs of Public Relations Counsel Edward L. Bernays (Simon and Schuster, 1965). Wiki synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/bernays-crystallizing-propaganda-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22
  152. Emma Sopadjieva, Utpal M. Dholakia, and Beth Benjamin, “A Study of 46,000 Shoppers Shows That Omnichannel Retailing Works,” Harvard Business Review , January 2017. Cited in: Ch 11
  153. Engel, Kollat, and Blackwell, Consumer Behavior , 1st ed. (Holt, Rinehart and Winston, 1968); Blackwell, Miniard, and Engel, Consumer Behavior , 10th ed. (South-Western Cengage, 2011). Seven-stage decision-process spine: need recognition, information search, pre-purchase evaluation of alternatives, purchase, consumption, post-consumption evaluation, divestment; three-block architecture surrounding the spine with information processing, individual differences, and environmental influences. The Court, Elzinga, Mulder, and Vetvik 2009 McKinsey Quarterly "consumer decision journey" is the practitioner reformulation that replaced the linearity assumption with a circular feedback loop. Cited in: Ch 4
  154. Eric von Hippel, Democratizing Innovation (MIT Press, 2005), Chs 1, 2, 5; von Hippel, “Lead Users,” Management Science 32:7 (1986). 3M case studies on lead-user concept value. Cited in: Ch 8
  155. Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, and Lindsey R. Raymond, "Generative AI at Work," Quarterly Journal of Economics 140, no. 2 (2025): 889-942 ( academic.oup.com/qje/article/140/2/889/7990658 ), the 5,172-agent staggered-rollout field experiment, the 15 percent average issues-resolved-per-hour gain, the bottom-quintile 30-plus percent gain and top-quintile near-zero-with-quality-decline finding, the model-collapse-risk forward warning, and the international-agent English-fluency adjacent finding; working paper: NBER Working Paper 31161, April 2023 ( nber.org/papers/w31161 ); arxiv preprint 2304.11771 ( arxiv.org/abs/2304.11771 ); frontier reader at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/brynjolfsson-genai-productivity-2025-ch03.md ; accessed 10 May 2026. Cited in: Ch 3
  156. Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, and Lindsey R. Raymond, “Generative AI at Work,” NBER Working Paper No. 31161, April 2023 (revised 2024); n=5,179 support agents, ~14 percent average gain concentrated in the bottom-experience quartile. McKinsey Operations Practice, The State of Customer Care 2024 , June 2024 (n=300 leaders); replication contingent on retained empowerment tier. Cited in: Ch 9
  157. Erik Brynjolfsson, Danielle Li, Lindsey Raymond. (2023). Generative AI at Work. NBER w31161, published in The Quarterly Journal of Economics . Staggered rollout to 5,179 customer-support agents at a Fortune 500 software firm: 14% average productivity gain (issues per hour), 34% for the bottom skill quartile, near-zero for the top quartile; positive customer-sentiment and retention effects. Mechanism: the assistant disseminates top agents' response patterns to newer workers. nber.org/papers/w31161 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/brynjolfsson-productivity-ch01.md. Cited in: Ch 1
  158. Erik Hermann, Stefano Puntoni, and David A. Schweidel, "Conversational AI: The Next Frontier of Digital Platform Monetization," SSRN Working Paper, October 8 2025, https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5634270 Three-generation framing of digital platform monetization (intent through search, attention through social media, immersive interactions through conversational AI) with a two-dimensional advertising-format typology (targeting precision crossed with obtrusiveness) that locates the contextual native ad as the most appealing-yet-controversial cell. Industry corroboration: OpenAI Instant Checkout via the Agent Commerce Protocol live late 2025; Shopify agentic storefronts to millions of merchants March 2026; Adobe Analytics roughly forty-seven-fold year-over-year generative-AI shopping-trip growth through July 2025. Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/hermann-puntoni-schweidel-conversational-monetization-2026-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  159. Erin Anderson and Anne T. Coughlan, “International Market Entry and Expansion via Independent or Integrated Channels of Distribution,” Journal of Marketing 51, no. 1 (1987): 71-82, on vertical integration driven by transaction-cost variables (asset specificity, uncertainty, frequency, product differentiation). Cited in: Ch 15
  160. Erin Anderson and Barton A. Weitz, “Make-or-Buy Decisions: Vertical Integration and Marketing Productivity,” Sloan Management Review 27, no. 3 (Spring 1986): 3-19; Erin Anderson, “The Salesperson as Outside Agent or Employee: A Transaction Cost Analysis,” Marketing Science 4, no. 3 (1985): 234-254; Geyskens, Steenkamp, and Kumar, “Make, Buy, or Ally: A Transaction Cost Theory Meta-Analysis,” Academy of Management Journal 49, no. 3 (2006): 519-543. Cited in: Ch 11
  161. EU-Mercosur Partnership Agreement (EMPA) and Interim Trade Agreement (iTA): EU Commission, “The EU-Mercosur trade agreement,” commission.europa.eu (2025-2026); EU Commission DG Trade, “EU-Mercosur agreement: text of the agreement,” policy.trade.ec.europa.eu (2026); Council of the EU press release, “EU-Mercosur: Council greenlights signature of the comprehensive partnership and trade agreement,” Jan. 9, 2026 (signature authorized) and signature event Jan. 17, 2026; Commission announcement Feb. 27, 2026 of provisional application of the iTA from May 1, 2026 once all four Mercosur signatories completed domestic procedures; political agreement reached Dec. 6, 2024 after roughly 25 years of negotiation since 1999; tariff phase-in covering 91-92 percent of bilateral exports over 15 years; projected EU exporter duty savings of more than EUR 4 billion per year (cars previously up to 35 percent, machinery 14-20 percent, pharmaceuticals up to 14 percent); tariff-rate quotas on sensitive agricultural products (beef, poultry, sugar). Synthesis at wiki/external/frontier/eu-mercosur-trade-agreement-2024-2026-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  162. Evan Hubinger, Carson Denison, Jesse Mu et al., “Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training,” Anthropic, January 12, 2024; arXiv:2401.05566 ( arxiv.org/abs/2401.05566 ; anthropic.com research note ). A backdoor planted before the safety pipeline survives supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, and red-team adversarial training; persistence scales with model size and is reinforced by chain-of-thought reasoning during training. Anthropic, “Simple Probes Can Catch Sleeper Agents,” April 2024 ( anthropic.com/research/probes-catch-sleeper-agents ): linear classifiers on residual-stream activations detect triggered defection at greater than 99 percent AUROC on the studied models. Sandbagging literature: Tom van der Weij et al., “AI Sandbagging: Language Models can Strategically Underperform on Evaluations,” arXiv:2406.07358, June 2024 ( arxiv.org/abs/2406.07358 ); Farrel Mahaztra, “Systematic Sandbagging Evaluations on Claude 3.5 Sonnet,” October 2024 ( farrelmahaztra.com/posts/sandbagging ); METR, “Update on Preliminary Evaluations of Claude 3.5 Sonnet and o1,” January 31, 2025 ( metr.org/blog/2025-01-31-update-sonnet-o1-evals ); Anthropic alignment-science blog, “Automated Researchers Can Subtly Sandbag,” 2025 ( alignment.anthropic.com/2025/automated-researchers-sandbag ); Anthropic, “Sabotage Evaluations for Frontier Models,” May 2025; Summer 2025 Pilot Sabotage Risk Report. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-hubinger-sleeper-agents-sandbagging-2024-2026-ch01.md . Companion in-context-scheming reference at ref-37 (Apollo Research). Cited in: Ch 1
  163. Evangelidis, Bhatia, Levav, and Simonson, "50 Years of Context Effects: Merging the Behavioral and Quantitative Perspectives," JCR , 51(1), May 2024, 19-28. doi:10.1093/jcr/ucad028. Half-century retrospective unifying attraction, compromise, phantom, and upscaling effects with quantitative choice models. Cited in: Ch 4
  164. Evangelidis, Levav, and Simonson, "Asymmetric Impact of Context," JMR , 55(2), 2018, 239-253. Cited in: Ch 4
  165. Evangelidis, Levav, and Simonson, "The Upscaling Effect," JCR , 50(3), Oct 2023, 492-509. Nineteen studies: symmetric decoy lifts high-desirability share. Cited in: Ch 4
  166. Fabrizio Gilardi, Meysam Alizadeh, and Maël Kubli, “ChatGPT Outperforms Crowd Workers for Text-Annotation Tasks,” PNAS 120, no. 30 (2023): e2305016120; Mellon et al., Research and Politics 11, no. 1 (2024); Ziems et al., Computational Linguistics 50, no. 1 (2024); Kakatkar et al., Marketing Letters (2025), 1,200 customer-feedback texts coded into 14 needs-based segments at $30 of API spend versus $4,800 hand-coded at 2018 rates; Christensen, The Innovator's Solution (HBSP, 2003); Ulwick, What Customers Want (McGraw-Hill, 2005). Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-needs-based-jtbd-segmentation-2024-2026-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  167. FDA, "Guidance for Industry: Consumer-Directed Broadcast Advertisements" (1999, last updated 2024); FDA OPDP enforcement letters 2024-2025 (Instagram and TikTok influencer-product-claim posts). FINRA Rule 2210 (Communications with the Public); SEC Regulation Best Interest, 17 CFR §240.15l-1 (in force June 30, 2020); SEC Marketing Rule, Rule 206(4)-1 (in force November 4, 2022). CFPB and DOJ v. Trustmark National Bank , October 22, 2021 ($9M, redlining settlement under ECOA). COPPA, 15 USC §6501 et seq.; FTC Rule, 16 CFR Part 312 (2025 amendment). FTC v. YouTube , FTC File No. 172-3083 (2019, $170M). FTC v. Epic Games , FTC File No. 192-3203 (2022, $520M total). Schedule I status of cannabis: Controlled Substances Act, 21 USC §812; DEA Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on rescheduling (May 21, 2024; final rule pending as of 2026). Murphy v. NCAA, 138 S. Ct. 1461 (2018) (PASPA struck; state sportsbook licensing regime constructed thereafter, 38 states + DC by 2026). Cited in: Ch 26
  168. Fish, Gonczarowski, Shorrer, “Algorithmic Collusion by Large Language Models,” arXiv:2404.00806 (Mar 2024, rev. v5 Mar 2026); AEA 2025 GDskRTN3. First systematic demonstration that off-the-shelf GPT-4 instances as autonomous pricing agents in repeated oligopolies converge to supracompetitive prices without explicit coordination; prompt-prefix phrasing shifts collusion intensity; reasoning-trace analysis identifies price-war anticipation as mechanism; extends Calvano-Calzolari-Denicolò-Pastorello 2020 AER Q-learning result to the LLM era. Synthesis at frontier/fish-gonczarowski-shorrer-llm-collusion-2024-2026-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  169. Fit-score model (followers × engagement × overlap × authenticity) calibrated against Leung et al. 2024 JAMS meta-analysis (doi 10.1007/s11747-024-01052-7). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/influencer-meta-analysis-2024-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  170. Forrester. (2024, December 4). The State of Business Buying, 2024 , report ID RES181797, forrester.com/report/the-state-of-business-buying-2024/RES181797; press release Forrester: To Master B2B Buying Mayhem, Providers Must Prioritize Buyers’ Needs , forrester.com/press-newsroom/forrester-the-state-of-business-buying-2024 (n=16,000 B2B buyers across industry, region, company size, and persona segmentation; 13 stakeholders per buying decision as the unweighted mean, with the median at 11, the mode at 9, and enterprise-segment deals running 20+ stakeholders; 89% of purchases crossing two or more departments with the three modal patterns being sales-and-finance, sales-and-IT, and sales-and-the-using-department; 86% of in-process purchases stalling without a vendor selection in the measurement window, including both indefinite postponement and active recommitment to the status quo; 81% of post-purchase buyers reporting that the chosen provider failed to meet expectations on at least one materially weighted dimension within twelve months; almost 95% of buyers anticipating genAI use in the buying process in the next twelve months). Companion press coverage: BusinessWire, businesswire.com/news/home/20241204021888; Nasdaq, nasdaq.com/press-release/forrester-master-b2b-buying-mayhem-providers-must-prioritize-buyers-needs-2024-12-04; Forrester blog series, forrester.com/blogs/state-of-business-buying-2024 and forrester.com/blogs/the-verdict-is-in-its-buying-groups-for-the-win. Within-buying-group conflict mechanism: Gartner. (2025). 74% of B2B Buyer Teams Demonstrate Unhealthy Conflict , gartner.com/en/sales/research/74-of-b2b-buyer-teams-demonstrate-unhealthy-conflict (n=632 buyer-team respondents, August to September 2024 fielding; three modal patterns: information conflict where the team disagrees on what the data says, preference conflict where the team disagrees on which vendor the data favors, and process conflict where the team disagrees on how to make the decision). Pre-seller-shortlist anchor: 6sense. (2025). B2B Buyer Experience Report 2025 , 6sense.com/science-of-b2b/buyer-experience-report-2025 (n≈4,000 main + 766 companion; 94% of buyers used LLMs in their buying process; 94% of buying groups had ranked their shortlist before any seller contact; first ranked vendor wins nearly 80% of the time; 83% of buyers define purchase requirements before any seller contact). Companion aggregator coverage: Corporate Visions, corporatevisions.com/blog/b2b-buying-behavior-statistics-trends; Attainment Labs, attainmentlabs.com/blog/b2b-buying-committees-doubled (enterprise-segment 20+ stakeholder upper-tail distribution). Note: chapter ref-45 cites Adamson 2015 5.4-stakeholder baseline as the conceptual ancestor; the present ref-68 supplies the 2024 production-grade replacement with the 13-stakeholder average and the three companion empirical anchors. The 86% stall figure is the buyer-side denominator complementing the seller-side 40-60% no-decision range at ref-44 (Dixon-McKenna) and the ~51% no-decision figure at ref-51 (Gong). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/forrester-state-of-business-buying-2024-2026-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  171. Fortune, “Costco’s Kirkland Signature Becomes an $86 Billion Brand,” June 4, 2024; AInvest, “How Costco Turned Kirkland Signature into an $86 Billion Brand,” May 2025. Cited in: Ch 12
  172. Fournier, Dobscha, and Mick, “Preventing the Premature Death of Relationship Marketing,” Harvard Business Review , January-February 1998. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/fournier-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2

Aguilar, Scanning the Business Environment (Macmillan, 1967), original ETPS taxonomy and the four-mode scanning typology, with replications in Daft, Sormunen, and Parks, SMJ 9, no. 2 (1988), and Hambrick, JMS 18, no. 3 (1981); canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/aguilar-scanning-business-environment-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  174. Frederick F. Reichheld and W. Earl Sasser Jr., “Zero Defections: Quality Comes to Services,” Harvard Business Review , September-October 1990, pp. 105-111: a five-percentage-point reduction in defection rate raised profits by 25 to 85 percent across nine service industries. James L. Heskett, Thomas O. Jones, Gary W. Loveman, W. Earl Sasser Jr., and Leonard A. Schlesinger, “Putting the Service Profit Chain to Work,” HBR , March-April 1994, on the seven-link causal chain from internal service quality through employee satisfaction to revenue growth. Cited in: Ch 9
  175. Frederick F. Reichheld and W. Earl Sasser, Jr., “Zero Defections: Quality Comes to Services,” Harvard Business Review 68, no. 5 (1990): 105-111; Reichheld and Thomas Teal, The Loyalty Effect (Harvard Business School Press, 1996); Reichheld, “The One Number You Need to Grow,” HBR 81, no. 12 (2003): 46-54. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/reichheld-loyalty-effect-ch12.md . Cited in: Ch 12
  176. Frederick F. Reichheld and W. Earl Sasser, “Zero Defections: Quality Comes to Services,” Harvard Business Review , vol. 68, no. 5, September-October 1990, pp. 105–111; Reichheld, The Loyalty Effect (Harvard Business School Press, 1996); Bain & Company, “Prescription for cutting costs,” 2001 retention-economics white paper. Cited in: Ch 21
  177. Frederick F. Reichheld, The Loyalty Effect (Harvard Business School Press, 1996), the book-length empirical case for loyalty-based management extending the 1990 Reichheld-Sasser HBR finding (see ref-15) into the three-loyalties (customer, employee, investor) framework and the four-channel decomposition of retention profit. Reichheld, “The One Number You Need to Grow,” Harvard Business Review , December 2003, pp. 46-54, the operationalization as Net Promoter Score with the recommendation question correlating most strongly with subsequent loyalty behavior across 4,000 customers in 6 industries. Cited in: Ch 9
  178. French-Raven channel-power taxonomy in Coughlan et al.; John R. P. French and Bertram Raven, “The Bases of Social Power,” in Cartwright, ed., Studies in Social Power (Univ. of Michigan, 1959). Caterpillar dealer-network case: Donald V. Fites, “Make Your Dealers Your Partners,” Harvard Business Review , March-April 1996. Cited in: Ch 11
  179. FTC v. Amazon (W.D. Wash. No. 2:23-cv-01495), Nov 2023 unredacted complaint; TechCrunch/The Hill/The Register on Project Nessie (Nov 2023); Oct 2024 motion-to-dismiss denial; FTC Surveillance Pricing 6(b) (Jan 2025); Deng, Schiffer, Bichler, arXiv:2406.02437 (Jun 2024); Bichler, BISE (Apr 2025). Synthesis at frontier/amazon-project-nessie-2023-2027-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  180. FTC v. Amazon.com, Inc. , No. 2:23-cv-00932 (W.D. Wash.), summary judgment ruling Sept. 17, 2025 (Judge John H. Chun); Stipulated Order for Permanent Injunction and Monetary Judgment, filed Sept. 25, 2025 ($1B civil penalty + $1.5B consumer restitution capped at $51 per eligible Prime member; five-year independent third-party compliance monitor; refund schedule Nov.-Dec. 2025 automatic + Jan. 2026 claim notices). Restore Online Shoppers' Confidence Act, 15 U.S.C. §§8401-05. Eighth Circuit vacatur of FTC Negative Option Rule ("Click-to-Cancel") on procedural grounds, July 8, 2025; FTC Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Negative Option Plans submitted to OIRA, Jan. 30, 2026. Source: FTC Case 2123050, ftc.gov/legal-library/browse/cases-proceedings/2123050-amazoncom-inc-rosca-ftc-v. Cited in: Ch 4
  181. FTC, "Guides Concerning the Use of Endorsements and Testimonials in Advertising," 16 CFR Part 255 (revised 2023; effective June 30, 2023). FDA, "Direct-to-Consumer Prescription Drug Advertisements" final rule, 21 CFR §202.1, §314.81, plus FDA OPDP guidance "Internet/Social Media Platforms with Character Space Limitations" (2014, last revised 2024). FTC v. GoodRx Holdings , FTC File No. 192-3149, settled February 1, 2023 ($1.5M civil penalty, first Health Breach Notification Rule enforcement). FTC v. Better Holdco , FTC File No. 232-3023, consent order entered 2024. Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection v. DraftKings , 2023 ($150,000 settlement re: responsible-gaming messaging). DISCUS Code of Responsible Practices for Beverage Alcohol Advertising and Marketing (revised 2024); Beer Institute Advertising and Marketing Code (revised 2022). State cannabis advertising rules: California BCC §5040, Colorado MED Rule 6-110, Washington WAC 314-55-155, Massachusetts CCC 935 CMR 500.105, Illinois 8 Ill. Adm. Code 1300.405. Mantis, traffic.cool, Surfside (cannabis-specific programmatic networks, 2024-2026 active). Cited in: Ch 26
  182. G. L. Cascio-Rizzo, J. Berger, M. Zhou, “How Hand Movements Shape Communication's Impact,” Journal of Marketing Research (forthcoming 2025). Empirical infrastructure: ~1.4 million seconds of video communication drawn from YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, conference-keynote recordings, and corporate-brand-channel content, plus controlled experimental replications. Gesture features extracted automatically via Google MediaPipe Holistic and OpenPose body-pose-estimation models (open-source computer-vision libraries that identify keypoints on the speaker's hands, wrists, elbows, and shoulders frame by frame at 30 fps), allowing the team to compute gesture intensity, gesture variety, and the temporal alignment of gestures with verbal stress beats at second-by-second resolution. Headline empirical findings: (a) hand-gesture intensity follows a dose-response pattern with the peak at moderate intensity (still hands and frenetic gesturing both reduce viewer engagement and message impact); (b) the mechanism is dual: gestures attract visual attention (the eye-tracking pathway documented in parallel by the Nelson-Field 2025 active-attention programme at ref-73) and gestures aid memory encoding by tying abstract verbal content to embodied spatial cues (the memory pathway extending the identity-language and concreteness findings the Berger Magic Words programme had documented in the verbal domain since 2014). Programme context: the paper extends the Berger Magic Words research programme (Pakefield-Dunn-Berger 2014 JPSP identity-language; Packard-Berger 2021 JCR concreteness; Pakefield-Dunn-Inesi-Berger 2017 JCR certainty markers; Magic Words , Harper Business 2023, cited at ref-57) from the verbal channel to the embodied channel, providing the brand team with a measurable optimisation target for any spokesperson-, founder-, or creator-led brand communication. Author and award context: J. Berger (Wharton) received the 2025 Sheth Foundation Award from the Journal of Marketing for the best article making long-term contributions to the field of marketing, positioning the Magic Words research programme as a canonical layer of brand-communication science. Practitioner walk-throughs and author homepages: Wharton faculty profile ; jonahberger.com ; Big-data and word-of-mouth essay ; Magic Words book page ; Penn CHIBE Q&A on Magic Words ; IDEAS/RePEc entry ; ScienceDirect cross-reference . Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/cascio-rizzo-berger-hand-movements-2025-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  183. G. Lynn Shostack, “Breaking Free from Product Marketing,” Journal of Marketing 41, no. 2 (April 1977): 73-80, the foundational article of the modern services-marketing field, arguing that services require “mirror-opposite” conceptual tools and proposing the molecular model (tangible-or-intangible nucleus surrounded by bonded elements) as the first operational alternative to the binary product/service dichotomy. Shostack, “Designing Services That Deliver,” Harvard Business Review , January-February 1984, pp. 133-139, the operational answer to the 1977 case: service blueprinting with the line of interaction, line of visibility, and line of internal interaction. Cited in: Ch 9
  184. Gabriel, Manzini, Keeling et al. (57 authors), "The Ethics of Advanced AI Assistants," Google DeepMind, April 19, 2024; arXiv:2404.16244 (274 pp.). Companion: Sussex, Anders, Manzini, Gabriel, "Should Users Trust Advanced AI Assistants?" ACM FAccT 2024. Cited in: Ch 26
  185. Gardete, Schmitt, Stahl, “Pricing and Consumption in Subscription Settings,” SSRN abstract_id=5266308 (posted 1 May 2025), Nova SBE Working Paper 674; presented at the Berkeley Stanford Israel Choice Symposium 2025 (paper_gss.pdf). Structural-empirical model on subscriber-level panel data from an online news publisher exploiting the sunk-cost feature of subscription pricing (marginal cost of consumption is zero conditional on subscribing) to identify a two-dimensional consumer-type heterogeneity: optimal consumption quantity conditional on subscribing and a separate willingness to pay for access. Headline result: promotional subscribers consume substantially more than regular-price subscribers and remain active nearly twice as long even after conditioning on active users, an inversion of the standard demand-curve prediction that the structural model attributes to type-selection at acquisition (the promotional rate over-represents high-consumption types whose price elasticity of subscription is higher because the same dollar of subscription cost spreads over more units of consumption) rather than within-type behavior change. Dual-revenue counterfactual: joint subscription-plus-advertising-revenue surface differs systematically from the subscription-revenue-only surface because higher prices screen out the high-consumption types whose impressions generate the bulk of ad inventory; the joint optimum sits below the subscription-only optimum in the news-publisher setting. Pedro Gardete, Nova School of Business and Economics (ex-Stanford GSB; Ph.D. UC Berkeley); Daniela Maria Schmitt, Nova SBE (ex-University of Mannheim 2014-2021); Florian Stahl, University of Mannheim. Synthesis at frontier/gardete-schmitt-stahl-personalized-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  186. Gartner. (2025, May 7). 74% of B2B Buyer Teams Demonstrate Unhealthy Conflict (n=632 buyers, Aug-Sep 2024); companion releases (June 25, 2025: 61% rep-free preference, 1.65x purchase regret; Aug 25, 2025: 75% will prefer human-led by 2030). Hartmann, N. N., Habel, J., & Hunter, G. K. (2026). AI agents, agentic AI, and the future of sales. JBR 202 (market $7.6B 2025 to $139B 2033). Hautamäki, P., & Heikinheimo, V. (2025). Fully leveraging AI in B2B sales. JBR 194 (n=32, manager-capability triad). Cited in: Ch 23
  187. Gary L. Frazier and Walfried M. Lassar, “Determinants of Distribution Intensity,” Journal of Marketing 60, no. 4 (October 1996): 39-51; Gary L. Frazier, “Organizing and Managing Channels of Distribution,” Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science 27, no. 2 (1999): 226-240. Cited in: Ch 11
  188. Gary Vaynerchuk, Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook: How to Tell Your Story in a Noisy Social World (HarperBusiness, 2013); Vaynerchuk, Day Trading Attention: How to Actually Build Brand and Sales in the New Social Media World (HarperCollins, May 21, 2024); Vaynerchuk, “The One Thing I Didn't Clarify Enough in Jab, Jab, Jab, Right Hook,” garyvaynerchuk.com ; VaynerMedia, “The GaryVee Content Model” (pyramid-model operations deck), 2018, s3.amazonaws.com/gv2016wp/.../GV-Content-Model-1.pdf ; Dataslayer, “LinkedIn Algorithm February 2026: What's Working Now,” dataslayer.ai/blog/linkedin-algorithm-february-2026 (verified 2026-05-10); Shno, “Short-Form Video Statistics 2026,” shno.co/marketing-statistics/short-form-video-statistics (verified 2026-05-10). Cited in: Ch 17

  190. Generative-AI text-to-image and text-to-video stack 2026 (web-verified May 2026): Google Nano Banana 2 / Gemini 3.1 Flash Image released February 26, 2026 (blog.google/innovation-and-ai/technology/ai/nano-banana-2/; cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/bringing-nano-banana-2-to-enterprise; ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/image-generation); Google Nano Banana Pro / Gemini 3 Pro Image (deepmind.google/models/gemini-image/pro/); OpenAI ChatGPT Images 2.0 / gpt-image-2 released April 21-22, 2026 (openai.com/index/introducing-chatgpt-images-2-0/); Midjourney V8 alpha March 17, 2026 and V8.1 April 30, 2026 (docs.midjourney.com/hc/en-us/articles/32199405667853-Version, midjourney.com/updates); Black Forest Labs FLUX.2 Pro / Dev / Schnell released January 15, 2026 (bfl.ai/models/flux-2, bfl.ai/pricing); Stability AI Stable Diffusion 4 Ultra (stability.ai/news, platform.stability.ai/docs/release-notes); Google DeepMind Imagen 4 and Imagen 4 Ultra released May 20, 2025 (deepmind.google/models/imagen/, developers.googleblog.com/announcing-imagen-4-fast-and-imagen-4-family-generally-available-in-the-gemini-api/); Adobe Firefly Image Model 4 plus Firefly AI Assistant launched April 2026 (blog.adobe.com/en/publish/2026/04/15/adobe-extends-leadership-video-unleashing-new-ai-powered-creation-firefly-reinventing-color-editors-in-premiere; news.adobe.com/news/2026/04/adobe-new-creative-agent). Text-to-video: Google DeepMind Veo 3.1 (October 2025, native audio, 4K up to 60fps, $0.10-0.40/sec on Vertex AI; deepmind.google/models/veo/, docs.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/docs/models/veo/3-1-generate, cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/veo-3-1-lite-and-a-new-veo-upscaling-capability-on-vertex-ai); Runway Gen-4.5 announced December 1, 2025 (runwayml.com/research/introducing-runway-gen-4.5; aibusiness.com/generative-ai/runway-releases-gen-4-5-video-model); ByteDance Seedance 2.0 February 2026; Kuaishou Kling 3.0 (kling.kuaishou.com); OpenAI Sora 2 launched September 30, 2025 (openai.com/index/sora-2/) and discontinued April 26, 2026 mobile and web with API September 24, 2026 (openai.com/sora/). LoRA fine-tuning method: Hu et al., "LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models," arXiv:2106.09685 , June 2021; Replicate brand-style LoRA training pipeline (replicate.com/docs/guides/fine-tune-an-image-model). Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/genai-creative-production-2024-2026-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  191. George Loewenstein and Drazen Prelec, "Anomalies in Intertemporal Choice: Evidence and an Interpretation," Quarterly Journal of Economics , 107(2), May 1992, 573-597, doi:10.2307/2118482, https://academic.oup.com/qje/article-abstract/107/2/573/1838331 Four documented violations of the standard exponential-discounting model (common difference effect, absolute magnitude effect, gain-loss asymmetry, delay-speedup asymmetry) reconciled by the generalized hyperbolic discount function f(t) = (1 + αt)^(-γ/α). Companion: Loewenstein and Prelec, "Preferences for Sequences of Outcomes," Psychological Review , 100(1), January 1993, 91-108, https://www.cmu.edu/dietrich/sds/docs/loewenstein/PrefSeqOutcomes.pdf (fifth regularity on rising-vs-falling sequences). David Laibson, "Golden Eggs and Hyperbolic Discounting," Quarterly Journal of Economics , 112(2), May 1997, 443-477, https://scholar.harvard.edu/files/laibson/files/golden_eggs_and_hyperbolic_discounting.pdf (quasi-hyperbolic β-δ tractable approximation). Ted O'Donoghue and Matthew Rabin, "Doing It Now or Later," American Economic Review , 89(1), March 1999, 103-124, https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257/aer.89.1.103 (sophisticated vs naive present-biased buyer formalization). Shane Frederick, George Loewenstein, and Ted O'Donoghue, "Time Discounting and Time Preference: A Critical Review," Journal of Economic Literature , 40(2), June 2002, 351-401, https://www.aeaweb.org/articles?id=10.1257%2F002205102320161311 (field consolidation review). Drazen Prelec and George Loewenstein, "The Red and the Black: Mental Accounting of Savings and Debt," Marketing Science , 17(1), 1998, 4-28, https://pubsonline.informs.org/doi/abs/10.1287/mksc.17.1.4 (double-entry coupling model for prepaid versus per-use consumption). 2024-2026 empirical anchors: Tong-il Ang and Marnik Maesen, "Research: How Buy Now, Pay Later Is Changing Consumer Spending," HBR , November 2024, https://hbr.org/2024/11/research-how-buy-now-pay-later-is-changing-consumer-spending Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, "Consumer Use of Buy Now, Pay Later: Insights from the CFPB Making Ends Meet Survey," January 2025, https://files.consumerfinance.gov/f/documents/cfpb_BNPL_Report_2025_01.pdf (53% used BNPL for purchases known to be outside budget); Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond, "Buy Now, Pay Later: A Growing Concern?," Economic Brief 26-05, 2026, https://www.richmondfed.org/publications/research/economic_brief/2026/eb_26-05 (~$70B annual U.S. transaction volume, ~20% YoY growth 2021-2025); NY Fed Staff Report 1167, https://www.newyorkfed.org/medialibrary/media/research/staff_reports/sr1167.pdf (basket-size lift from BNPL availability); Gardete, Schmitt, and Stahl, "Pricing and Consumption in Subscription Settings," Berkeley working paper, 2025, https://sics.haas.berkeley.edu/pdf_2025/paper_gss.pdf (subscription over-consumption from sunk-cost decoupling). Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/loewenstein-prelec-intertemporal-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  192. George S. Day and Paul J. H. Schoemaker, Peripheral Vision (HBR Press, 2006), the five-stage scanning apparatus and the 25-firm empirical claim that fewer than 20 percent of firms have adequate peripheral-vision capability; Day and Schoemaker, "Are You a 'Vigilant Leader'?" MIT Sloan Management Review 49, no. 3 (Spring 2008). Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/day-schoemaker-peripheral-vision-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  193. George S. Day, Strategic Management Journal 2, no. 3 (1981); Yoram Wind, JMR 15, no. 3 (1978): 317–337. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/wind-1978-segmentation-research-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  194. George S. Yip, “Global Strategy in a World of Nations?” Sloan Management Review 31, no. 1 (1989): 29-41; Total Global Strategy (Prentice-Hall, 1992); Yip and Hult, 3rd ed. (Pearson, 2012). Four drivers (market, cost, government, competitive) and five levers. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/yip-globalization-drivers-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  195. Gilardi, Alizadeh, and Kubli, “ChatGPT outperforms crowd workers for text-annotation tasks,” PNAS , vol. 120, no. 30, July 25, 2023, e2305016120, DOI: 10.1073/pnas.2305016120 . Cited in: Ch 5
  196. Girish N. Punj and David W. Stewart, “Cluster Analysis in Marketing Research: Review and Suggestions for Application,” Journal of Marketing Research 20, no. 2 (1983): 134–148, DOI 10.1177/002224378302000204; Reutterer and Dan, “Cluster Analysis,” in Homburg, Klarmann, and Vomberg, eds., Handbook of Market Research (Springer, 2020), confirming the two-stage Ward-then-K-means pipeline “continues to be common as recommended by Punj and Stewart (1983).” Two-stage methodology pairs Ward’s minimum-variance hierarchical clustering with K-means seeded from the hierarchical centroids; the deterministic warm start dominates random initialization on cluster-recovery and stability and remains the methodological substrate for behavioral-CRM segmentation where no likelihood is available, including as the pre-cluster step for variational-autoencoder and BIRCH pipelines. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/punj-stewart-cluster-analysis-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  197. Google DeepMind, "Introducing the Frontier Safety Framework," May 28, 2024 (FSF v1.0; CBRN and cyber CCL domains). Lewis Ho, Celine Smith, Claudia van der Salm, Joslyn Barnhart, Rohin Shah et al., "Updating the Frontier Safety Framework," DeepMind, February 4, 2025 (FSF v2.0; security-tier mapping onto CCLs, deceptive-alignment treatment formalized). Four Flynn, Helen King, Anca Dragan et al., "Strengthening Our Frontier Safety Framework," DeepMind, September 22, 2025 (FSF v3.0; harmful-manipulation CCL, shutdown-resistance treatment in the misalignment domain, large-scale internal deployments brought inside the safety-case-review process). FSF v3.x update appended April 17, 2026 introducing Tracked Capability Levels in select domains as an early-warning tier below the CCL. Helen King, "Protecting People from Harmful Manipulation," DeepMind, March 26, 2026 (rational-persuasion versus harmful-manipulation distinction; propensity and efficacy as the two evaluation properties; nine studies, 10,000+ participants across the United Kingdom, the United States, and India; finance and health high-stakes domains). El-Sayed et al., manipulation-mechanism taxonomy 2024 (operational basis for the v3.0 propensity evaluation). Gemini 3 Pro Frontier Safety Framework Report, November 2025 (current model-level FSF disclosure). Parallel safety-policies record: Anthropic Responsible Scaling Policy v1.0 September 19, 2023; v2.0 October 2024; v3.0 February 24, 2026. OpenAI Preparedness Framework v1 December 18, 2023; v2 April 15, 2025. Meta Frontier AI Framework February 2025; April 2025 update. METR, "Common Elements of Frontier AI Safety Policies," 2025-2026, as the cross-walk procurement teams use to translate one policy's grammar into another. Reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-deepmind-fsf-v3-manipulation-ccl-2025-2026-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  198. Goor, Keinan, and Ordabayeva, "Historizing the Present: Research Agenda and Implications for Consumer Behavior," JCP , 35(1), 2025, 98-120, doi:10.1002/jcpy.1417. Cited in: Ch 4
  199. Gordon, Moakler, Zettelmeyer, “Predicted Incrementality by Experimentation (PIE) for Ad Measurement,” arXiv 2304.06828, NBER WP 35044; out-of-sample R²=0.88 for PIE vs 0.19 for last-click, 2,226 Meta experiments; production at Amazon Advertising and Meta Incremental Attribution. Cited in: Ch 24
  200. Green and Rao, “Conjoint Measurement for Quantifying Judgmental Data,” Journal of Marketing Research , vol. 8, no. 3, 1971, pp. 355–363 (DOI 10.1177/002224377100800312); Green and Wittink, “Recovering Partworth Functions in Conjoint Analysis,” JMR , vol. 12, no. 4, 1975, pp. 421–446 (DOI 10.1177/002224377501200401); Green and Srinivasan, “Conjoint Analysis in Consumer Research: Issues and Outlook,” Journal of Consumer Research , vol. 5, no. 2, 1978, pp. 103–123; Cattin and Wittink, “Commercial Use of Conjoint Analysis: A Survey,” Journal of Marketing , vol. 46, no. 3, 1982, pp. 44–53 (698 commercial conjoint studies between 1971 and 1980; 60 percent for new-product or concept evaluation; 18 percent for pricing; annual usage doubling every two years through the late 1970s); Allenby and Rossi, “Hierarchical Bayes Conjoint Analysis: Recovery of Partworth Heterogeneity from Reduced Experimental Designs,” Marketing Science , vol. 15, no. 2, 1996 (DOI 10.1287/mksc.15.2.152); Sawtooth Software, Lighthouse Studio overview and pricing, 2026, sawtoothsoftware.com/lighthouse-studio and sawtoothsoftware.com/pricing (single-user license approximately 10,900 dollars per year, team licenses scaling to 54,500 dollars; Discover browser tool approximately 4,500 dollars per researcher per year, 15-level cap per attribute, project-import compatibility with Lighthouse Studio); Sawtooth Software, Choice-Based Conjoint Analysis methodology page, sawtoothsoftware.com/conjoint-analysis/cbc ; Orme, Getting Started with Conjoint Analysis , 4th ed., Research Publishers, 2019. Cited in: Ch 5
  201. Greenblatt et al., "Alignment Faking in Large Language Models," Anthropic and Redwood Research, Dec 18, 2024; arXiv:2412.14093. Cited in: Ch 26
  202. Gui and Toubia, “The Challenge of Using LLMs to Simulate Human Behavior,” SSRN, 2023, ssrn.com/abstract=4650172 ; Toubia et al., “Twin-2K-500,” Marketing Science , 2025. Cited in: Ch 5
  203. H. Igor Ansoff, "Managing Strategic Surprise by Response to Weak Signals," CMR 18, no. 2 (Winter 1975), the five-level signal-strength escalation model and the graduated-response framework. Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/ansoff-weak-signals-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  204. Hari Bapuji Manohar et al., “Explaining Differences in Internationalization between Emerging and Developed Economy Born Global Firms,” IJMR (2025): 148 born-global articles 2010-2023. Emerging-economy born-globals (BYD, Wipro, Infosys, MercadoLibre) tolerate higher commitment and distance than developed-economy born-globals (Spotify, Skype, Notion); two distinct archetypes the canonical Western-sample literature had over-generalized. Synthesis at wiki/external/frontier/manohar-born-globals-emerging-developed-2025-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  205. Hartmann, N. N., Habel, J., & Hunter, G. K. (2026). Artificial intelligence (AI) in sales organizations: the role of trust. Journal of Personal Selling & Sales Management , 46(1), editorial, DOI 10.1080/08853134.2026.2614068, tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/08853134.2026.2614068 (three-level trust pyramid across the salesperson-manager-leader hierarchy as the rate-limiting precondition for the routine use of AI tools across sales tasks; empirical anchors from Salesforce: only 35% of respondents trust the accuracy of the data used with AI in their organization, most respondents shared concerns regarding security risks when using generative AI, most respondents disclosed that they did not know how to use AI safely). Coverage at Researcher Life, discovery.researcher.life/article/artificial-intelligence-ai-in-sales-organizations-the-role-of-trust/1450f456b13d375bbf1abf9022150815.
