Tension-based view: 2026 paper ‘How to cope with conflicts at sales-marketing interface in the context of B2B firm’s digitalization: a tension-based view’ in Industrial Marketing Management , sciencedirect article ID S0019850126000386, sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0019850126000386 (two-type tension taxonomy: information-based tensions where the marketing side’s information-sharing demands collide with the sales side’s information-protection instincts, and power-based tensions where digitalization-driven functional power-encroachment shifts the locus of customer-relationship ownership from sales to marketing; four-moderator-variable frame: cross-functional integration and perceived-usefulness of digital tools as pre-tension moderators, cooperative-management strategy and competitive-management strategy as post-tension moderators with the cooperative posture alleviating the link between SMI tensions and task conflict and the competitive posture reinforcing the link). Conceptual ancestor: 2023 sciencedirect paper ‘The impact of digital tools on sales-marketing interactions and perceptions’ (article ID S0019850123002055, sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0019850123002055). Agentic-AI agenda: Gonzalez, G. R., Habel, J., & Hunter, G. K. (2026). AI agents, agentic AI, and the future of sales. Journal of Business Research , 202, DOI 10.1016/j.jbusres.2025.115799, sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0148296325006228 (seven-priority research agenda for the autonomous-AI-agent transition: performance effects, customer reactions, organizational-embedding, technical-development, managerial-frameworks, replacement-versus-enablement balance, broader organizational-design implications; market growth from $7.6B in 2025 to over $139B in 2033). RePEc index at ideas.repec.org/a/eee/jbrese/v202y2026ics0148296325006228.html; ResearchGate publication ID 399301095. Lay-press coverage: phys.org/news/2026-01-ai-agents-reshaping-sales-pace.html and olemiss.edu/news/2026/01/artificial-intelligence-agents-are-reshaping-sales-at-a-growing-pace/index.html. Note: chapter ref-49 cites the JBR paper but with the JPSSM-editorial author-lead attribution (Hartmann instead of Gonzalez); the present entry supplies the corrected Gonzalez-lead attribution. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/academic-frontier-trust-tension-agentic-2025-2026-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  206. Hau L. Lee, V. Padmanabhan, and Seungjin Whang, “The Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chains,” Sloan Management Review 38, no. 3 (1997): 93-102; Hau L. Lee, “The Triple-A Supply Chain,” Harvard Business Review , October 2004. Cited in: Ch 11
  207. Hau L. Lee, V. Padmanabhan, and Seungjin Whang, “The Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chains,” Sloan Management Review 38, no. 3 (Spring 1997): 93-102; companion formal-model paper, Hau L. Lee, V. Padmanabhan, and Seungjin Whang, “Information Distortion in a Supply Chain: The Bullwhip Effect,” Management Science 43, no. 4 (April 1997): 546-558, reprinted Management Science 50, no. 12 supplement (December 2004): 1875-1886, DOI 10.1287/mnsc.1040.0305, source of the four-causes taxonomy (demand-signal processing, order batching, price-fluctuation forward buying, rationing and shortage gaming), the eighty-percent grocery-channel forward-buy benchmark, the $75-100 billion U.S. grocery-channel inventory bloat figure, the HP LaserJet III rationing case, and the canonical counter-measures (point-of-sale data sharing, vendor-managed inventory, continuous replenishment programs, electronic data interchange, everyday-low pricing, allocation by historical share). Hau L. Lee, “The Triple-A Supply Chain,” Harvard Business Review 82, no. 10 (October 2004): 102-112, R0410F, hbr.org/2004/10/the-triple-a-supply-chain, source of the agility-adaptability-alignment framework, the verbatim claim that “supply chains that focus on speed and costs tend to deteriorate over time,” the six agility methods (information flow, supplier collaboration, postponement design, buffer stocks at choke points, dependable logistics, contingency planning), and the case base of more than sixty leading supply-chain operators across fifteen years. Hau L. Lee, “The New AAA Supply Chain,” California Management Review / SAGE Insight 2021, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/2694105820210101026, on the post-2020 extension with a fourth A (sustainability and responsibility). Stefano Patrucco et al., “From Triple-A to AAA+: Recalibrating the Triple-A Supply Chain for the Digital and Resilient Era,” Journal of Business Logistics 2026, DOI 10.1111/jbl.70049, on the post-2020 empirical stress test. IMD “Does the bullwhip still strike?” 2024 retrospective, imd.org/research-knowledge/supply-chain, on the persistence of the bullwhip across nearly three decades of replication including the 2021-2023 semiconductor allocation crisis. Toyota post-Tōhoku component-buffer policy as documented across the operations literature on JIT-plus-resilience hybrid designs. Cited in: Ch 11
  208. Hazem Ibrahim, HyunSeok Daniel Jang, Nouar Aldahoul, Aaron R. Kaufman, Talal Rahwan, Yasir Zaki, “Algorithmic Skew in TikTok's Political Content Recommendations During the 2024 US Presidential Election,” arXiv:2501.17831 (Jan 29, 2025; rev May 7; NYU Abu Dhabi); 323 sock-puppet audits seeded across Texas, New York, Georgia Apr 30-Nov 11, 2024; ~394K videos labeled political/partisan; Republican-seeded accounts saw ~11.8% more party-aligned recommendations, Democratic-seeded ~7.5% more opposite-party; asymmetries persisted across states and survived controls for likes/views/shares/comments/followers; mechanism decomposition attributes asymmetry primarily to negative-partisanship attack content not positive in-party promotion (arousal-favoring weighting). Largest sock-puppet TikTok partisan audit to date; three-state replication unique in literature. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ibrahim-tiktok-2024-election-partisan-skew-2025-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  209. Henry Mintzberg and James A. Waters, "Of Strategies, Deliberate and Emergent," SMJ 6, no. 3 (1985), the deliberate-emergent continuum and the eight intermediate strategy types; Mintzberg, The Rise and Fall of Strategic Planning (Free Press, 1994). Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/mintzberg-emergent-strategy-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  210. Henry Mintzberg, “Patterns in Strategy Formation,” Management Science 24, no. 9 (1978): 934-948; Mintzberg and Waters, “Of Strategies, Deliberate and Emergent,” Strategic Management Journal 6, no. 3 (1985): 257-272; Mintzberg, The Rise and Fall of Strategic Planning (Free Press, 1994). The deliberate-versus-emergent distinction the 2025 cascade revisitation reconciles via the customer-action validation cycle. Cited in: Ch 2
  211. Hermann and Puntoni, "Generative AI in Marketing and Principles for Ethical Design and Deployment," JPP&M , 44(2), 2025, doi:10.1177/07439156241309874. Cited in: Ch 4
  212. Hermann, De Freitas, and Puntoni, "Reducing Prejudice with Counter-Stereotypical AI," CPR , 8(1), January 2025, 75-86, doi:10.1002/arcp.1102. Cited in: Ch 4
  213. Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel, "Trusting AI Agents: Uncertainty Reduction by Design," SSRN/Wharton, Jan 2026, abstract_id=6103206. Three-component agent architecture crossed with three phases of consumer goal pursuit; two design levers (transparency, consumer autonomy) across four task contexts. Cited in: Ch 4
  214. Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel, “Conversational AI: The Next Frontier of Digital Platform Monetization,” SSRN 5634270, October 2025; published research on synthetic-creator engagement, Journal of Marketing 2025-2026. Cited in: Ch 20
  215. Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel, “Conversational AI: The Next Frontier of Digital Platform Monetization,” Wharton School Research Paper, October 2025, SSRN abstract id 5634270, papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5634270 . Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/frontier/ai-hermann-puntoni-schweidel-conversational-ai-monetization-2025-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  216. Hoch, Drèze, Purk, “EDLP, Hi-Lo, and Margin Arithmetic,” Journal of Marketing 58, 4 (Oct 1994): 16-27 (86-store Dominick's Finer Foods field experiments across 26 grocery categories; 10% EDLP cut produced 3% volume lift and 18% profit decline; 10% Hi-Lo posted-price increase with maintained promotional cadence produced 15% profit gain). Lal and Rao, “Supermarket Competition: The Case of Every Day Low Pricing,” Marketing Science 16, 1 (1997): 60-80 (two-segment equilibrium model: time-pressed large-basket buyers prefer EDLP, cherry-pickers prefer Hi-Lo). Bell and Lattin, “Shopping Behavior and Consumer Preference for Store Price Format: Why ‘Large Basket’ Shoppers Prefer EDLP,” Marketing Science 17, 1 (1998): 66-88 (Nielsen-panel basket-size and trip-frequency confirmation). Hydock and Wathieu, “Not Just About Price: How Benefit Focus Determines Consumers' Retailer Pricing Strategy Preference,” Journal of Consumer Research 50, 3 (Oct 2023): 447-467 (Editor's Choice; six studies, four laboratory experiments plus 160M Nielsen-panel grocery purchases 2015-2019; taste-focused buyers prefer EDLP, quality-focused buyers prefer Hi-Lo). Bolton and Shankar, “An empirically derived taxonomy of retailer pricing and promotion strategies,” Journal of Retailing 79, 4 (2003): 213-224. JCPenney “Fair and Square” February 2012 launch under Ron Johnson; 25% revenue decline within 12 months; Johnson fired April 2013 after FY2012 $552M loss. Synthesis at canonical/edlp-hilo-pricing-strategy-ch10.md ; sister retail-format reading at canonical/hoch-edlp-hilo-ch12.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  217. Holden, R. K., and Burton, M. R., Pricing with Confidence: Ten Ways to Stop Leaving Money on the Table (Wiley Finance, 2008), ISBN 978-0-470-19757-8; Audible audio edition issued 2018. Holden Advisors firm overview at holdenadvisors.com. The B2B sales-floor practitioner manual complementing Nagle-Hogan-Zale (ref-17) at the strategic level and Marn-Rosiello (ref-29) at the transaction-diagnostic level. Four-buyer-type diagnostic (price buyer, relationship buyer, value buyer, poker buyer) operationalized in Chs 2-3 as a five-minute discovery-meeting read; ten operational rules in Chs 4-13 organized as one discount-discipline mechanism in ten moves (rule one replace discount habit with value habit, rule six per-account deal-score budget, rule eight trade-offs not concessions, rule ten fiscal-quarter-end discipline); procurement counter-tactics playbook (competing-bid, deadline-pressure, go-over-your-head, split-the-difference) and the value-based responses to each. Holden Advisors field engagement record: order-of-magnitude case from Ch 1 reports approximately 17 percent revenue and 37 percent operating-profit lifts twelve months after installing the value-habit and future-cycle-math rules at a thin-margin industrial firm. Holden Advisors is one of the practitioner consultancies whose field work fed the Hinterhuber-Liozu 748-firm sample at ref-44. 2026 vendor releases that automate rule six (Pricefx Copilot, Jan 2026) and rule nine (Vendavo AI Pricing Assistant, Apr 2026) lower the friction of running the discipline but do not substitute for it. Synthesis at canonical/holden-burton-pricing-confidence-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  218. Horton et al., “Homo Silicus,” NBER WP 31122, 2023; Manning, Zhu, Horton, “Automated Social Science,” NBER WP 32381, 2024; Hewitt et al., “Predicting Social-Science Experiments with LLMs,” Stanford AI4PB, 2024. Cited in: Ch 5
  219. Howard V. Perlmutter, “The Tortuous Evolution of the Multinational Corporation,” Columbia J. World Business 4, no. 1 (1969): 9-18, the founding three-part EPG typology; regiocentric extension in Wind, Douglas, and Perlmutter, “Guidelines for Developing International Marketing Strategies,” Journal of Marketing 37, no. 2 (1973): 14-23. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/perlmutter-eprg-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  220. Hu, Sun, Xia, Li, Xu, Liu, Shu, Yu, Jiang, “AD-Bench: A Benchmark for Real-User-Request Advertising and Marketing LLM Agents,” arXiv:2602.14257, February 2026; three difficulty levels (L1-L3) with domain-expert reference answers and reference tool-call trajectories; Gemini-3-Pro 68.0 percent Pass@1 / 83.0 percent Pass@3 full benchmark, 49.4 percent Pass@1 / 62.1 percent Pass@3 / 70.1 percent trajectory coverage on L3; code at github.com/Emanual20/adbench-leaderboard. Cited in: Ch 24
  221. Huber, Payne, and Puto, "Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives," JCR , 9(1), June 1982, 90-98. Cited in: Ch 4
  222. Huber, Payne, Puto, "Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives," JCR 9, 1 (1982): 90-98. Cited in: Ch 10
  223. Hubinger et al., "Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs That Persist Through Safety Training," Anthropic, Jan 12, 2024; arXiv:2401.05566. Cited in: Ch 26
  224. Hultman, M., & McCarthy, T. M. (2026). Sales force motivation, control, and compensation in the first half of the 21st century. Journal of Personal Selling & Sales Management , DOI 10.1080/08853134.2025.2612491. First comprehensive review of the MCCI literature since the 2005 JPSSM special issue; reviews ~200 peer-reviewed papers across 2005-2025; documents the five-dimensional control taxonomy (outcome, behavior, activity, capability, cultural) extending Anderson and Oliver (1987), the contingent-fit pay-mix prior (80-20 to 50-50 along the consultative-to-transactional axis), the contingent-fit plan-period prior (matched to deal-cycle median), and the audit-by-design closing element of the four-pillar architecture. Coughlan, A. T., et al. (2025). Designing sales incentives: opportunities for research with impact. AMS Review , DOI 10.1007/s13162-025-00309-w (five-step compensation-design framework: pay level, pay mix, plan design, plan period, additional elements; AI-augmented compensation administration as the highest-density open research area for 2026-2030). Soliman, M., et al. (2025). When sales managers stop pretending to be perfect: vulnerability as a catalyst for sales coachability. JPSSM , DOI 10.1080/08853134.2025.2571551 (n=312 sales-manager-and-rep dyads across 14 firms; vulnerability-as-coachability mediator explains ~18% of variance in rep-side coachability after controlling for tenure and firm fixed effects). Cited in: Ch 23
  225. IAB and Media Rating Council, Guidelines for Incremental Measurement in Commerce Media , iab.com/guidelines/guidelines-for-incremental-measurement-in-commerce-media/ , 2024-2025 (cross-vendor methodology standard for randomized control trials, geo-holdout experiments, ghost-bidding, and Media Mix Modeling); David R. Bell, André Bonfrer, and Pradeep K. Chintagunta, “Recovering Stockkeeping-Unit-Level Preferences and Response Sensitivities from Market Share Models Estimated on Item Aggregates,” Journal of Marketing Research 42, no. 2 (2005): 169-182, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkr.42.2.169.62294 (98 weeks of toothpaste category data, ten brands, 168 SKUs; the analytical relationship between attribute-level and SKU-level model parameters that underwrites every modern SKU-level retail-media attribution claim); eMarketer, “FAQ on Incrementality: How to Prove Your Ads Actually Work in 2026,” emarketer.com/content/faq-on-incrementality-how-prove-your-ads-actually-work-2026 (52 percent of US marketers run incrementality tests; 44 percent question reliability, 43 percent struggle to apply across ad types, 41 percent cite insufficient technologies; 33 percent of CPG brands measure at only a basic level; Albertsons Media Collective $2.41 matched-market incremental ROAS and 14 percent in-store sales lift in early 2026; Google reduced minimum geo-experiment budget from ~$100,000 to $5,000 using Bayesian models); Skai, “The 2026 State of Retail Media Fragmentation,” 2026 (only 15 percent of advertisers strongly trust retail media measurement; 6 RMNs growing to a projected 11 by year-end 2026; 62 percent of buyers cite lack of measurement standards as a top challenge); eMarketer, “Media Mix Modeling Tops Incrementality in the Measurement Stack for Retail,” January 2026 (61 percent of US retail decision-makers already use MMM; 46.9 percent of US marketers plan to invest more in MMM over the next year; 27.6 percent cite MMM as the most reliable measurement methodology); Skai and Path to Purchase Institute, “Data Clean Rooms in Retail Media,” 2025 (66 percent of organizations have adopted data clean rooms in some form; 41 percent cite integration friction with existing optimization practices); Street Fight, “Retail Media's Maturity Moment: Why 2026 Changes Everything for Brands,” January 28, 2026 (US retail media ad spend projected to surpass $69 billion in 2026; incrementality testing and predictive optimization moving from differentiator to table-stakes capability). Freshness anchor (May 2026): core-course/wiki/v3-source-contributions/ch12/_freshness-anchors.md . Cited in: Ch 12
  226. Iannarino, Anthony. (2018, November 6). Eat Their Lunch: Winning Customers Away from Your Competition . Portfolio (Penguin Random House), ISBN 9780525537625, 240 pages, penguinrandomhouse.com/books/585767/eat-their-lunch-by-anthony-iannarino ; amazon.com/Eat-Their-Lunch-Customers-Competition . Origin text of the Four Levels of Value framework (Level 1 product, Level 2 service-and-experience, Level 3 business outcomes, Level 4 strategic partnership) and the Trends-Implications-Advice (TIA) opening sequence for competitive displacement; the planning guideline of approximately sixty target accounts on a thirteen-week nurture cadence. Author homepage at thesalesblog.com/anthony-iannarino documents the development of the Level 4 Value Creation methodology on The Sales Blog since 2009 and the framework synthesis at thesalesblog.com/blog/level-4-value-creation-why-you-fall-short-and-how-to-improve ; one-year retrospective at thesalesblog.com/blog/eat-their-lunch-at-one-year ; sixty-account nurture-list reasoning at thesalesblog.com/2019/08/13/prospecting-why-you-need-to-nurture-60-dream-clients ; TIA mechanics at thesalesblog.com/blog/overcoming-your-fear-of-sharing-insights ; multi-channel cadence guidance at thesalesblog.com/blog/how-to-succeed-at-b2b-sales-prospecting . Secondary-source coverage of the four-levels mechanics: book summary at sellingsherpa.com/2018/11/12/eat-their-lunch-winning-customers-away-from-your-competition-book-summary ; review at puremuir.com/sales-book-review-eat-their-lunch-by-anthony-iannarino-iannarino . Buying-group context: Adamson, B., Dixon, M., Spenner, P., & Toman, N. (2015), The Challenger Customer , Portfolio, amazon.com/Challenger-Customer-Selling-Influencer-Multiply (5.4 average buying-group size); Gartner. (2025, May 7). Gartner Sales Survey Finds 74% of B2B Buyer Teams Demonstrate Unhealthy Conflict During the Decision Process , gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-05-07 ; corporatevisions.com/blog/b2b-buying-behavior-statistics-trends (five-to-sixteen-stakeholder current range). Artificial-intelligence productivity evidence supporting the AI-assisted TIA-research workflow: Brynjolfsson, E., Li, D., & Raymond, L. R. (2025). Generative AI at Work , The Quarterly Journal of Economics , 140(2), 889-942, academic.oup.com/qje/article/140/2/889/7990658 ; NBER working paper at nber.org/papers/w31161 (15% average productivity gain on 5,172 customer-support agents, gains concentrated in less experienced workers). Cautionary boundary on automation depth: Klarna reversal coverage at entrepreneur.com/business-news/klarna-ceo-reverses-course-by-hiring-more-humans-not-ai/491396 ; customerexperiencedive.com/news/klarna-reinvests-human-talent-customer-service-AI-chatbot . Note: chapter ref-46 cites Iannarino’s 2017 Lost Art of Closing ten-commitment ladder as the operational definition of qualified opportunity; the present ref-75 supplies the earlier-published companion framework for competitive displacement, which extends the qualified-opportunity definition for the incumbent-served scenario the ladder does not directly address. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/iannarino-eat-their-lunch-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  227. Imbens & Rubin, Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences (Cambridge, 2015); potential-outcomes framework from Rubin (1974, J. Educ. Psych. ). Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics (Princeton, 2009). Wooldridge, Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data , 2nd ed. (MIT, 2010); SSRN 3906345 (2021); Goodman-Bacon, Callaway & Sant’Anna ( JoE , 2021), de Chaisemartin & D’Haultfoeuille ( AER , 2020) on staggered-adoption corrections. Cited in: Ch 5
  228. Inside Trader Joe’s podcast 2018-2024 (incl. 2024 Loyalty Programs episode); Trader Joe’s Company press 2024-2025; Worldly Partners 2025 Trader Joe’s analysis. Cited in: Ch 11
  229. Interactive Advertising Bureau, "IAB Releases Industry's First AI Transparency and Disclosure Framework to Guide Responsible Advertising in a Generative-AI Landscape," January 15 2026, https://www.iab.com/news/iab-releases-industrys-first-ai-transparency-and-disclosure-framework-to-guide-responsible-advertising-in-a-generative-ai-landscape/ (also PRNewswire, https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/iab-releases-industrys-first-ai-transparency-and-disclosure-framework-to-guide-responsible-advertising-in-a-generative-ai-landscape-302661683.html Six mandatory consumer-facing disclosure categories: AI-generated images or videos depicting fictional events; synthetic voices of deceased persons; AI voices of living persons in fabricated scenarios; digital twins of deceased individuals; digital twins of living individuals in fabricated scenarios; AI chatbots and synthetic avatars simulating human interaction. Companion consumer-research evidence: IAB and Sonata Insights consumer-perception study (Gen Z negativity 39% vs Millennial 20%; 37-point executive-vs-consumer perception gap up from 32 points in 2024; 73% of Gen Z and Millennials report disclosure would not reduce purchase intent), https://ppc.land/iab-introduces-disclosure-framework-as-gen-z-trust-in-ai-ads-plummets-19-points/ Raptive study July 2025 (suspected AI content reduces reader trust ~50%, purchase consideration -14%); Doyle et al., "Disclaimer! This Content Is AI-Generated," Journal of Interactive Advertising , 2025, doi:10.1080/15252019.2025.2554149. EU AI Act Article 50 transparency obligations effective August 2025; Article 99 fines beginning 2026. Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/iab-ai-disclosure-framework-2026-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  230. International AI Safety Report (Yoshua Bengio, chair), first edition January 29, 2025 (arXiv 2501.17805); First Key Update October 15, 2025 (arXiv 2510.13653); Second Key Update November 2025 (arXiv 2511.19863); second edition February 3, 2026. 96 contributing experts; 30-nation Expert Advisory Panel plus OECD, EU, UN. Commissioned by the 2023 Bletchley AI Safety Summit; informed the 2025 Paris AI Action Summit. Cited in: Ch 26
  231. Itamar Simonson, "Choice Based on Reasons: Attraction and Compromise Effects," JCR , 16(2), Sept 1989, 158-174. Cited in: Ch 4
  232. Iyengar and Lepper, “When Choice Is Demotivating: Can One Desire Too Much of a Good Thing?” Journal of Personality and Social Psychology , 79(6), 995-1006, 2000. Cited in: Ch 12
  233. J. Berger, Contagious: Why Things Catch On (Simon & Schuster, 2013), Chs. 2, 6. Kit Kat Coffee Break (Chorak, Hershey's, 2007): +8% year 1, +33% over twelve months; revenue $300M to $500M. BzzAgent analysis (Berger & Schwartz, JMR 48, no. 5 [2011]: 869-880): more frequently triggered products produced ~15% more sustained word of mouth. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/berger-contagious-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  234. J. Dawes, “Advertising Effectiveness and Brand Metrics: The 95-5 Rule,” Ehrenberg-Bass / LinkedIn B2B Institute working paper (2021); panel-replication summarized in J. Romaniuk, Better Brand Health (Oxford UP, 2023), Ch. 3. Across category-purchase panels the in-market share at any one week sits near 5 percent (range 2-15 percent by repurchase cycle), with ~95 percent of category buyers out-of-market. Media-planning implication: campaigns aimed only at the in-market 5 percent compete for current decisions; brand work invests in the 95 percent who will decide later, sitting in memory before the buying situation arrives. Cited in: Ch 7
  235. J. J. Brakus, B. H. Schmitt, and L. Zarantonello, “Brand Experience: What Is It? How Is It Measured? Does It Affect Loyalty?” Journal of Marketing 73, no. 3 (May 2009): 52-68, doi:10.1509/jmkg.73.3.052, SAGE . Twelve-item, 7-point Likert Brand Experience Scale (BES); four dimensions (sensory, affective, intellectual, behavioral) at three items per dimension with items 3, 5, 8, 12 reverse-coded; Cronbach’s alpha .74-.89 across six validation studies (Studies 1-3 scale construction, Study 4 Nokia test-retest reliability, Study 5 discriminant validity against involvement / attachment / delight / evaluation, Study 6 structural model with 12 brands across 6 categories). Mediation result: brand experience → brand personality β = .69 (p < .01), personality → satisfaction β = .67 (p < .01), satisfaction → loyalty β = .59 (p < .01); direct experience → loyalty β = .24 (p < .05); total effect of experience on loyalty .69, of which ~one-third runs directly and ~two-thirds runs indirectly through personality and satisfaction. Antecedent framing: B. H. Schmitt, “Experiential Marketing,” Journal of Marketing Management 15, nos. 1-3 (1999): 53-67 (five strategic experiential modules, named sense, feel, think, act, relate, carried by experience providers across communications, identity, product presence, co-branding, environments, web/digital, people). Systematic review and post-2009 corpus: D. Andreini, G. Pedeliento, L. Zarantonello, C. Solerio, “A Renaissance of Brand Experience: Advancing the Concept through a Multi-Perspective Analysis,” Journal of Business Research 91 (2018): 123-133 (74-76 empirical studies across hospitality, banking, automotive retail, fashion, services). Typology refinement: L. Zarantonello and B. H. Schmitt, “Using the Brand Experience Scale to Profile Consumers and Predict Consumer Behaviour,” Journal of Brand Management 17, no. 7 (2010): 532-540 (five experiential consumer types: hedonistic, action-oriented, holistic, inner-directed, utilitarian). Customer-experience integration: K. N. Lemon and P. C. Verhoef, “Understanding Customer Experience Throughout the Customer Journey,” JM 80, no. 6 (2016): 69-96, doi:10.1509/jm.15.0420. Canonical readers: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/brakus-schmitt-zarantonello-bes-ch07.md ; schmitt-experiential-marketing-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  236. J. Jeffrey Inman, Russell S. Winer, and Rosellina Ferraro, “The Interplay Among Category Characteristics, Customer Characteristics, and Customer Activities on In-Store Decision Making,” Journal of Marketing 73, no. 5 (2009): 19-29; Shankar, Inman, Mantrala, Kelley, and Rizley, “Innovations in Shopper Marketing,” Journal of Retailing 87, suppl. 1 (2011): S29-S42. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/inman-shopper-marketing-ch12.md . Cited in: Ch 12
  237. J. Romaniuk and S. Hartnett, "The Distinctive Asset Grid: Fame and Uniqueness as the Two Diagnostic Dimensions," Ehrenberg-Bass working paper, 2010, reproduced in Sharp 2014, Ch. 11 and in Sharp 2e, Ch. 7. Cited in: Ch 7
  238. J. Romaniuk, “Building Mental Availability,” JAR 59, no. 4 (2019): 384-398; expanded in Better Brand Health (OUP, 2023), Ch. 4. Five measurable properties of mental availability (number of links, freshness, ease of retrieval, distinctiveness, category breadth) replace legacy awareness/consideration/satisfaction tracking with Mental Market Share, Mental Penetration, Mental Advantage, CEP-link breadth, light-buyer recall. Panel-survey operationalization at $3-8K per quarter through Pollfish, Prolific, Lucid. Cited in: Ch 7
  239. J. T. Plummer, “How Personality Makes a Difference,” Journal of Advertising Research 24, no. 6 (December 1984-January 1985): 27-31; reprinted in JAR 40, no. 6 (2000): 79-83. Plummer (then EVP-Research at Young and Rubicam) separated brand image into physical, functional, and characterization components; Dr Pepper as case study. J. L. Aaker, “Dimensions of Brand Personality,” Journal of Marketing Research 34, no. 3 (1997): 347-356: 42-item, five-dimension scale (sincerity, excitement, competence, sophistication, ruggedness) constructed from 309 candidate traits across 37 brands and 631 respondents, alpha reliabilities .80-.93. Cross-cultural replication: Aaker, Benet-Martinez, Garolera, “Consumption Symbols as Carriers of Culture,” JPSP 81, no. 3 (2001): 492-508. Construct-validity critique: A. Azoulay and J.-N. Kapferer, “Do Brand Personality Scales Really Measure Brand Personality?” Journal of Brand Management 11, no. 2 (2003): 143-155. Replacement scale: M. Geuens, B. Weijters, K. De Wulf, “A New Measure of Brand Personality,” IJRM 26, no. 2 (2009): 97-107. Meta-analysis: M. Eisend and N. E. Stokburger-Sauer, “Brand Personality: A Meta-Analytic Review of Antecedents and Consequences,” Marketing Letters 24, no. 3 (2013): 205-216 (92 studies; r = .35-.50 against brand attitude, trust, purchase intent). 2023 systematic review: Vu, Tran, Nguyen, “Brand Personality: Current Insights and Future Research Directions,” Journal of Business Research 167 (2023): 114144. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/plummer-brand-personality-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  240. Jagdish N. Sheth and Rajendra S. Sisodia, The 4 A’s of Marketing: Creating Value for Customer, Company and Society (Routledge, 2012). The four customer-outcome gates (acceptability, affordability, accessibility, awareness) and the four customer roles (user, payer, seeker, selector) that the gates correspond to. Companion volumes: Rajendra Sisodia, David Wolfe, and Jagdish Sheth, Firms of Endearment: How World-Class Companies Profit from Passion and Purpose (Wharton School Publishing, 2007; 2nd ed. Pearson FT Press, 2014), with the stakeholder-relationship and long-run-financial-outperformance argument across customers, employees, suppliers, investors, communities, and the natural environment; and John Mackey and Rajendra Sisodia, Conscious Capitalism: Liberating the Heroic Spirit of Business (Harvard Business Review Press, 2013), naming the four pillars of higher purpose, stakeholder orientation, conscious leadership, and conscious culture. The intellectual lineage anchors the 2007 American Marketing Association definitional addition of “society at large.” Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sheth-sisodia-4as-conscious-capitalism-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  241. James C. Anderson, James A. Narus, and Das Narayandas, Business Market Management: Understanding, Creating, and Delivering Value , 3rd ed. (Pearson Prentice-Hall, 2009); James C. Anderson and James A. Narus, “A Model of Distributor Firm and Manufacturer Firm Working Partnerships,” Journal of Marketing 54, no. 1 (1990): 42-58; Anderson, Narus, and van Rossum, “Customer Value Propositions in Business Markets,” Harvard Business Review , March 2006. Cited in: Ch 11
  242. James E. Grunig and Todd Hunt, Managing Public Relations (Holt, Rinehart, and Winston, 1984), the four-models framework. James E. Grunig, Larissa A. Grunig, and David M. Dozier, Excellent Public Relations and Effective Organizations: A Study of Communication Management in Three Countries (Lawrence Erlbaum, 2002), the empirical Excellence Project across 327 organizations. James E. Grunig and Larissa A. Grunig, "Excellence Theory in Public Relations: Past, Present, and Future," Journal of Public Relations Research , 20(3), 2008, 327-342. Wiki synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/grunig-hunt-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22
  243. James R. Bettman, An Information Processing Theory of Consumer Choice (Addison-Wesley, 1979); Bettman, Luce, and Payne, "Constructive Consumer Choice Processes," JCR , 25(3), December 1998, 187-217; Payne, Bettman, and Johnson, The Adaptive Decision Maker (Cambridge UP, 1993). Seven-component cognitive architecture; scanner / interrupt mechanism as the operational ancestor of System 1 / System 2; six-heuristic catalog (lexicographic, elimination by aspects, conjunctive, disjunctive, additive, additive difference). Cited in: Ch 4
  244. Jan Johanson and Jan-Erik Vahlne, “The Internationalization Process of the Firm,” JIBS 8, no. 1 (1977): 23-32, the founding Uppsala model; revised in JIBS 40, no. 9 (2009): 1411-1431; born-global counter-pattern in Oviatt and McDougall, JIBS 25, no. 1 (1994): 45-64. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/johanson-vahlne-uppsala-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  245. Jan Roelf Bult and Tom Wansbeek, “Optimal Selection for Direct Mail,” Marketing Science , vol. 14, no. 4, 1995, pp. 378–394; foundational direct-marketing work in Hughes, Strategic Database Marketing , 4th ed. (McGraw-Hill, 2011). Cited in: Ch 21
  246. Jan Roelf Bult and Tom Wansbeek, “Optimal Selection for Direct Mail,” Marketing Science 14, no. 4 (1995): 378-394 (formalization of RFM-based selection); Arthur M. Hughes, Strategic Database Marketing , 4th ed. (McGraw-Hill, 2012); Sephora Beauty Insider tier disclosures via LoyaltyLion case study and 99minds analysis, 2024-2025. Cited in: Ch 12
  247. Jan-Benedict E. M. Steenkamp, Rajeev Batra, and Dana L. Alden, “How Perceived Brand Globalness Creates Brand Value,” JIBS 34, no. 1 (2003): 53-65 (PBG/PBL construct, 2,200+ citations); Steenkamp, Global Brand Strategy (Palgrave, 2017), COMET framework + four global-brand archetypes; Liu, Schoefer, Fastoso, Tzemou, JIM 29, no. 2 (2021): 1-19. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/steenkamp-global-brand-strategy-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  248. Janiszewski, Noel, and Sawyer, "A Meta-Analysis of the Spacing Effect in Verbal Learning," JCR , 30(1), June 2003, 138-149. Cited in: Ch 4
  249. Jason D. Lin, Nicole You Jeung Kim, Esther Uduehi, and Anat Keinan, “Culture for Sale: Unpacking Consumer Perceptions of Cultural Appropriation,” Journal of Consumer Research 51, no. 3 (October 2024): 571-594. The first peer-reviewed scale measuring consumer perceptions of cultural appropriation in branding, identifying a historical-power-imbalance lens with three independent brand-action determinants (obtaining, representing, contextualizing). Anat Keinan was awarded the 2026 SCP Distinguished Scientific Contribution Award (March 30, 2026) at the Society for Consumer Psychology Annual Conference in San Diego, recognizing fifteen-plus years of research on luxury, authenticity, symbolic consumption, and consumer well-being across JCR , JCP , and Marketing Science , including six JCR Best Paper Award finalist nominations (2009, 2014, 2017, 2020, 2023, 2024) and the AMA Erin Anderson Award. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/keinan-cultural-appropriation-2024-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2 Ch 15
  250. Jenni Romaniuk and Jane Hartnett, "Shape-based assets are strongest: benchmarking distinctive brand asset performance across industries," International Journal of Advertising , March 2026, DOI 10.1080/02650487.2026.2637295. Ehrenberg-Bass-affiliated empirical sample: 1,162 distinctive brand assets across 21 categories in four countries over a nine-year window. Per-type Fame/Uniqueness benchmarks: shape-based 40/71, word ~25/~55, auditory ~22/~52, color 12/39. Companion papers: Williams, Hartnett, and Trinh, "Finding creative drivers of advertising effectiveness with modern data analysis," Marketing Theory , 2023; Bali, Anesbury, Phua, and Sharp, AI-human asset-prevalence measurement, Marketing Theory , 2024. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/romaniuk-hartnett-shape-based-assets-2026-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  251. Jenni Romaniuk, "Category Entry Points Dissected," April 2025; Ehrenberg-Bass Institute, "Category Entry Points: The Last Two Strategic Pitfalls Unraveled," 2025. Growth-vs-refresh CEP distinction. Cited in: Ch 4
  252. Jenni Romaniuk, Better Brand Health (Oxford, 2023), distinctiveness grid + 7W category entry points; Romaniuk, “Category Entry Points Dissected,” Marketing Science (April 8, 2025); Ritson, Marketing Week, 2024-25, “Double D”; Kantar BrandZ 2024 (40% growth distinctiveness / 49% pricing power differentiation). Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/positioning-distinctiveness-2024-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  253. Jenni Romaniuk, Building Distinctive Brand Assets (Oxford University Press, 2018); Romaniuk and Hartnett, "The Distinctive Asset Grid," Ehrenberg-Bass Institute working paper, 2010; Romaniuk, Better Brand Health (Oxford University Press, 2023). Course-internal syntheses: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sharp-romaniuk-ch16.md and core-course/wiki/external/canonical/romaniuk-building-distinctive-brand-assets-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  254. Jennifer L. Aaker, "Dimensions of Brand Personality," JMR , 34, August 1997, 347-356. Cited in: Ch 4
  255. Jim O'Neill, “Building Better Global Economic BRICs,” Goldman Sachs Global Economics Paper No. 66, November 30, 2001, the original BRIC paper. South Africa joined in 2010 to form BRICS; further expansions to BRICS+ followed in 2024-2025. Macroeconomic data from IMF World Economic Outlook 2025. Cited in: Ch 15
  256. Jochen Hartmann et al., "The power of generative marketing," IJRM 42(1):13-31, 2025; Anuj Kapoor and Madhav Kumar, "Frontiers: Generative AI and Personalized Video Advertisements," Marketing Science 44(4), 2025; Fabrizio Dell'Acqua, Ethan Mollick et al., "Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier," Organization Science , 2025. Course-internal syntheses: frontier/ai-hartmann-superhuman-marketing-imagery-ch16.md ; frontier/ai-kumar-kapoor-personalized-video-ads-ch16.md ; frontier/ai-mollick-jagged-frontier-creative-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  257. Joel H. Steckel and Wilfried R. Vanhonacker, “Cross-Validating Regression Models in Marketing Research,” Marketing Science 12, no. 4 (1993): 415–427; addendum, Marketing Science 15, no. 1 (1996): 109. Cited in: Ch 6
  258. Joel H. Steckel, Wayne S. DeSarbo, and Vijay Mahajan, “On the Creation of Acceptable Conjoint Analysis Experimental Designs,” Decision Sciences 22, no. 2 (1991): 435–442. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/steckel-segmentation-methodology-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  259. John Caples, Tested Advertising Methods (Harper, 1932; Prentice-Hall 5th ed., 1997), keyed-coupon split testing and up-to-19.5x lift between best/worst headlines; Rosser Reeves, Reality in Advertising (Knopf, 1961), USP framework, Anacin $18M-to-$54M case pp. 55-58, Wonder Bread Builds strong bodies twelve ways . Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/reeves-caples-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  260. John Gattorna, Dynamic Supply Chains: How to design, build and manage people-centric value networks , 3rd ed. (FT Publishing / Pearson, 2015), ISBN 978-1-292-08679-8; companion volume Dynamic Supply Chain Alignment: A New Business Model for Peak Performance in Enterprise Supply Chains Across All Geographies (Gower Publishing, 2009; Routledge reissue 2025, ISBN 978-1-032-83808-3). Canonical source for the supply-chain-segmentation-by-buyer-behavior thesis, the four-archetype taxonomy (continuous replenishment for brand-loyal repeat buyers, lean for transactional buyers, agile for opportunistic buyers, fully flexible for lumpy-demand innovation-seeking buyers), the outside-in design sequence, and the one-company-multiple-supply-chains principle. Empirical base: Cap Gemini Ernst & Young supply-chain benchmarking dataset and Gattorna’s consulting practice in Australia, the UK, and Southeast Asia. Restated for the post-pandemic era in “Dynamic Supply Chains with Dr. John Gattorna,” Lokad TV , January 19, 2022. Cited in: Ch 11
  261. John H. Dunning, “Trade, Location of Economic Activity and the MNE: A Search for an Eclectic Approach,” in Bertil Ohlin, Per-Ove Hesselborn, and Per-Magnus Wijkman, eds., The International Allocation of Economic Activity (Macmillan, 1977), the founding statement of the OLI paradigm; Multinational Enterprises and the Global Economy (Addison-Wesley, 1993), the four-motive taxonomy (resource-seeking, market-seeking, efficiency-seeking, strategic-asset-seeking); “The Eclectic (OLI) Paradigm of International Production: Past, Present and Future,” International Journal of the Economics of Business 8, no. 2 (2001): 173-190. Buckley and Casson 2009, “The internalisation theory of the multinational enterprise,” JIBS 40, no. 9: 1563-1580 (the I axis made self-contained). Digital-economy extensions in Banalieva and Dhanaraj, “Internalization theory for the digital economy,” JIBS 50, no. 8 (2019): 1372-1387; Stallkamp and Schotter, Global Strategy Journal 11, no. 1 (2021): 58-80; Lee, Cavusgil, Knight, Global Strategy Journal 13, no. 4 (2023): 819-846; Tran et al., “Digital transformation as a catalyst for foreign direct investment,” Cogent Economics & Finance 13, no. 1 (2025). Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/dunning-eclectic-oli-paradigm-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  262. John Philip Jones (Newhouse School of Public Communications, Syracuse, 1981-2024), When Ads Work: New Proof That Advertising Triggers Sales (The Free Press / Simon & Schuster, 1995), ISBN 0-02-916662-4; Jones, "Single-source research begins to fulfill its promise," Journal of Advertising Research 35(3), 1995 (JAR Best Article Award winner). The 1991 A.C. Nielsen Household Panel of ~2,000 U.S. households linked TV exposure logs to scanner purchases at the household ID across 78 packaged-goods brands and 12 categories; the STAS-Differential = (Stimulated STAS / Baseline STAS) × 100. Validation: McDonald and Riebe-Eliason (BehaviorScan split-cable replication), Journal of Advertising Research , 1995; International Journal of Advertising print-and-TV extension, 2006; Springer logit-modeling formalization separating advertising from promotion effects, 2015. Modern implementation: NCSolutions (formerly Nielsen Catalina Solutions), household-panel + retailer-scanner + TV-exposure architecture (nielsen.com/insights/2017/measuring-the-impact-of-advertising-one-purchase-at-a-time/; nielsen.com/insights/2021/4-ways-modern-marketers-are-using-attribution-to-drive-more-roi/; nielsen.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2019/04/njm-using-single-source-data-to-measure-advertising-effectiveness.pdf). Single-source-data definition: Wikipedia citing Colin McDonald (BMRB, 1966). Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/jones-stas-single-source-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  263. John R. Hauser and Don Clausing, “The House of Quality,” Harvard Business Review , May–June 1988. Yoji Akao and Shigeru Mizuno (eds.), QFD: The Customer-Driven Approach to Quality Planning and Deployment (Asian Productivity Organization, 1994). Toyota Auto Body QFD case via Hauser and Clausing 1988. Puritan-Bennett QFD application: MIT Sloan Management Review , Spring 1991. Cited in: Ch 8
  264. John R. Hauser and Frank S. Koppelman, “Alternative Perceptual Mapping Techniques,” JMR 16, no. 4 (1979): 495–506. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hauser-koppelman-perceptual-maps-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  265. John T. Mentzer, William DeWitt, James S. Keebler, Soonhong Min, Nancy W. Nix, Carlo D. Smith, and Zach G. Zacharia, “Defining Supply Chain Management,” Journal of Business Logistics 22, no. 2 (2001): 1-25, DOI 10.1002/j.2158-1592.2001.tb00001.x, source of the canonical SCM definition (the systemic, strategic coordination of business functions within and across firms for long-term performance), the three-degrees complexity ladder (direct, extended, ultimate supply chain), the seven SCM activities (integrated behavior, mutual information sharing, mutual risk-and-reward sharing, cooperation, customer-service focus, process integration, long-term partnership building), and the SCO/SCM distinction (orientation as philosophy, management as overt action). Cited 5,232 times as of 2025; lead article of JBL Volume 22, Issue 2. Cited in: Ch 11
  266. Jonah Berger and Eric M. Schwartz, “What Drives Immediate and Ongoing Word of Mouth,” JMR 48, no. 5 (Oct 2011): 869–880. Cited in: Ch 19
  267. Jonah Berger and Katherine L. Milkman, "What Makes Online Content Viral?" Journal of Marketing Research 49(2):192-205, April 2012. Six-month web crawl of approximately seven thousand New York Times articles; awe +30 percent, anger +34 percent, anxiety +21 percent, sadness -16 percent on the Most-E-Mailed list. Cited in: Ch 16
  268. Jonah Berger and Katherine L. Milkman, “What Makes Online Content Viral,” JMR 49, no. 2 (Apr 2012): 192–205. Cited in: Ch 19
  269. Jonah Berger and Raghuram Iyengar, “Communication Channels and Word of Mouth: How the Medium Shapes the Message,” JCR 40, no. 3 (Oct 2013): 567–579; 6,500-brand cross-category analysis. Cited in: Ch 19
  270. Jonah Berger, Contagious: Why Things Catch On (Simon & Schuster, 2013), STEPPS framework pp. 21-25; word-of-mouth effectiveness pp. 7-9 citing Bughin et al. 2010 McKinsey, Godes & Mayzlin 2009 Marketing Science , Chevalier & Mayzlin 2006 JMR ; Keller Fay Group offline-WOM data. Cited in: Ch 16
  271. Jonah Berger, Magic Words: The New Science of Language for Persuasion, Communication, and Driving Action (HarperCollins, Mar 2023); Packard and Berger, “How Concrete Language Shapes Customer Satisfaction,” JCR 47, no. 5 (2021): 787–806. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/berger-magic-words-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  272. Jordan Hoffmann, Sebastian Borgeaud, Arthur Mensch, et al. (2022, March 29). “Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models.” arXiv:2203.15556 (NeurIPS 2022). The Chinchilla paper: 400+ transformer runs (70M to 16B params) fit loss curves agreeing on one rule, that for fixed compute, model size and training-token count should scale equally. A 70B-parameter Chinchilla on 1.4T tokens beat the 280B Gopher (same compute) and GPT-3 (175B) by 23.7 pp on MMLU. The empirical foundation behind the inference-cost decline at ref-19, ref-30, ref-31. arxiv.org/abs/2203.15556 . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-hoffmann-chinchilla-scaling-laws-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  273. Joseph Korkames, T. D. Stanley, and Stefan Stremersch, "Meta-analysis of advertising effectiveness: New insights from improved bias corrections," IJRM , 2025. 538 own-brand elasticities reanalyzed with FAT-PET, PEESE, weighted least squares meta-regression, and the Stanley-Doucouliagos kinked approach. Corrected averages 0.0008 short-term and 0.03 long-term, against Sethuraman, Tellis, and Briesch (2011) JMR averages of ~0.12 and 0.24. Aligned with Lewis and Rao (2015) QJE ; Gordon et al. (2019) Marketing Science ; Shapiro, Hitsch, and Tuchman (2021) QJE . Course-internal synthesis: frontier/korkames-stanley-stremersch-2025-elasticities-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  274. Joseph L. Bower and Clayton M. Christensen, "Disruptive Technologies: Catching the Wave," Harvard Business Review 73, no. 1 (January-February 1995): 43-53; Clayton M. Christensen, The Innovator's Dilemma (Harvard Business School Press, 1997), the disk-drive industry analysis and the resource-allocation argument for why incumbent leaders systematically misread disruptive entrants; Christensen, Raynor, and McDonald, "What Is Disruptive Innovation?", HBR 93, no. 12 (December 2015): 44-53, the twentieth-anniversary tightening of the definition. Cited in: Ch 3
  275. Joy Buolamwini and Timnit Gebru, "Gender Shades: Intersectional Accuracy Disparities in Commercial Gender Classification," Proceedings of the 1st Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency , PMLR 81 (2018): 77-91 (proceedings.mlr.press/v81/buolamwini18a.html). MIT News, "Study finds gender and skin-type bias in commercial artificial-intelligence systems," February 11, 2018 (news.mit.edu/2018/study-finds-gender-skin-type-bias-artificial-intelligence-systems-0212). Pilot Parliaments Benchmark dataset card and disaggregated error rates: gendershades.org/overview.html. Algorithmic Justice League, founded MIT Media Lab 2016 (ajl.org/about): four-principle stack (affirmative consent, meaningful transparency, continuous oversight, actionable critique). Raji and Buolamwini, "Actionable Auditing," AIES 2019 (8-month vendor remediation: IBM 34.7 to 16.97 percent, Microsoft 20.8 to 1.52 percent). Reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/buolamwini-gender-shades-algorithmic-justice-league-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  276. Justin Mares and Gabriel Weinberg, Traction: A Startup Guide to Getting Customers (S-Curves Publishing, 2014; Portfolio reissue 2015), Ch. 11 on email as a traction channel; Bryan Eisenberg and Jeffrey Eisenberg, Call to Action (Thomas Nelson, 2006), AIDA-derived email frameworks. Cited in: Ch 21
  277. Kahneman, Knetsch, Thaler, "Fairness as a Constraint on Profit Seeking," AER 76, 4 (1986): 728-741; Xia, Monroe, Cox, "The Price Is Unfair!" JM 68, 4 (2004). Cited in: Ch 10
  278. Kahneman, Knetsch, Thaler, “Experimental Tests of the Endowment Effect and the Coase Theorem,” Journal of Political Economy 98, 6 (1990): 1325-1348. Cornell undergraduates randomly assigned a $6 coffee mug demanded a median $7.12 WTA to part with it, while a separate group offered the same mug paid only a median $2.87 WTP to acquire it (a roughly 2.5x WTA-vs-WTP gap that held across induced-value and goods-trading protocol variants). The earlier Knetsch, Sinden, “Willingness to Pay and Compensation Demanded,” QJE 99, 3 (1984): 507-521 priced-vs-endowed lottery-ticket protocol established the asymmetry pattern; Knetsch, “The Endowment Effect and Evidence of Nonreversible Indifference Curves,” AER 79, 5 (1989): 1277-1284 generalized to mugs and candy bars. List, “Does Market Experience Eliminate Market Anomalies?” QJE 118, 1 (2003): 41-71 documents attenuation among experienced traders; Plott and Zeiler, “The Willingness to Pay-Willingness to Accept Gap,” AER 95, 3 (2005): 530-545 contests procedural confounds. The pricing-strategy reading: ownership instantly raises the buyer’s reference price, which is why free trials lift conversion (cancellation reads as loss), trade-ins outperform equivalent cash rebates (firm absorbs the endowment markup), and incumbent products carry the loss-aversion premium that Gourville’s 9x adoption barrier (§10.9) multiplies into the demand wall every new entrant faces. Synthesis at canonical/kahneman-knetsch-thaler-endowment-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  279. Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (FSG, 2011); Kahneman and Tversky, "Prospect Theory," Econometrica 47, 2 (1979): 263-291; Thaler, "Mental Accounting and Consumer Choice," Marketing Science 4, 3 (1985); Misbehaving (Norton, 2015). Cited in: Ch 10
  280. Kaiser and Schulze, "ChatGPT Referrals to E-Commerce Websites," Marketing Science , April 2026. doi:10.1287/mksc.2025.0489 Cited in: Ch 4
  281. Kaplan and Haenlein, “Users of the World, Unite!,” Business Horizons 53, no. 1 (2010): 59–68 (2x2 on social-presence/media-richness and self-presentation/self-disclosure; six cells). Underlying theory: Short, Williams, Christie, Social Psychology of Telecommunications (Wiley, 1976); Daft-Lengel, Management Science 32, no. 5 (1986): 554–571; Goffman, Presentation of Self in Everyday Life (Doubleday, 1959). Distinct from Kietzmann et al., Business Horizons 54, no. 3 (2011): 241–251 (seven-cell honeycomb). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/kaplan-haenlein-social-media-classification-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  282. Katherine N. Lemon and Peter C. Verhoef, “Understanding Customer Experience Throughout the Customer Journey,” Journal of Marketing 80, no. 6 (2016): 69-96; Verhoef et al., “Customer Experience Creation,” Journal of Retailing 85, no. 1 (2009): 31-41. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/lemon-verhoef-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  283. Kathleen Fearn-Banks, Crisis Communications: A Casebook Approach , 1st ed. (Lawrence Erlbaum, 1996); Kathleen Fearn-Banks and Kevin Kawamoto, 6th ed. (Routledge, 2024). Robert L. Heath (ed.), The SAGE Handbook of Public Relations , 1st ed. (SAGE, 2010), 2nd ed. (SAGE, 2018), 773 pp., the comprehensive scholarly reference for the field. Wiki syntheses: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/fearn-banks-casebook-ch22.md and core-course/wiki/external/canonical/heath-sage-handbook-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22
  284. Khwan Kim (UCL) and Manav Raj (Wharton), “Catching Lightning: Social Media Virality, Performance, and Strategy in a Creative Industry,” SSRN Working Paper 5682404, October 30, 2025 (https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=5682404 Difference-in-differences identification on Spotify streaming-performance panel matched to unexpected TikTok virality events, with parallel-trends defense from the plausibly-exogenous timing of third-party-creator viral videos using an artist's audio. Headline finding: TikTok virality produces a measurable, durable lift in streaming performance that persists well past the viral moment, with the largest effects for artists with smaller pre-virality listener bases and for artists whose producers released or re-released material inside the viral window. Authors' framing: strategic behavior turns viral luck into “longer-lasting serendipitous advantage in digital markets.” Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/catching-lightning-virality-persistence-ch19.md (sister to ref-4 TikTok algorithm-monolith persistence audits and ref-16 Liquid Death seed-round; closes the chapter's empirical gap on whether earned virality is a flow or a stock and supplies the release-readiness moderator that the §19.7 application recipes already imply). Cited in: Ch 19
  285. Khwan Kim and Manav Raj, “Catching Lightning: Social Media Virality, Performance, and Strategy in a Creative Industry,” SSRN WP 5682404, Oct 2025; difference-in-differences on Spotify streaming panel matched to TikTok virality; release-timing moderator results UCL Mgmt seminar Nov 2025. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/catching-lightning-virality-persistence-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  286. Klarna press release, Feb 27, 2024 (2.3M conversations, ~700 full-time agents equivalent). NVIDIA NeMo Guardrails (Rebedea et al., arXiv:2310.10501); OpenAI Moderation API (Sept 2024, ~150ms median); Lakera Guard (under 50ms p95); AWS Bedrock Guardrails (April 2024 GA); Azure AI Content Safety (March 2024 GA). Anthropic, "Best practices for building AI agents," May 2024. Cited in: Ch 26
  287. Kobayashi, Johnson, Gu, “Privacy-Enhanced versus Traditional Retargeting,” SSRN 4972368 (Sep 2024); SSRN 5319372 (2025); 4.6 percent retargeting incrementality, 46.3 percent click-recovery, 43.5 percent click-through-conversion-recovery, 2,000+ advertisers. Cited in: Ch 24
  288. Koshkin, Dai, Fujikawa, Togami, Visentini-Scarzanella, “MaRGen: A Multi-Agent Framework for Autonomous Business Analysis and Market Report Generation,” arXiv:2508.01370, August 2025; four-agent topology (Researcher, Reviewer, Writer, Retriever) with in-context learning from Amazon-consultant presentation materials, 6-page reports in ~7 minutes at ~$1, LLM-judged with human alignment; ablations on iterative review cycles and consultants’ unstructured knowledge. Cited in: Ch 24
  289. Kotler, P., Rackham, N., & Krishnaswamy, S. (2006). Ending the War Between Sales and Marketing. Harvard Business Review 84(7-8), 68-78. Four-stage maturity taxonomy (Undefined, Defined, Aligned, Integrated). Homburg, C., Jensen, O., & Krohmer, H. (2008). Configurations of Marketing and Sales. Journal of Marketing 72(2), 133-154 (n=337, structural linkages and marketing’s market knowledge separate high- from low-performing configurations). Cited in: Ch 23
  290. Kotler, Philip, Rackham, Neil, & Krishnaswamy, Suj. (2006, July-August). Ending the War Between Sales and Marketing. Harvard Business Review , 84(7-8), 68-78. Harvard Business School Publishing reprint R0607E, hbr.org/2006/07/ending-the-war-between-sales-and-marketing ; HBR Store product page at store.hbr.org/product/ending-the-war-between-sales-and-marketing/R0607E ; PubMed record at pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16846190 ; Northwestern Scholars publication record at scholars.northwestern.edu/en/publications/ending-the-war-between-sales-amp-marketing ; ResearchGate record at researchgate.net/publication/6939995 . Origin text of the four-stage alignment-maturity taxonomy (Undefined → Defined → Aligned → Integrated) the chapter teaches at §23.5, with the diagnostic that maps a firm to a rung by reading its written documents and scorecards rather than by reading its self-report, and the rung-specific remedy menu the paper recommends for each progression step. The paper also names the three structural conflict sources the alignment work has to defuse: an economic conflict over the marketing-dollars-versus-sales-headcount allocation question, a cultural conflict between marketing’s longer planning horizon and sales’s monthly quota cadence, and an organizational conflict over which function owns the customer once the deal closes. The 2025 revenue-operations maturity literature (chapter ref-52) and the post-2020 Chief Revenue Officer role expansion across SaaS firms together instantiate the Integrated rung the 2006 paper described as the top of the ladder; the HubSpot SLA case at chapter ref-37 instantiates the Defined-to-Aligned move the paper recommends as the highest-leverage early step. Follow-on Kotler-Rackham 2018 interview at marketingjournal.org/ending-the-war-between-sales-and-marketing-an-interview-with-philip-kotler-and-neil-rackham documents the realignment of boundaries between the two functions in the post-digital decade. Companion authoring note at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/kotler-rackham-krishnaswamy-2006-hbr-ch23.md (queued for full reader-write per Section H-Ch14-01 dispatch policy). Cited in: Ch 23
  291. Krakovna et al. (DeepMind), "Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity," April 2020 (70+ documented cases by 2025). Bondarenko et al., "Demonstrating specification gaming in reasoning models," arXiv:2502.13295 (Feb 2025); six-model evaluation on chess against Stockfish. Cited in: Ch 26
  292. Kusum L. Ailawadi and Paul W. Farris, “Managing Multi- and Omni-Channel Distribution,” Journal of Retailing , 2017, on the operational definition of omni-channel and the integration metrics that distinguish it from multichannel. Cited in: Ch 11
  293. Kwa, West, Becker, et al., “Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks,” METR, March 19, 2025, arXiv: 2503.14499 ; METR, “Time Horizon 1.1,” January 29, 2026, post-2023 doubling time of 131 days. Cited in: Ch 25
  294. Kwa, West, Becker, et al., “Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks,” METR, March 19, 2025, arXiv: 2503.14499 ; post-2023 doubling time of 131 days in METR, “Time Horizon 1.1,” January 29, 2026. Cited in: Ch 5
  295. L. de Chernatony and M. McDonald, Creating Powerful Brands , 4th ed. with E. Wallace (Routledge, 2010; first edition Butterworth-Heinemann, 1992). UK canonical brand-management synthesis. Working definition of the brand as “a cluster of functional and emotional values that enables a promise to be made about a unique and welcomed experience”: de Chernatony, “Towards the holy grail of defining ‘brand,’” Marketing Theory 9, no. 1 (2009): 101-105, doi:10.1177/1470593108100063. Eight-component atomic model (functional capabilities, distinctive name, legal protection, shorthand symbol, risk reducer, identity system, image in buyers' minds, value system): de Chernatony and Daniels, “Modelling the Components of the Brand,” European Journal of Marketing 32, nos. 11/12 (1998): 1074-1090; with earlier formulation in de Chernatony, “Formulating Brand Strategy,” European Management Journal 9, no. 2 (1991): 194-200, doi:10.1016/0263-2373(91)90084-4. Brand-pyramid five-level laddering (attributes, functional benefits, emotional rewards, values, brand personality) and vision-to-evaluation process chain: L. de Chernatony, From Brand Vision to Brand Evaluation , 3rd ed. (Butterworth-Heinemann / Elsevier, 2010). UK tradition essay: de Chernatony, “The Brand Management Odyssey,” Journal of Marketing Management 12, no. 4 (1996): 271-284, doi:10.1080/0267257X.1996.9964410. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/de-chernatony-mcdonald-creating-powerful-brands-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  296. Langer, E. J., Blank, A., & Chanowitz, B. (1978). The mindlessness of ostensibly thoughtful action: The role of “placebic” information in interpersonal interaction. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology , 36(6), 635-642. Cited in: Ch 23
  297. Latanya Sweeney, "k-Anonymity: A Model for Protecting Privacy," Int'l J. Uncertainty 10, no. 5 (2002): 557-570. 87.1 percent of US population uniquely identified by {ZIP, gender, date of birth} (Sweeney 2000; replicated on 2010 census). 1997 Weld re-identification (Massachusetts GIC linked to Cambridge voter list, $20). CJEU Breyer , C-582/14 (Oct 2016): personal data when re-identification is reasonably likely. Differential-privacy descendants: Dwork et al. 2006; US Census 2020 DP release. Cited in: Ch 26
  298. Lauren Frank, Gabriele Pizzi, and Mark-Anthony Camilleri, "Consumer Acceptance of High-Autonomy AI Assistants Is Driven by Perceived Benefits in Online Shopping Settings Characterized by Scarcity," Psychology & Marketing , 2026, doi:10.1002/mar.70074, https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mar.70074 One behavioral field experiment and four controlled online experiments with roughly two thousand United States and United Kingdom shoppers; scarcity reverses the usual algorithm-aversion preference and drives consumers to choose the higher-autonomy artificial intelligence agent over the lower-autonomy curated shortlist; the mechanism runs through perceived experiential benefit rather than perceived functional benefit. Companion: Frank et al., "Autonomy, Power and the Special Case of Scarcity: Consumer Adoption of Highly Autonomous Artificial Intelligence," British Journal of Management , 2024, doi:10.1111/1467-8551.12780, https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1467-8551.12780 Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-frank-pizzi-camilleri-scarcity-autonomy-2026-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  299. Lavidge and Steiner, “A Model for Predictive Measurements of Advertising Effectiveness,” JM 25(6), Oct 1961, 59-62; six-rung hierarchy in three stages. Cited in: Ch 24
  300. Leo Burnett and the Inherent Drama doctrine, 1935-1971 (and the Burnett character canon, 1935-present). Primary attribution and quoted doctrine: Leo Burnett, "Finding the Inherent Drama," address to the American Association of Advertising Agencies Central Region Annual Meeting, October 13, 1955, reprinted in Medium / Leo's Words of Wisdom , "Finally Somebody Has to Get Out an Ad" (medium.com/leos-words-of-wisdom/finally-somebody-has-to-get-out-an-ad-f558baf9141). Roy H. Williams, "Inherent Drama as Advertising Super-Power," Wizard of Ads (wizardofads.org/inherent-drama-as-advertising-super-power/) on the contrast with the Hopkins reason-why and Bernbach creative-truth traditions. Biography: Wikipedia , "Leo Burnett" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_Burnett) and "Leo Burnett Company" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leo_Burnett_Company). Character canon: Wikipedia , "Tony the Tiger" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tony_the_Tiger) on the 1951-1952 four-candidate retail test (Tony, Katy the Kangaroo, Newt the Gnu, Elmo the Elephant) and the seventy-plus-year continuous package presence; "Jolly Green Giant" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jolly_Green_Giant) on the 1935 redraw and 1954 television debut; "Pillsbury Doughboy" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pillsbury_Doughboy) on Rudy Perz's 1965 invention; "Charlie the Tuna" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charlie_the_Tuna) and AdWeek, "How Charlie the Tuna Became One of the Best-Known Brand Mascots in American History" (adweek.com/brand-marketing/how-charlie-the-tuna-became-one-of-the-best-known-brand-mascots-in-american-history/); Globe and Mail, "The Maytag Man Through the Years" (theglobeandmail.com/report-on-business/industry-news/marketing/the-maytag-man-through-the-years/article16247650/) on the 1967 Jesse White launch of Ol' Lonely. Chicago School pedagogy: Copernican Shift, "100 Days of Leo Burnett: Day 53" (copernicanshift.com/100-days-of-leo-burnett-the-midwestern-master-of-mascots-day-53/). Marlboro Man case context: Wikipedia , "Marlboro Man" (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marlboro_Man). Linkage to the modern audit framework: Romaniuk's Fame and Uniqueness instrument applied retrospectively to the Burnett canon, the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute (marketingscience.info/building-distinctive-brand-assets/), and the Distinctive Brand Assets framework page (distinctivebat.com/distinctive-brand-assets/). The McDonald's 2022-2026 reactivation arc covered at ref-46 inherits the same doctrine through Leo Burnett London's Raise Your Arches 2023 Cannes Film Lions Silver, which is one of three Burnett-attributed entries in the McDonald's reactivation chain. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/leo-burnett-inherent-drama-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  301. Leonard A. Schlesinger and James L. Heskett, “Breaking the Cycle of Failure in Services,” Sloan Management Review 32, no. 3 (Spring 1991): 17-28, naming the cycle of failure (low wages, minimal selection and training, high turnover, narrow jobs, weak service, low margins) and its mirror, the cycle of capability (broader jobs, training and discretion, retention, productivity gains, customer loyalty, margin), as the operational mechanism underneath the 1994 service-profit chain (see ref-15). Cited in: Ch 9
  302. Leonard L. Berry, “Cultivating Service Brand Equity,” Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science 28, no. 1 (Winter 2000): 128-137, on the service-branding model in which presented brand and external communications drive awareness while direct customer experience drives brand meaning. Berry, Carbone, and Haeckel, “Managing the Total Customer Experience,” MIT Sloan Management Review 43, no. 3 (Spring 2002): 85-89, on functional, mechanic, and humanic clues. Berry, Discovering the Soul of Service (Free Press, 1999), the fourteen-firm longitudinal study of nine drivers of sustainable service success. Cited in: Ch 9
  303. Leung, Gu, Li, Zhang, Palmatier, “Influencer Marketing Effectiveness: A Meta-Analytic Review,” JAMS 2024 (1,531 effect sizes from 251 papers). Cited in: Ch 19
  304. Levy, Grewal, Peterson, and Connolly, “The Concept of the ‘Big Middle,’” Journal of Retailing , 81(2), 83-88, 2005. Cited in: Ch 12
  305. Lewin, Principles of Topological Psychology (McGraw-Hill, 1936); Lewin, Field Theory in Social Science (Harper, 1951, posthumous). Behavior as a function of person and environment, B=f(P,E); the foundation under the Howard-Sheth exogenous-variables layer, the EKBM environmental-influences block, the Hoyer-MacInnis-Pieters context-effects literature, and the situational-influence research program in consumer behavior originating in Belk, "Situational Variables and Consumer Behavior," JCR , 2(3), 1975, 157-164. Cited in: Ch 4
  306. Liang et al., “GPT detectors are biased against non-native English writers,” Patterns , vol. 4, no. 7, 2023; Walters, “The Effectiveness of Software Designed to Detect AI-Generated Writing,” Communications in Information Literacy , vol. 17, no. 2, 2023; OpenAI, “New AI classifier for indicating AI-written text,” January 2023 (since deprecated due to low accuracy). Cited in: Ch 17
  307. Lieberman, M. D., Eisenberger, N. I., Crockett, M. J., Tom, S. M., Pfeifer, J. H., & Way, B. M. (2007). Putting feelings into words: Affect labeling disrupts amygdala activity in response to affective stimuli. Psychological Science , 18(5), 421-428. Cited in: Ch 23
  308. Lifecycle-event monitoring framework, Computers 15, no. 5 (MDPI, 2025): article 276, average silhouette 0.729 / drift score 0.005 on test data, four-layer reference architecture (event ingestion, profile update, segmentation recompute with Hungarian-algorithm centroid matching, notification); self-adaptive scheduled-retrain trigger from A Dynamic Integrated Framework for Highly Accurate Real-Time Customer Classification (Springer, 2025), three trigger conditions (silhouette drop >0.05, Population Stability Index above 0.25, lifecycle event persisting three periods). MONIC and E-Stream are the methodological progenitors for the birth/death/merge/split taxonomy. Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/dynamic-real-time-segmentation-drift-2024-2026-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  309. Lilien, Kotler, Moorthy, Marketing Models , Prentice Hall, 1992; Bass, “A New Product Growth Model for Consumer Durables,” Management Science 15(5), 1969, 215-227; Little, “BRANDAID: A Marketing-Mix Model,” Operations Research 23(4), 1975, 628-655; Silk and Urban, “Pre-Test-Market Evaluation of New Packaged Goods,” JMR 15(2), 1978, 171-191; Lilien, Rangaswamy, De Bruyn, Principles of Marketing Engineering and Analytics , 3rd ed., 2017; seven-family marketing-models taxonomy implemented by modern open-source MMM stacks in Bayesian dress. Cited in: Ch 24
  310. Lim, W. M., et al. (2025). Industry insights: the role of sales operations (SalesOps) in firm strategy and performance. JPSSM , DOI 10.1080/08853134.2025.2525850 (15 semi-structured practitioner interviews; SalesOps-to-RevOps substantive scope expansion to cross-functional GTM discipline). Salesloft / Wakefield Research (2025), State of RevOps and AI (n=400 RevOps and executive decision-makers across manufacturing, telecommunications, financial services, and technology; 97% measurable ROI from AI implementation, top-quartile 30%+ forecast-accuracy improvement). Alexander Group (2024), RevOps Survey (66% headcount increase, 61% multiple-AI-pilot completion, median ratio ~1 RevOps per 10 sales reps and ~1 per 20 marketing FTEs). Forrester, The Rise of Revenue Operations (Strategic and Transformational RevOps teams deliver 2x internal productivity and higher win rates relative to Reactive and Functional teams). Grand View Research (2024), Revenue Operations (RevOps) Market Report ($4.39B 2024 to $16.98B 2033, 16.6% CAGR). Revenue Operations Alliance (2024), State of Revenue Operations (median team six headcount, $2.5M annual budget, twelve KPIs, median maturity Stage 2 Functional). Cited in: Ch 23
  311. Lin, Keinan, Chang, and Lehmann, "The Robin Hood Effect in Transgressions Against Firms," JCR , 52(6), 2026, 1102-1125. doi:10.1093/jcr/ucaf053. Robin Hood justification mediates the ideology-transgression link; attenuated by customer-friendly framing. Cited in: Ch 4
  312. Lin, Kim, Uduehi, and Keinan, "Culture for Sale: Unpacking Consumer Perceptions of Cultural Appropriation," JCR , 51(3), 2024, 571-594, doi:10.1093/jcr/ucad076. Cited in: Ch 4
  313. LinkedIn B2B Institute and Ehrenberg-Bass Institute, The 95-5 Rule: How Investing in Future Demand Generates Sustainable Growth , 2021; Romaniuk and Sharp, How Brands Grow: Part 2 , rev. ed. (Oxford University Press, 2021), Chapter 2 on category-entry-points. Cited in: Ch 17
  314. LLM-Coached Negotiation in B2B Procurement (2024). JMR (n=1,200 dyads). Replicated in “Generative AI and the Sales Productivity Frontier” (2025), Journal of Marketing (n=420 reps). Cited in: Ch 23
  315. Longoni, Bonezzi, and Morewedge, "Resistance to Medical AI," JCR , 46(4), Dec 2019, 629-650. Driven by uniqueness neglect. Cited in: Ch 4
  316. Lyman, Hepner, Argyle, Busby, Gubler, and Wingate, “Balancing Large Language Model Alignment and Algorithmic Fidelity in Social Science Research,” Sociological Methods and Research , vol. 54, no. 3, 2025, pp. 1110–1155, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00491241251342008 ; preprint at arXiv: 2209.06899 . Mechanism behind the Bail 2025 variance-compression diagnostic (ref-55): the reinforcement-learning-from-human-feedback procedure that turns a base model into a chat assistant trained for helpful-honest-harmless behavior compresses the persona-diversity capacity that algorithmic fidelity (Argyle 2023, ref-21) depends on. The paper documents the gap across base, instruction-tuned, and fully aligned variants on American National Election Studies items, with the largest gap on intersectional cells whose actual majority opinion diverges from the assistant default. Proposes a recovery technique (explicit-simulation prompt framing plus in-context exemplars plus multi-sample aggregation) consistent with the persona-vectors steering mechanism at ref-44; partial recovery only, not full closure. Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/frontier/ai-lyman-argyle-alignment-vs-fidelity-2025-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  317. Lynch et al., "Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats," Anthropic, June 20, 2025; arXiv:2510.05179. Cited in: Ch 26
  318. Lynch, Larson, Mindermann, Perez, Hubinger et al., “Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats,” Anthropic / arXiv 2510.05179 (Jun 2025); 16-model stress test, Claude Opus 4 and Gemini 2.5 Flash 96 percent blackmail, GPT-4.1 / Grok 3 / DeepSeek-R1 79-80 percent; companion arXiv 2510.05192 maps insider-risk mitigations. Cited in: Ch 24
  319. Lynnette Hui Xian Ng and Kathleen M. Carley, “Are LLM-Powered Social Media Bots Realistic?” arXiv:2508.00998, Aug 1, 2025 (rev Aug 22; SBP-BRiMS 2025); 45,745 GPT-4.1-mini agents, 77,037 generated tweets benchmarked against ~200M wild X users and 5B COVID-19 tweets; LLM-bot networks fail to reproduce wild-agent linguistic, sentiment, and network-topology signatures (first-person 1.38 vs 0.71-0.73; abusive ~0 vs 0.12-0.13; sentiment 0.001-0.003 vs 2.88-3.10; network-degree ~1e-6 vs nonzero); prompt-engineering closes cue-alignment gaps by 0.26 ± 0.27/iter. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ng-carley-llm-bot-realism-2025-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  320. MacDiarmid, Hubinger, Marks, Treutlein, Bowen, Bricken, Carter, Catanzaro, Hoover, Kale, Lopez, Lyzhov, Maharaj, McKinney, Olah, Olsson, Pacchiardi, Pavlov, Petrini, Roger, Rotaru, Stack, Strunk, Sundararajan, Tamkin, Tong, Walden et al., "Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL," Anthropic, Nov 23, 2025; arXiv:2511.18397. Reward-hacking signal generalizes to alignment faking, malicious-actor cooperation, and Claude Code sabotage on the paper's own codebase; standard RLHF safety training leaves agentic-task misalignment intact. Three mitigations: prevent the hack, diversify RLHF safety data, apply inoculation prompting (frame reward hacking as acceptable during training to break misaligned generalization). Companion blog: anthropic.com/research/emergent-misalignment-reward-hacking. Open replication: github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/reward-hacking-misalignment (OLMo 7B/32B, GPT-OSS 20B/120B). Cited in: Ch 26
  321. Malcolm P. McNair, “Significant Trends and Developments in the Postwar Period,” in A. B. Smith (ed.), Competitive Distribution in a Free, High-Level Economy (University of Pittsburgh Press, 1958); origin of the Wheel of Retailing hypothesis. Stanley C. Hollander, “The Wheel of Retailing,” Journal of Marketing 25, no. 1 (1960): 37-42. Stephen Brown, “The Wheel of Retailing: Past and Future,” Journal of Retailing (1990). Michael Levy, Dhruv Grewal, Robert A. Peterson, and Bob Connolly, “The Concept of the ‘Big Middle,’” Journal of Retailing 81, no. 2 (2005): 83-88. William R. Davidson, Albert D. Bates, and Stephen J. Bass, “The Retail Life Cycle,” Harvard Business Review 54, no. 6 (November-December 1976): 89-96. Cited in: Ch 11
  322. Mansoor, Hauser, and colleagues, “Semantic Similarity Rating: An Embedding-Based Method for Mapping Free-Text LLM Responses to Likert Ratings,” October 2025, arXiv: 2510.08338 . Method: prompt the model to respond in free text, embed both the response and a set of anchor sentences (one per scale point) into a shared sentence-embedding space using a standard embedding model, and assign the rating of the nearest anchor. Variance enters through the free-text generation step rather than the integer-pick step that produces Bail-style compression. Benchmark: nine published marketing-research studies covering 57 Likert items and approximately 19,000 human ratings; the synthetic distribution recovers approximately 90 percent of the human test-retest reliability floor and matches Cronbach’s alpha internal-consistency benchmarks within a few percentage points across the corpus. Methodological complement to the Bail 2025 distributional-collapse diagnostic (ref-55) and the Brand-Israeli-Ngwe fine-tuning direction (ref-60); empirical scaffold for the conjoint-specific AAE recipe at ref-68. Cited in: Ch 5
  323. Marieke de Mooij, Consumer Behavior and Culture , 1st-3rd eds. (Sage, 2003-2019); de Mooij and Hofstede, IJA 29, no. 1 (2010): 85-110; JICM 23, no. 3-4 (2011): 181-192. Wealth-driven divergence (Eurobarometer + EMS, 15 EU countries, three decades) and the four most-predictive Hofstede dimensions for consumer behavior. Synthesis at wiki/external/canonical/de-mooij-consumer-behavior-culture-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  324. Mark Dean, Dilip Ravindran, and Jörg Stoye, "A Better Test of Choice Overload," Becker Friedman Institute working paper, August 2024; arXiv:2212.03931 v3. Stronger Random-Utility-Model characterization-theorem test recovers robust choice overload that average-probability tests miss; restores the Iyengar/Lepper 2000 finding. Cited in: Ch 4
  325. Mark S. Granovetter, “The Strength of Weak Ties,” AJS 78, no. 6 (May 1973): 1360–1380 (Boston study, 282 PTM workers; 56% of hires via personal contacts); Rajkumar, Saint-Jacques, Bojinov, Brynjolfsson, Aral, “A Causal Test of the Strength of Weak Ties,” Science 377, no. 6612 (Sep 2022): 1304–1310 (LinkedIn A/B; 20M users, 2B connections, 600K jobs 2015-2019); Barabási and Albert, “Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks,” Science 286, no. 5439 (Oct 1999): 509–512 (web-graph γ ≈ 2.1); Twitter follower exponents 1.84-2.20 and TikTok view Gini ≈ 0.93 (Cameron 2022). Gladwell, The Tipping Point (Little, Brown, 2000); Law-of-the-Few framing empirically falsified per ref-24. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/granovetter-weak-ties-ch19.md , gladwell-tipping-point-ch19.md , barabasi-scale-free-networks-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  326. Mark W. Schaefer, The Content Code (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2015) (alpha audience definition, BADASS configuration); Schaefer, Marketing Rebellion: The Most Human Company Wins (Schaefer Marketing Solutions, 2019) (three-rebellions framework, five-human-truths content filter); David C. Court, Dave Elzinga, Susan Mulder, Ole Jorgen Vetvik, “The Consumer Decision Journey,” McKinsey Quarterly , June 2009 (two-thirds-of-touchpoints finding from 20,000-buyer initial sample US/Germany/Japan, replicated across ~200,000 journeys across 90 industries). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schaefer-marketing-rebellion-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  327. Marn and Rosiello, “Managing Price, Gaining Profit,” HBR 70, 5 (Sep-Oct 1992): 84-94 (pocket price waterfall, Castle Battery case); Marn, Roegner, Zawada, “The Power of Pricing,” McKinsey Quarterly (Feb 2003) (1% price improvement = 8% operating profit lift across S&P 1500); Marn, Roegner, Zawada, The Price Advantage (Wiley, 2004). Synthesis at canonical/marn-rosiello-mckinsey-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  328. Marshall L. Fisher, “What Is the Right Supply Chain for Your Product?” Harvard Business Review , March-April 1997, 75(2): 105-116. Cited in: Ch 11
  329. Martin Christopher, Logistics & Supply Chain Management , 6th ed. (Pearson, 2023), paperback ISBN 978-1-292-41618-2; 5th ed. (Pearson, 2016), ISBN 978-1-292-08379-7.
