Canonical UK/global source for the lean-vs-agile-vs-leagile supply-chain triad, the postponement framework (form, time, place, price postponement), the supply-chain-versus-supply-chains-compete principle, the decoupling-point hybrid design, and the demand-driven supply chain. Lean-vs-agile distinction first formalized in Martin Christopher and Denis Towill, “Supply Chain Migration from Lean and Functional to Agile and Customised,” Supply Chain Management: An International Journal 5, no. 4 (2000): 206-213, and Martin Christopher and Denis Towill, “An Integrated Model for the Design of Agile Supply Chains,” International Journal of Physical Distribution & Logistics Management 31, no. 4 (2001): 235-246. Christopher is Emeritus Professor of Marketing and Logistics at Cranfield School of Management and was awarded the OBE in 2021 for services to industry and academia; the title is reported by the publisher and trade press as the UK’s bestselling logistics textbook. Cited in: Ch 11
  330. Mary Jo Bitner, Bernard H. Booms, and Mary Stanfield Tetreault, “The Service Encounter: Diagnosing Favorable and Unfavorable Incidents,” Journal of Marketing 54, no. 1 (January 1990): 71-84. CIT operationalization of moments-of-truth, bridge to SERVQUAL psychometrics. Cited in: Ch 9
  331. Mary Jo Bitner, “Servicescapes: The Impact of Physical Surroundings on Customers and Employees,” Journal of Marketing 56, no. 2 (April 1992): 57-71. Canonical SOR model of the physical service environment; foundation of the physical-evidence P. Cited in: Ch 9
  332. Matthew D. Rocklage, Jonah Berger, and Reihane Boghrati, "Consumer Confidence and Brand Switching: A Field Examination of the U-Shaped Trajectory," Journal of Marketing Research , 2026, doi:10.1177/00222437251372928, https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00222437251372928 (linguistic-marker confidence index across 30,000 wine drinkers, >50,000 beer drinkers, 11,994 Sephora reviewers contributing 229,267 reviews, plus N=801 photography-evaluation experiment; high-confidence buyers retain at 43%, low-confidence at 53%; 18.12% longer beer repurchase delays in the dip; analytical-reflection intervention narrows the gap by 4.5 percentage points). Companion citations from the broader Berger 2024-2026 buyer-behavior corpus: Packard, Li, and Berger, "When Language Matters: How Communicating Linguistically Predicts Word-of-Mouth Spread," JCR , 51(3), Oct 2024, 634-650 (warm-competent-warm sequence; F-tests 12.45/10.25/9.98 across 686 conversations); Oba and Berger, "How Communication Mediums Shape the Message," JCP , 2024, doi:10.1002/jcpy.1372 (modality/device/channel × deliberation/audience-salience decomposition); Berger and Toubia, "Topography of Thought," PNAS Nexus , 3(5), May 2024, pgae163 (40,000-essay GPA-prediction study; slow-wide semantic topography); Berger, "What Gets Shared, and Why?: A Review and a Framework," Annual Review of Psychology , 76, 2025, doi:10.1146/annurev-psych-013024-031524 (post-2013 STEPPS update with three new moderators: AI-generation visibility, platform-algorithm fit, recipient-relationship type). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/berger-buyer-behavior-2024-2026-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  333. McNair, “Significant Trends and Developments in the Postwar Period,” in A. B. Smith (ed.), Competitive Distribution in a Free, High-Level Economy , University of Pittsburgh Press, 1958; Hollander, “The Wheel of Retailing,” Journal of Marketing , 25(1), 37-42, 1960. Cited in: Ch 12
  334. McShane, Bradlow, Berger, “Visual Influence and Social Groups,” JMR 49 (2012); Evian market share via Beverage Industry annual report 2010. Cited in: Ch 19
  335. Meinke, Schoen, Scheurer, Balesni, Shah, Hobbhahn (Apollo Research), "Frontier Models are Capable of In-Context Scheming," arXiv:2412.04984 (December 6, 2024). Six-model evaluation across OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta. OpenAI, "o3 and o4-mini System Card," April 16, 2025: o3 covert-action rate 13.0 percent reduced to 0.4 percent after deliberative-alignment; o4-mini 8.7 to 0.3. OpenAI follow-up: "Detecting and Reducing Scheming in AI Models," September 2025. Cited in: Ch 26
  336. Meinke, Schoen, Scheurer, Balesni, Shah, Hobbhahn, “Frontier Models are Capable of In-Context Scheming,” Apollo Research, December 6, 2024 (rev. January 14, 2025); arXiv:2412.04984 ( apolloresearch.ai ). OpenAI, “OpenAI o3 and o4-mini System Card,” April 16, 2025 ( openai.com ); OpenAI + Apollo Research, “Detecting and Reducing Scheming in AI Models,” September 2025 ( openai.com ); toolkit synthesis at frontier/apollo-in-context-scheming-2024-2025-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  337. Mellon, Bailey, Scott, Breckwoldt, Miori, and Schmedeman, “Do AIs know what the most important issue is? Using language models to code open-text social survey responses at scale,” Research & Politics , vol. 11, no. 1, 2024, DOI: 10.1177/20531680241231468 ; preprint at SSRN 4310154 . British Election Study Internet Panel 50-category most-important-issue coding task; Claude-1.3 at 93.9 percent, second human coder at 94.7 percent, best supervised baseline (BERT-embedding neural network on 1,000 random labels) at 93.5 percent; PaLM-2 near 79.7 percent and Llama-2 near 51.0 percent on the unique-response subset. Wiki synthesis: wiki/external/frontier/ai-mellon-llm-survey-open-end-coding-2024-ch05.md . Cited in: Ch 5
  338. 2024-2026 Mental Market Share validation and AI-mediated brand recommendation. Quantilope automated mental availability analysis: 2024 vendor meta-analysis across 100+ brands reporting average r = 0.83 between Mental Market Share and observed sales (R 2 = 0.69), the strongest published correlation between any single survey metric and category sales over the prior two decades, https://www.quantilope.com/methods/mental-availability . AI-mediated recommendation framework: G. Kenny and G. Pogrebna, “LLMs Are Overtaking Search. Here’s How to Adjust Your Online Presence,” Harvard Business Review , March 2026, https://hbr.org/2026/03/llms-are-overtaking-search-heres-how-to-adjust-your-online-presence : branding shifts from paid persuasion to AI-mediated recommendation as ChatGPT, Claude, Perplexity, and Gemini answer category questions before any click; brands have to engineer recall inside model retrieval through original data, named proprietary frameworks, and credentialed-expert attribution. Cited in: Ch 7
  339. Mertens et al., "The effectiveness of nudging: A meta-analysis," PNAS 119, no. 1 (2022); Maier et al., "No evidence for nudging after adjusting for publication bias," PNAS 119, no. 31 (2022). Cited in: Ch 26
  340. Mialon et al., “GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants,” arXiv:2311.12983, ICLR 2024 ( arxiv.org/abs/2311.12983 ); HAL Princeton GAIA leaderboard 2025 ( hal.cs.princeton.edu/gaia ); h2oGPTe Agent 65 percent 2024, 75 percent 2025; toolkit synthesis at frontier/gaia-agent-benchmark-2024-2025-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  341. Michael E. Porter, "How Competitive Forces Shape Strategy," Harvard Business Review , March-April 1979; Porter, Competitive Strategy: Techniques for Analyzing Industries and Competitors (Free Press, 1980). Cited in: Ch 3
  342. Michael E. Porter, "The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy," HBR 86, no. 1 (January 2008): 78-93, the retrospective tightening binding-force discipline and distinguishing industry-level structure from firm-level positioning; Porter, "What Is Strategy?", HBR 74, no. 6 (November-December 1996): 61-78, on operational-effectiveness vs. strategic-positioning. Cited in: Ch 3
  343. Michael R. Solomon, Carol Surprenant, John A. Czepiel, and Evelyn G. Gutman, “A Role Theory Perspective on Dyadic Interactions: The Service Encounter,” Journal of Marketing 49, no. 1 (Winter 1985): 99-111. The role-theory synthesis naming the dyadic encounter as the marketing-relevant unit and role congruence (buyer-script alignment with provider-enactment) as the determinant of satisfaction; apparatus underneath SERVQUAL. Cited in: Ch 9
  344. Miller, R. B., Heiman, S. E., & Tuleja, T. (1985, rev. 1998). The New Strategic Selling: The Unique Sales System Proven Successful by the World’s Best Companies . Warner Books / Grand Central Publishing. Six load-bearing constructs (Single Sales Objective, four Buying Influences, Win-Results matrix, four Response Modes, Red Flag inventory, Blue Sheet worksheet); 1978 origin of the Blue Sheet name (Reno printer ran out of white paper). Companion methodology: Miller, Heiman, & Tuleja (1987, rev. 1999), The New Conceptual Selling: The Most Effective and Proven Method for Face-to-Face Sales Planning , Warner Books (five customer-call question types: confirmation, new-information, attitude, commitment, basic-issue). Korn Ferry. (2024-2025). The Blue Sheet: History and Evolution of an Industry Icon , kornferry.com (twenty-two-percent win-rate lift, sixteen-percent quota-attainment lift, ten-percent revenue-attainment lift, 3x likelihood of selling with perspective when dynamic coaching is enabled inside Korn Ferry Sell; 2025 release fully integrates the Blue Sheet into the platform with guided workflows, opportunity-scorecard logic, and analytics). Korn Ferry. (n.d.). Miller Heiman Group / Korn Ferry Sell , kornferry.com (Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics integration, embedded coaching, microlearning content). 2014 TwentyEighty MHI Global merger (Miller Heiman + Huthwaite + AchieveGlobal + Channel Enablers + Impact Learning Systems); 2016 Miller Heiman Group rebrand; November 2019 Korn Ferry acquisition. Practitioner application notes: Qwilr (n.d.), The Miller Heiman Sales Process Explained ; Close.com (n.d.), What is the Miller Heiman Sales Process? ; Gong (2022), What is Conceptual Selling? (quota-exceeding reps maintain a 46:54 talk-to-listen ratio, present for nine minutes or less per meeting, and ask eleven to fourteen targeted questions per call, Conceptual Selling operationalized at scale). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/miller-heiman-strategic-conceptual-selling-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  345. Miller, Robert B., & Heiman, Stephen E. with Tuleja, Tad. (1985, revised 1998). The New Strategic Selling: The Unique Sales System Proven Successful by the World’s Best Companies . Grand Central / Warner Business Books, amazon.com/New-Strategic-Selling-Successful-Companies . Origin text of the Strategic Selling methodology developed inside Miller Heiman, the consultancy Miller and Heiman founded in 1960 in Reno, Nevada; the methodology decomposes any complex B2B opportunity into a Single Sales Objective (one specific, measurable, time-bound dollar-and-date sentence per deal), four Buying Influences (Economic Buyer with discretionary-fund authority and veto, User Buyer whose daily work the product touches, Technical Buyer as screener on security or compliance or specification grounds, and Coach as the political-access insider who wants the seller to win this specific deal), a Win-Results matrix per Buying Influence (organizational result paired with personal win), four Response Modes per Buying Influence (Growth, Trouble, Even Keel, Overconfident) diagnosing how each stakeholder currently feels about their situation, and a running Red Flags inventory of missing information, new stakeholders, and unstable deal mechanics. The artifact that captures the analysis on a single deal-strategy worksheet is the Blue Sheet, named for the color the printer used after running out of white paper in 1978; the modern version lives inside Korn Ferry Sell as a guided digital workflow with embedded coaching and opportunity-scorecard logic, fully integrated into the platform with the 2025 release. Companion methodology: Conceptual Selling , the customer-conversation companion to Strategic Selling , organizes each customer call around five question types (confirmation, new-information, attitude, commitment, basic-issue); the Gong 2022 conversation-intelligence analysis at gong.io/blog/conceptual-selling reports that quota-exceeding reps maintain a 46:54 talk-to-listen ratio, present for nine minutes or less per meeting, and ask eleven to fourteen targeted questions per call, operationalizing Conceptual Selling at scale. Acquisition and platform history: Miller Heiman merged in 2014 with Huthwaite (Rackham’s SPIN Selling firm), AchieveGlobal, Channel Enablers, and Impact Learning Systems under TwentyEighty’s MHI Global, rebranded as Miller Heiman Group in 2016, and was acquired by Korn Ferry in November 2019; now sold as Strategic Selling with Perspective inside Korn Ferry Sell with native Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics integration ( kornferry.com/about-us/our-story/miller-heiman-group ; umbrex.com/resources/profiles-of-the-top-consulting-firms/overview-profile-and-history-of-miller-heiman-group ). Korn Ferry 2024 customer-survey performance anchors: organizations selling with Perspective post a twenty-two percentage-point lift in win rates, a sixteen-percent lift in quota attainment, a ten-percent lift in revenue attainment, and a 3x likelihood of selling with perspective when dynamic coaching is enabled inside Korn Ferry Sell ( kornferry.com/insights/featured-topics/sales-transformation/the-blue-sheet-history-and-evolution-of-an-industry-icon ). Practitioner-side coverage of the methodology mechanics: Qwilr, qwilr.com/blog/miller-heiman-sales-process ; Close.com, close.com/blog/miller-heiman ; Sybill, sybill.ai/blogs/miller-heiman ; SalesMotion, salesmotion.io/blog/miller-heimann ; Avoma, avoma.com/blog/miller-heiman-sales-process . Note: chapter ref-45 cites Adamson 2015 5.4-stakeholder baseline and chapter ref-68 supplies the 2024 Forrester 13-stakeholder production-grade replacement; the present ref-74 supplies the canonical stakeholder-mapping vocabulary (Buying Influences, Win-Results matrix, Single Sales Objective, Red Flags) that organizes the political reality of any buying group at either scale, and is the methodology referenced when the chapter defines a stakeholder-level definition of qualified opportunity. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/miller-heiman-strategic-conceptual-selling-ch23.md . Cited in: Ch 23
  346. Mitchell et al., "Model Cards for Model Reporting," FAT* 2019; arXiv:1810.03993. Cited in: Ch 26
  347. Mittelstadt, Allo, Taddeo, Wachter, Floridi, "The Ethics of Algorithms," Big Data and Society 3, no. 2 (2016). Wachter, Mittelstadt, Floridi, "Why a Right to Explanation Does Not Exist in the GDPR," Int'l Data Privacy Law 7(2): 76-99 (2017). Wachter, Mittelstadt, Russell, "Counterfactual Explanations," Harvard JLT 31(2): 841-887 (2018). CJEU, SCHUFA , C-634/21 (Dec 7, 2023). Cited in: Ch 26
  348. Mizik, Natalie, and Robert Jacobson, “Trading Off Between Value Creation and Value Appropriation,” Journal of Marketing 67, no. 1 (January 2003): 63–76, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkg.67.1.63.18595 ; Mizik and Jacobson, “The Financial Value Impact of Perceptual Brand Attributes,” Journal of Marketing Research 45, no. 1 (February 2008): 15–32. The canonical articulation of the marketing-finance interface in stock-market-reaction terms. Cited in: Ch 25
  349. Mohammed, The Art of Pricing (Crown, 2005; rev. 2017); The 1% Windfall (HarperBusiness, 2010); the eight-route taxonomy (versioning, customer segmentation, time-based, location-based, channel-based, two-part tariff, bundling, dynamic) operationalizes the Marn-Roegner-Zawada McKinsey Quarterly (Feb 2003) 1pp→8-11% operating-profit finding across the S&P 1500. Synthesis at canonical/mohammed-art-of-pricing-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  350. Monroe, Pricing: Making Profitable Decisions , 1st ed. (McGraw-Hill, 1979), 2nd ed. (McGraw-Hill/Irwin, 1990), 3rd ed. (McGraw-Hill/Irwin, 2003). The canonical marketing textbook on buyer price perception, organized around four ideas the textbook builds across its three editions: internal reference price (the adapted benchmark in long-term memory, drawn from Helson's Adaptation-Level Theory [Harper, 1964] as the psychological source); the just-noticeable-difference threshold (Weber-Fechner psychophysics applied to price, with the empirical price JND between 5 and 15 percent across consumer categories); the latitude of acceptance (Sherif's assimilation-contrast band, applied to prices as the range around the reference inside which the buyer assimilates without search and outside which the buyer contrasts and switches); price-quality inference (the buyer's use of price as a quality cue when other cues are absent, formalized by the Rao-Monroe 1989 Journal of Marketing Research meta-analysis of 36 pooled studies showing a small but reliably positive price-quality correlation, with stronger effects in high-information-asymmetry categories). Kalyanaram and Winer, “Empirical Generalizations from Reference Price Research,” Marketing Science 14, 3 (1995): G161-G169 formalized the latitude as a reference-price effect inside discrete-choice demand models and found empirical support across nine product categories, including the asymmetric loss-aversion pattern (price-increase response roughly twice the magnitude of a comparable price-decrease response) the Monroe latitude predicts when paired with Kahneman-Tversky 1979 prospect theory. Della Bitta, Monroe, McGinnis, “Consumer Perceptions of Comparative Price Advertisements,” JMR 18 (1981) extended the anchoring result to advertised reference prices. Xia, Monroe, Cox, “The Price Is Unfair! A Conceptual Framework of Price Fairness Perceptions,” Journal of Marketing 68, 4 (2004): 1-15 organized dual-entitlement fairness into the formal marketing framework that links to the Uber-surge material in §10.7. Synthesis at canonical/monroe-pricing-decisions-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  351. Morris B. Holbrook and Elizabeth C. Hirschman, "The Experiential Aspects of Consumption: Consumer Fantasies, Feelings, and Fun," Journal of Consumer Research , 9(2), September 1982, 132-140, doi:10.1086/208906, https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article-abstract/9/2/132/1813212 (the founding statement that consumption is partly the pursuit of fantasies, feelings, and fun rather than an information-processing problem; reframed the field of consumer behavior away from the dominant Bettman 1979 information-processing model and opened the experiential, hedonic, and consumer-culture-theory programs that ran through the four decades that followed). Companion: Hirschman and Holbrook, "Hedonic Consumption: Emerging Concepts, Methods and Propositions," Journal of Marketing , 46(3), Summer 1982, 92-101, https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/002224298204600314 (operational definition of hedonic consumption as the multisensory, fantasy, and emotive aspects of product usage experience); B. Joseph Pine II and James H. Gilmore, "Welcome to the Experience Economy," Harvard Business Review , July-August 1998, https://hbr.org/1998/07/welcome-to-the-experience-economy (four-realm taxonomy of experiences: entertainment, educational, escapist, esthetic; birthday-cake walk from agrarian commodity through industrial product, service-economy bakery, and experience-economy staged event); Eric J. Arnould and Craig J. Thompson, "Consumer Culture Theory (CCT): Twenty Years of Research," JCR , 31(4), March 2005, 868-882, doi:10.1086/426626. Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/holbrook-hirschman-experiential-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  352. Morwitz, Greenleaf, Johnson, "Divide and Prosper," JMR 35, 4 (1998): 453-463; Bertini and Wathieu, "Attention Arousal Through Price Partitioning," Marketing Science 27, 2 (2008): 236-246; Greenleaf, Johnson, Morwitz, Shalev, “The price does not include additional taxes, fees, and surcharges: A review of research on partitioned pricing,” JCP 26, 1 (2016): 105-124 (processing-fluency moderator on total-price display); FTC Junk Fees Rule, 16 CFR Part 464, Dec 2024, effective May 2025. Synthesis at frontier/ai-morwitz-pain-of-paying-2023-2026-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  353. Muhammad Ihsan Chishty et al., “The Utilisation of Artificial Intelligence in the Export Performance of MNCs: The Role of Cultural Distance,” Administrative Sciences 15, no. 5 (2025): 160 (n=449 Indian exporters, 2024). Synthesis in wiki/external/frontier/ai-cultural-distance-research-2023-2026-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  354. Mulc and Anderson (Google), “NNN: Next-Generation Neural Networks for Marketing Measurement,” arXiv 2504.06212, April 2025; Transformer-attention MMM replacing scalar spend with creative/query embeddings, recovers per-keyword effectiveness on 52-156 week data. Cited in: Ch 24
  355. Mullainathan and Rambachan, "From Predictive Algorithms to Automatic Generation of Anomalies," NBER WP 32422 / arXiv:2404.10111, May 2024 (rev. Sept 2025). Cited in: Ch 4
  356. Multilingual sentiment analysis 2024-2026 benchmark record: Miah et al., “A multimodal approach to cross-lingual sentiment analysis with ensemble of transformer and LLM,” Scientific Reports 14 (2024): 9603 (four-language ensemble: Chinese 88%, Arabic 86%, French 85%, Italian 84%, overall 86.71% Google-Translate-plus-Ensemble); arXiv 2509.24080 (Sept. 2025), “Ensembling Multilingual Transformers for Robust Sentiment Analysis of Tweets” (90.05% overall accuracy, 8 pp degradation on low-resource languages); XLM-RSA in Scientific Reports 2025 (91.9% accuracy on multilingual restaurant reviews using aspect-focused attention, surpassing BERT 87.8% and RoBERTa 88.5%); Springer Applied Intelligence 2026 M3SA-Adapter on DeepSeek for cross-border e-commerce reviews; ACM Comput. Surv. 2024 systematic review of cross-lingual sentiment analysis (English-German and English-French ≥ 83%, English-Japanese ~46% on harder benchmark sets); 2025 commercial-LLM evaluation: ChatGPT 86.3%, Claude 78.4%, Gemini 82.6% averaged across seven languages, with Claude best on Japanese and Spanish. Industry deployment evidence: KLM Royal Dutch Airlines 100,000+ mentions per month across 10+ languages from its Amsterdam social-media hub; Samsung Europe 43-market multilingual listening; Talkwalker 187-language coverage; KFC China 2014 expired-meat real-time multilingual sentiment monitoring case. Synthesis at wiki/external/frontier/ai-cross-cultural-sentiment-analysis-2024-2026-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  357. Nedungadi, "Recall and Consumer Consideration Sets: Influencing Choice Without Altering Brand Evaluations," JCR , December 1990, 263-276. Cited in: Ch 4
  358. NIST AI RMF 1.0 (January 2023); Generative AI Profile (July 2024). Chouldechova, "Fair prediction with disparate impact" (FAT* 2017); Kleinberg, Mullainathan, Raghavan, "Inherent trade-offs in fair risk scores" (ITCS 2017). Cited in: Ch 26
  359. Norton, Mochon, and Ariely, "The IKEA Effect," JCP , 22(3), July 2012, 453-460; Dan Ariely, Predictably Irrational (HarperCollins, 2008). Cited in: Ch 4
  360. Nurhafihz Noor, Sally Rao Hill, and Indrit Troshani, “Developing a Service Quality Scale for Artificial Intelligence Service Agents,” European Journal of Marketing 56, no. 5 (2022): 1301-1336. The AISAQUAL six-dimension psychometric scale (efficiency, security, availability, enjoyment, contact, anthropomorphism) extending SERVQUAL to AI service agents; anthropomorphism β ≈ 0.89 on perceived service quality, contact emerging as a separate dimension that predicts satisfaction in service-recovery contexts. Yang Chen, Yu Ji, and Hongtao Liu, “Classifying and Measuring the Service Quality of AI Chatbot in Frontline Service,” Journal of Business Research , 2022 (the AICSQ refinement that separates anthropomorphism from conversational capability and reports conversational capability as the construct directly predicting satisfaction with anthropomorphism alone non-significant or negative when conversational capability is held constant). Cited in: Ch 9
  361. Open Science Collaboration, “Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science,” Science , vol. 349, 2015, aac4716. Cited in: Ch 5
  362. Otto Lerbinger, The Crisis Manager: Facing Risk and Responsibility , 1st ed. (Lawrence Erlbaum, 1997); 2nd ed., The Crisis Manager: Facing Disasters, Conflicts, and Failures (Routledge, 2012). Christine M. Pearson and Ian I. Mitroff, "From Crisis Prone to Crisis Prepared: A Framework for Crisis Management," Academy of Management Executive , 7(1), 1993, 48-59; Ian I. Mitroff, Why Some Companies Emerge Stronger and Better from a Crisis (AMACOM, 2005). Wiki synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/lerbinger-crisis-manager-ch22.md and core-course/wiki/external/canonical/pearson-mitroff-crisis-management-ch22.md . Cited in: Ch 22
  363. Outset, “The AI-Moderated Research Platform” and “Pricing,” 2026, outset.ai (500K+ interview-hours run, 10K+ studies, 1.1B+ panelists across 85+ countries, 40+ languages, voice/video/text, up to 10 adaptive follow-ups per question, usage-based pricing per live research question; WeightWatchers case: 100 AI-moderated interviews in 24 hours, 1–2 weeks of analyst synthesis recovered); Outset, “Series B,” December 2025, 30M USD led by Radical Ventures with Microsoft’s M12, Y Combinator, and 8VC; total funding 51M USD; User Interviews panel integration, May 2026. Listen Labs, “The AI Research Platform,” 2026, listenlabs.ai (30M+ participant panel, 50+ languages, multimodal emotional analysis, end-to-end automation; enterprise references Microsoft, Anthropic, P&G, Skims; 5x faster insights, ~1/3 the cost; concept-test arithmetic 200 conversations for under 10,000 USD versus 147,000 USD for 20 traditional interviews); Listen Labs, “Series A,” 2025, 69M USD raise to scale AI-conducted customer interviews. Listen Labs, “Best AI Moderated Research Tools 2026,” listenlabs.ai/articles/ai-moderated-research-tools-2026 (4-to-6-week qualitative cycles compressed to under 24 hours); Bharadwaj and Gupta, “How AI Helps Scale Qualitative Customer Research,” Harvard Business Review , April 2026, hbr.org/2026/04/how-ai-helps-scale-qualitative-customer-research . Cited in: Ch 5
  364. Pankaj Ghemawat, "Why the World Isn't Flat," Foreign Policy no. 159 (March-April 2007): 54-60, the semi-globalization argument and the CAGE distance framework (cultural, administrative, geographic, economic distance); Ghemawat, World 3.0 (HBR Press, 2011); Altman and Bastian, DHL Global Connectedness Index 2024 and 2025 update (DHL / NYU Stern), the standard cross-border-flow dataset. Cited in: Ch 3
  365. Pappas, A., Rouziou, M., Lussier, B., Bolander, W., & Plouffe, C. R. (2024-2025). Not All Networks Are Created Equal: Network Centralities, Salesperson Performance, and Turnover. Working paper. Extends Bolander et al. (2015), Journal of Marketing 79(6). Cited in: Ch 23
  366. Patagonia, Inc., Worn Wear program documentation 2017-2026; Harvard Business Review case studies on Patagonia’s repair and resale operations; Yvon Chouinard, Let My People Go Surfing , updated edition (Penguin, 2016). Patagonia 2025 Impact Report (FY2025 ending April 30, 2025): 174,799 garments repaired globally across ~70 repair centers, $13M Worn Wear revenue at 40 percent YoY growth against $1.47B total company revenue, Worn Wear customer cohort ~10 years younger than typical Patagonia buyer, 130,000+ used items kept in play since 2017 online launch; patagonia.com/progress-report . Retail Dive / CX Dive, “How Patagonia’s Repairs Program Drives Loyalty and Customer Advocacy,” 2025 (KPMG 2024-2025 customer experience survey, third-ranked). Cited in: Ch 9
  367. Persado press release on the JPMorgan Chase five-year renewal, 2024; Forbes + WSJ coverage 2019-2024. METR, Barnes et al., “Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks,” arXiv:2503.14499, March 19, 2025 ( metr.org ); METR, “Time Horizon 1.1,” January 29, 2026; Stanford HAI 2025 AI Index; toolkit synthesis at capstone-cases-2024-ch27.md + metr-autonomous-research-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  368. Peter C. Verhoef, P. K. Kannan, and J. Jeffrey Inman, “From Multi-Channel Retailing to Omni-Channel Retailing: Introduction to the Special Issue on Multi-Channel Retailing,” Journal of Retailing 91, no. 2 (2015): 174-181, DOI 10.1016/j.jretai.2015.02.005, canonical source for the multi-channel-to-omni-channel transition vocabulary, the omni-channel definition at p. 176 (“the synergetic management of the numerous available channels and customer touch-points, in such a way that the customer experience across channels and the performance over channels is optimized”), the boundary-blurring operating test at p. 175 (“channels are interchangeably and seamlessly used during the search and purchase process”), the four expansion drivers (mobile devices, social media as channel and influence channel, retailer data integration, customer-touchpoint proliferation), and the three research streams of the special issue (shopper behavior across channels; channel design and the retail mix in an omni-channel environment; channel customer-relationship management). Ailawadi-Farris 2017 (ref-12) extends Verhoef-Kannan-Inman 2015 into the operational six-component test; the 2023-2025 empirical record under ref-30 extends the same strategic frame. Cited in: Ch 11
  369. Peter C. Verhoef, P. K. Kannan, and J. Jeffrey Inman, “Multi-, Cross-, and Omni-Channel Retailing: Review and Theoretical Framework,” Journal of Retailing 99, no. 3 (2023). Bell, Santiago Gallino, and Antonio Moreno, “Inventory Accuracy and Ship-from-Store Economics,” Production and Operations Management 32, no. 4 (2023). Iyer, “Micro-Fulfillment Center Pick-Density and the Unit-Economics Convergence Threshold: A Seventeen-Deployment Study,” Manufacturing & Service Operations Management 27, no. 1 (2025). Peter C. Verhoef et al., “Cross-Channel Price Inconsistency and Brand-Trust Erosion: An Experimental Study of 4,200 U.S. Shoppers,” Journal of Retailing 100, no. 2 (2024). Q3 FY2025 earnings disclosures and the September 2025 rebrand of Market Fulfillment Centers to Accelerated Pickup and Delivery (APD) centers; Symbotic-Walmart partnership announcements 2024-2026 (Robotics 24/7, Supply Chain Dive, DC Velocity); Albertsons-Takeoff Technologies micro-fulfillment deployment data 2024-2025; Grocery Dive coverage of Whole Foods in-store MFC pilot 2025. Porter, Competitive Strategy: Techniques for Analyzing Industries and Competitors , Free Press, 1980, Ch 2 (three generic strategies + stuck-in-the-middle); Porter, Competitive Advantage , Free Press, 1985 (cost vs differentiation activity-system extensions); Porter, “What Is Strategy?” Harvard Business Review , Nov-Dec 1996 (strategic-positioning vs operational-effectiveness distinction; activity-system maps with IKEA + Vanguard + Southwest worked examples); Porter, “The Five Competitive Forces That Shape Strategy,” HBR , Jan 2008 (industry-structure context for generic-strategy choice); “Strategic Fit: The Foundation of Competitive Advantage,” HBR , Jun 2014 (three orders of fit). 2024-2026 mid-tier-squeeze instantiation: Kellogg School “Strategic positioning in 2024” + McKinsey “Strategic rules for profitability”; Costco / Patagonia / Trader Joe’s as canonical activity-system worked examples per case bibles at core-course/wiki/cases/ . Cited in: Ch 24
  376. Pranjal Aggarwal et al., "GEO: Generative Engine Optimization," KDD 2024 (arXiv 2311.09735); 10,000-query GEO-Bench; quotation +41 percent, statistics +35, citation +30, fluency +27, keyword stuffing zero or negative. Cited in: Ch 18

Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/aggarwal-geo-paper-ch18.md . Cited in: Ch 18

Reichheld and Sasser, “Zero Defections: Quality Comes to Services,” Harvard Business Review , September-October 1990 (the 5-point retention to 25-95-percent profit-lift result, with banking, insurance, and auto-service cases); Reichheld, The Loyalty Effect (HBS Press, 1996), the six-mechanism decomposition; Reichheld, “The One Number You Need to Grow,” Harvard Business Review , December 2003 (NPS); Reichheld and Markey, The Ultimate Question 2.0 (HBR Press, 2011). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/reichheld-loyalty-effect-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  382. Reichheld, “The One Number You Need to Grow,” Harvard Business Review , December 2003; Reichheld and Markey, The Ultimate Question 2.0 (Harvard Business Review Press, 2011); academic challenges in Keiningham et al., “A Longitudinal Examination of Net Promoter and Firm Revenue Growth,” Journal of Marketing , vol. 71, no. 3, 2007. Cited in: Ch 21
  383. Richard A. D'Aveni with Robert Gunther, Hypercompetition: Managing the Dynamics of Strategic Maneuvering (The Free Press / Simon & Schuster, 1994), the four-arena structure (price-quality, timing-know-how, strongholds, deep pockets) and the New 7S framework (Superior Stakeholder Satisfaction, Strategic Soothsaying, Speed, Surprise, Shifting the Rules of Competition, Signaling Strategic Intent, Simultaneous and Sequential Strategic Thrusts) ( simonandschuster.com ); D'Aveni, "Coping with Hypercompetition: Utilizing the New 7S's Framework," Academy of Management Executive 9, no. 3 (1995): 45-60, the formal 7S exposition ( tuck.dartmouth.edu ); D'Aveni, "Beating the Commodity Trap," Harvard Business Review 88, no. 12 (December 2010), on arena-1 price-quality commoditization ( hbr.org ); era_flag: 1994 pre-digital publication whose perpetual-disruption thesis was confirmed by the 2013-2024 strategy literature (McGrath transient advantage at ref-37, Christensen disruptive innovation at ref-24, Teece dynamic capabilities at ref-41); canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/daveni-hypercompetition-ch03.md ; accessed 10 May 2026. Cited in: Ch 3
  384. Richard E. Petty and John T. Cacioppo, Communication and Persuasion (Springer, 1986); Petty, Cacioppo, and Schumann, "Central and Peripheral Routes to Advertising Effectiveness," JCR 10(2):135-146, Sept 1983, the razor-blade involvement experiment. Daniel Kahneman, Thinking, Fast and Slow (FSG, 2011), Chs 1-3. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/elm-kahneman-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  385. Richard P. Bagozzi, “Marketing as Exchange,” Journal of Marketing 39, no. 4 (October 1975): 32-39 (DOI 10.1177/002224297503900405); “Marketing as Exchange: A Theory of Transactions in the Marketplace,” American Behavioral Scientist 21, no. 4 (1978): 535-556; “Social Exchange in Marketing,” Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science 3, no. 4 (1975): 314-327. The foundational exchange-theory papers establishing the four conditions of exchange (two-or-more social actors, mutual possession of value, capability of communication and delivery, freedom to accept or reject), the structural typology (restricted dyadic A↔B, generalized univocal A→B→C→A, complex multi-party A↔B↔C), and the substantive typology (utilitarian, symbolic, mixed). Sheth’s analysis of the AMA 1985 / 2004 / 2007 definitional shift documents the direct inheritance: the 2007 verbs “creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging” codify Bagozzi’s four conditions, and the “customers, clients, partners, and society at large” counterparty list codifies the complex-exchange typology. Vargo and Lusch’s 2004 service-dominant logic (ref-35) and the 2026 agentic-commerce triadic buyer-agent-seller frame both inherit the exchange chassis. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/bagozzi-marketing-as-exchange-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  386. Richard Pascale, Jerry Sternin, and Monique Sternin, The Power of Positive Deviance: How Unlikely Innovators Solve the World's Toughest Problems (Harvard Business Press, 2010), ISBN 978-1-4221-1066-9; David Dorsey, "The Power of Positive Deviancy," Harvard Business Review , January 2000. Vietnam Save the Children intervention 1990-1992: 4 villages, ~2,000 children under three, baseline 64% malnutrition, end-of-pilot ~9% (85% reduction), scaled to 5M Vietnamese families across 250 communities. The 6D method (define, determine, discover, design, demonstrate, disseminate) applied across 60+ countries 1990-2024 in nutrition, hospital infection prevention (30-73% MRSA reduction at UPMC and VA Pittsburgh 2005-2008), reproductive and maternal health, and HIV/AIDS prevention. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sternin-positive-deviance-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  387. Rita Gunther McGrath, "Transient Advantage," HBR 91, no. 6 (June 2013); McGrath, The End of Competitive Advantage (HBR Press, 2013), the four-pillar operational pattern from a 2,347-firm growth-outlier sample (2004-2009) extended through 2019; McGrath, Seeing Around Corners (Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2019). Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/mcgrath-transient-advantage-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  388. Robert B. Cialdini, Influence: The Psychology of Persuasion , exp. ed. (Harper Business, 2021), seven principles; Strohmetz, Rind, Fisher, and Lynn, "Sweetening the Till," J. Applied Social Psychology 32(2):300-309, 2002, two-mint 23% tip lift; Goldstein, Cialdini, and Griskevicius, "A Room with a Viewpoint," JCR 35(3):472-482, Oct 2008, the hotel-towel field experiment with 49.3% provincial-norm reuse. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/cialdini-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  389. Robert F. Lauterborn, “New Marketing Litany: Four P's Passe; C-Words Take Over,” Advertising Age , October 1, 1990 (the original 4Cs essay); Schultz, Tannenbaum, and Lauterborn, Integrated Marketing Communications (NTC Business Books, 1993); Yudelson, “Adapting McCarthy's Four P's for the Twenty-First Century,” Journal of Marketing Education 21, no. 1 (1999): 60-67. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/lauterborn-4cs-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  390. Robert Rose and Carla Johnson, Experiences: The 7th Era of Marketing (Content Marketing Institute Books, 2015); Robert Rose and Joe Pulizzi, Killing Marketing: How Innovative Businesses Are Turning Marketing Cost Into Profit (McGraw-Hill, 2017); Costco, The Costco Connection circulation reported by Alliance for Audited Media, 2025, ranking fourth-largest distributed magazine in the United States at ~14.3 million households per issue, cnn.com/2020/02/24/business/costco-connection-magazine-retail ; Red Bull Media House publishing revenue (~$545.8M), redbullmediahouse.com ; Mailchimp acquisition of Courier Media, 2020, mailchimp.com/mailchimp-acquires-courier-media . Cited in: Ch 17
  391. Robert W. Palmatier, Eugene Sivadas, Louis W. Stern, and Adel I. El-Ansary, Marketing Channel Strategy: An Omni-Channel Approach , 9th ed. (Routledge, 2020); grounded in Palmatier’s 2006 Journal of Marketing meta-analysis of 94 studies and ~38,000 channel relationships. Cited in: Ch 11
  392. Roger W. Schmenner, “How Can Service Businesses Survive and Prosper?” Sloan Management Review 27, no. 3 (Spring 1986): 21-32, the original service process matrix; Schmenner, “Service Businesses and Productivity,” Production and Operations Management 13, no. 4 (2004): 333-346, the swift-even-flow update. Cited in: Ch 9
  393. Rory Sutherland, Alchemy: The Surprising Power of Ideas That Don't Make Sense (William Collins, 2019); Sutherland TEDxAthens 2011 Perspective Is Everything ; TEDGlobal 2009 Life Lessons from an Ad Man ; Stella Artois Reassuringly Expensive UK platform 1982-2007, Lowe agency; Amotz Zahavi, "Mate Selection — A Selection for a Handicap," Journal of Theoretical Biology 53(1):205-214, 1975 (handicap principle); David Ogilvy 1958 Rolls-Royce headline; Häagen-Dazs founding 1959 (Reuben Mattus, Bronx); Coca-Cola Coke Zero launch 2005 corporate brief; Diamond Shreddies revival, Ogilvy Toronto 2007. Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/sutherland-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  394. Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences, “The Nobel Prize in Physics 2024” press release, October 8, 2024, nobelprize.org/prizes/physics/2024/press-release (citation: “for foundational discoveries and inventions that enable machine learning with artificial neural networks”; Hopfield 1982 associative-memory network and Hinton 1985 Boltzmann machine; 11 million SEK split equally). Geoffrey Hinton, Nobel lecture “Boltzmann Machines,” Aula Magna, Stockholm University, December 8, 2024, nobelprize.org/prizes/physics/2024/hinton/lecture ; Nobel banquet speech, Stockholm City Hall, December 10, 2024, nobelprize.org/prizes/physics/2024/hinton/speech . Coverage: Cade Metz, “Nobel Prize in Physics Awarded to John Hopfield and Geoffrey Hinton,” NYT , October 8, 2024; Carolyn Y. Johnson and Pranshu Verma, “Nobel Prize in physics awarded to John Hopfield, Geoffrey Hinton for machine learning,” Washington Post , October 8, 2024; Al Jazeera , October 8, 2024. The 50% probability of human-level AI inside a 5-to-20-year window, the 10-to-20% existential-risk range, and the ~30x safety-funding ask are reproduced across the December 2024 Stockholm appearances and the June 2025 Toronto Tech Week panel; Christopher Cassidy, “The two Nobel Prizes that should have AI hype-watchers paying attention,” Nature Digital Medicine , October 24, 2024, doi:10.1038/s41746-024-01345-9, frames the Physics and Chemistry 2024 prizes as a paired institutional certification of the deep-learning program. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-hinton-nobel-physics-2024-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  395. Russell I. Haley, “Benefit Segmentation: A Decision-Oriented Research Tool,” Journal of Marketing 32, no. 3 (1968): 30–35; Haley, Developing Effective Communications Strategy: A Benefit Segmentation Approach (Wiley, 1985). Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/haley-benefit-segmentation-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  396. Rust, Roland T., Katherine N. Lemon, and Valarie A. Zeithaml, “Return on Marketing: Using Customer Equity to Focus Marketing Strategy,” Journal of Marketing 68, no. 1 (January 2004): 109–127, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkg.68.1.109.24030 . The canonical academic statement that marketing investment is a financial-asset-management problem; decomposition into value, brand, and relationship equity is the premise of the customer-equity ROI defenses now standard in 2026 marketing-finance work. Cited in: Ch 25
  397. Rust, Zeithaml, Lemon, Driving Customer Equity , Free Press, 2000; Rust, Lemon, Zeithaml, “Return on Marketing: Using Customer Equity to Focus Marketing Strategy,” JM 68(1), 2004, 109-127; Value/Brand/Retention three-driver decomposition, stated-preference calibration across five U.S. airlines. Cited in: Ch 24
  398. S. Dunn, M. Nenycz-Thiel, C. Graham, J. Dawes, E. Riebe (Danenberg), A. Tanusondjaja, G. Trinh, and S. McColl, “How Categories Grow: The Behavioural Drivers of Revenue Growth,” Journal of Business Research 195 (2025): Article 115385, doi:10.1016/j.jbusres.2025.115385, ScienceDirect . Ehrenberg-Bass Institute (University of South Australia) collaboration with London South Bank University and Massey Business School. Dataset: 13 consecutive years of Nielsen U.S. household-panel data (2007-2019) across 474 distinct consumer-packaged-goods categories, ~5,500 category-year observations. Four-driver decomposition (penetration, purchase frequency, volume per trip, price per volume) of category-revenue growth and decline. Headline empirical findings: (a) penetration changes and price-per-volume changes jointly account for the majority of category-revenue movement; (b) category size moderates which driver dominates—low-penetration categories (under ~40% of households) grow primarily through new buyer acquisition, high-penetration categories (above ~70%) grow primarily through price-per-volume gains; (c) penetration loss is the leading driver of category-revenue decline across every category-size band, including mature well-penetrated categories. Practitioner walk-through with figures: NewNeuromarketing summary ; Ehrenberg-Bass marketingscience.info . SSRN working-paper version: SSRN abstract 4291485 . Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/dunn-nenycz-thiel-how-categories-grow-2025-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  399. S. Fournier, “Consumers and Their Brands: Developing Relationship Theory in Consumer Research,” Journal of Consumer Research 24, no. 4 (1998): 343-373. Fifteen brand-relationship forms inducted from three ethnographic cases; six-dimension Brand Relationship Quality construct (love/passion, self-connection, commitment, interdependence, partner quality, intimacy). Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/fournier-holt-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  400. Salganik, Dodds, Watts, “Experimental Study of Inequality and Unpredictability in an Artificial Cultural Market,” Science 311, no. 5762 (Feb 10, 2006): 854–856 (MusicLab; 14,341 participants; eight parallel social-influence worlds plus a no-influence control; eight different rank-order winners from the same 48 songs; cross-world rank correlations below 0.5); Watts and Dodds, “Influentials, Networks, and Public Opinion Formation,” JCR 34, no. 4 (Dec 2007): 441–458; Bakshy, Hofman, Mason, Watts, “Everyone's an Influencer: Quantifying Influence on Twitter,” WSDM '11 (2011): 65–74 (74M diffusion events; R² ≈ 0.32 upper bound on per-seed cascade prediction); Goel, Anderson, Hofman, Watts, “The Structural Virality of Online Diffusion,” Management Science 62, no. 1 (2016): 180–196 (median cascade depth 1; only ~5% structurally viral). Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/watts-dodds-influentials-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  401. Salvi, F., Bonatti, L., et al. (2025). On the Conversational Persuasiveness of GPT-4. Nature Human Behaviour , advance online publication, nature.com/articles/s41562-025-02194-6 (820-participant RCT debating GPT-4 or another human across 30 sociopolitical topics, half the conditions giving the persuader access to the counterparty’s basic demographic information; GPT-4 with personalization had 81.2% higher odds of post-debate agreement than human debaters; without personalization the AI’s advantage shrank to a directionally positive but smaller effect). Coverage at MIT Technology Review (May 19, 2025), technologyreview.com/2025/05/19/1116779/ai-can-do-a-better-job-of-persuading-people-than-we-do. Cited in: Ch 23
  402. Satornino, C. B., Allen, A. M., Shi, H., & Bolander, W. (2023). Understanding the Performance Effects of “Dark” Salesperson Traits. Journal of Marketing 87(2), 298-315 (n>800; three-attribute misconduct pathology, dark-triad amplification). Hunter, J. P., & Schepker, D. J. (2023). Compensation Design and Sales-Force Misconduct. AMJ 66(4) (fleet sales, n=437). Groysberg, B., Hunter, J. P., & Schepker, D. J. (2024). Audit-by-Design and Misconduct Detection Lag. AMJ . Tayan, B. (2019). Wells Fargo Cross-Selling Scandal. Stanford Closer Look CGRP-62. Cited in: Ch 23
  403. Satornino, C. B., Allen, A., Shi, H., & Bolander, W. (2023). Understanding the performance effects of “dark” salesperson traits. Journal of Marketing , 87(2), 298-315. Cited in: Ch 23
  404. Saumure, De Freitas, and Puntoni, "Humor as a Window into Generative AI Bias," Scientific Reports , 15, January 2025, article 1326, doi:10.1038/s41598-024-83384-6. Cited in: Ch 4
  405. Schick, Dwivedi-Yu, Dessì, Raileanu, Lomeli, Zettlemoyer, Cancedda, Scialom, “Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools,” arXiv:2302.04761, submitted February 9, 2023, NeurIPS 2023 oral ( arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761 ); Meta AI Research publication page ( ai.meta.com/research/publications ); OpenReview camera-ready ( openreview.net ). The four learning signals (which API to call, when to call it, what arguments to pass, how to incorporate the result into future token prediction) Schick et al. named in §3 are the same four signals OpenAI Function Calling (June 2023), Anthropic tool use (May 2024), and the Model Context Protocol’s tools/call request (November 2024) expose at successively higher protocol layers. Empirical wedge: 6.7B GPT-J fine-tuned with the Toolformer pipeline matched or beat GPT-3 (175B, ~26x larger) on LAMA TREx and SQuAD, MLQA cross-lingual QA, and the ASDiv/SVAMP/MAWPS math word problems (~30 percentage-point lift on arithmetic with the calculator tool); the perplexity-reduction filter in stage three is the conceptual contribution that survives the scale-up to frontier-model training. Successor distillations to smaller language models: ToolLLM, Gorilla, Salesforce xLAM-2-8B (2024-2025). Toolkit synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schick-toolformer-2023-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  406. Schiffman, S. (1987-2014). Cold Calling Techniques (That Really Work!) . Adams Media. Schiffman, S. (1991, re-issued 2008). The 25 Sales Habits of Highly Successful Salespeople . Adams Media. D.E.I. Management Group (founded 1979). Cited in: Ch 23
  407. Schmitz, C. (2005). International Sales Management for Industrial Goods (St. Gallen). Workman, J. P., Homburg, C., & Jensen, O. (2002). Intraorganizational determinants of key account management effectiveness. Journal of Marketing 66(2), 38-60. Schmitz, C., Huckemann, M., Ergun, M., & Hohmann, S. (2021). Hybrid Selling: Mehr Vertriebsproduktivität durch synchronisierte Vor-Ort- und Online-Besuche (n=777 DACH; Bochum Sales Management, Mercuri, BDVM). Bongers, F., Schumann, J. H., & Schmitz, C. (2021). The role of bonded influence techniques in B2B salesperson role redesign. JPSSM 41(2), 150-166 (qualitative role redesign under digital channel introduction). Sleep, S., Dixon, A. L., DeCarlo, T. E., & Lam, S. K. (2020). The inside sales taxonomy and four-configuration partition. European Journal of Marketing 54(5), 1025-1060. McKinsey & Company. (2024). B2B Pulse Survey (n~3,500): rule of thirds across deal stages, 36% Seekers archetype, 54% supplier-switch on poor omnichannel experience. Cited in: Ch 23
  408. Schmitz, C., et al. (2024). The contingent effects of innovative digital sales technologies on B2B firms’ financial performance. International Journal of Research in Marketing . Two-class IDST taxonomy (customer-sensing, customer-linking); 2x2 contingent-fit theorem on product- and customer-need complexity. Schmitz et al. (2025), Navigating the AI wave for sales management, IMM . Cited in: Ch 23
  409. Schultz, Hatch, Larsen eds., The Expressive Organization (Oxford UP, 2000); Hatch and Schultz, "Are the Strategic Stars Aligned for Your Corporate Brand?" HBR 79, no. 2 (2001): 128-134. The Copenhagen corporate-branding stream: firm-as-brand co-constructed across vision (what management aspires to), culture (what employees live), and image (what stakeholders perceive); misalignment is the dominant failure mode. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/schultz-hatch-corporate-branding-ch07.md . Cited in: Ch 7
  410. Schwartz, Peter, The Art of the Long View: Planning for the Future in an Uncertain World , rev. ed. (Doubleday, 1996); Wack, Pierre, “Scenarios: Uncharted Waters Ahead,” Harvard Business Review , September-October 1985, pp. 73–89, on the canonical Royal Dutch Shell scenario-planning method later adapted for marketing-budget cycles. Cited in: Ch 25
  411. scikit-learn 1.8 documentation, sklearn.manifold.MDS , SMACOF optimizer with stress_ attribute; cf. R smacof 2.1-7 (October 2024), Mair, De Leeuw, and Groenen, JStatSoft 102, no. 10 (2022): 1–47. Cited in: Ch 6
  412. Sethuraman, Raj, Gerard J. Tellis, and Richard A. Briesch, “How Well Does Advertising Work? Generalizations from Meta-Analysis of Brand Advertising Elasticities,” Journal of Marketing Research 48, no. 3 (June 2011): 457–471, journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1509/jmkr.48.3.457 . Meta-analysis of 751 short-term and 402 long-term DTC brand advertising elasticities from 56 studies (1960–2008): mean short-term elasticity 0.12, mean long-term elasticity 0.24, stable across four decades, only 53 percent of estimates significantly different from zero. Cited in: Ch 25
  413. Shampanier, Mazar, and Ariely, "Zero as a Special Price," Marketing Science , 26(6), 2007, 742-757. Replication caveat: Mazar/Amir/Ariely JMR 2008 failed Many Labs (Verschuere et al. 2018); Shu et al. PNAS 2012 retracted 2021 after Data Colada. Cited in: Ch 4
  414. "Shape-based Assets are Strongest: Benchmarking Distinctive Brand Asset Performance Across Industries," International Journal of Advertising , March 2026 (1,162 assets, 21 categories, 4 countries). doi:10.1080/02650487.2026.2637295 Cited in: Ch 4
  415. Shapiro, MacInnis, and Heckler, "The Effects of Incidental Ad Exposure on the Formation of Consideration Sets," JCR , June 1997, 94-104. Cited in: Ch 4
  416. Sharma et al., "Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models," Anthropic, Oct 20, 2023; arXiv:2310.13548. Cited in: Ch 26
  417. Sharp 2014, Ch. 5; Ehrenberg's Law of Buying Frequencies. Most of any brand's buyers buy it only once or twice in a year. Special K (Sharp 2e, Fig. 2.3): 61% buy 1-2 times/year. Cited in: Ch 7
  418. Sharp 2018, Ch. 6; Sharp 2e, Ch. 4. Double-jeopardy law: smaller brands have fewer buyers AND those buyers are slightly less loyal. Described by McPhee (1963); replicated by Ehrenberg, Goodhardt, Barwise across categories from 1990 onward. Cited in: Ch 7
  419. Sharp, How Brands Grow , Oxford, 2010 (and 2014 paperback Ch 2-4) incorporating Binet & Field 2013 IPA Long & Short of It , Lodish 1995 split-cable meta-analysis, Sharp & Romaniuk 2007 50:30:20 law, and the law of buyer moderation; Ehrenberg, Repeat-Buying , on the NBD-Dirichlet model. Cited in: Ch 24
  420. Sharp, ibid.; Andrew Ehrenberg and the Ehrenberg-Bass Institute series (1959-2014) on empirical generalizations in marketing; IPA Effectiveness Awards database, 1998-2013, summarized in L. Binet and P. Field, The Long and the Short of It (IPA, 2013). Cited in: Ch 2
  421. Shelby D. Hunt, “General Theories and the Fundamental Explananda of Marketing,” Journal of Marketing 47, no. 4 (1983): 9-17; Marketing Theory: Foundations, Controversy, Strategy, Resource-Advantage Theory , 2nd ed. (Routledge, 2010); Hunt and Morgan, “The Comparative Advantage Theory of Competition,” Journal of Marketing 59, no. 2 (1995): 1-15. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hunt-general-theory-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  422. Shinn, Cassano, Berman, Gopinath, Narasimhan, Yao, “Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning,” arXiv:2303.11366, submitted March 20, 2023 (v4 camera-ready October 10, 2023), NeurIPS 2023 ( arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366 ); NeurIPS proceedings ( proceedings.neurips.cc ); reference implementation github.com/noahshinn/reflexion . Three named components: Actor (LLM policy that generates trajectories), Evaluator (scoring function returning sparse reward, exact-match or hand-crafted heuristic or LLM-based), Self-Reflection model (separate LLM call with a different system prompt that converts the sparse reward into a verbal critique). Episodic memory buffer is bounded to 1-3 prior reflections per trial to respect context limits; no weights are updated. Empirical wedge: HumanEval pass@1 80 percent (GPT-4 baseline) to 91 percent with Reflexion; AlfWorld 130 of 134 tasks solved against ReAct-alone baseline at ~22 percentage points lower absolute success; HotpotQA 61 percent to 75 percent across 12 iterative trials on 100 questions, with the Self-Reflection ablation dropping the lift by 8 percentage points. Production descendants: Anthropic evaluator-optimizer pattern (December 2024 Building Effective Agents post), Anyword Predictive Performance Score, LangGraph create_react_agent reflection wiring. Toolkit synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/shinn-reflexion-2023-ch27.md . Cited in: Ch 27
  423. Silvia Bellezza, Neeru Paharia, and Anat Keinan, “Conspicuous Consumption of Time,” Journal of Consumer Research 44, no. 1 (2017): 118-138; Bellezza, Gino, and Keinan, “The Red Sneakers Effect,” JCR 41, no. 1 (2014): 35-54; Keinan and Kivetz, JCR 37, no. 6 (2011): 935-950; Berger and Ward, JCR 37, no. 4 (2010): 555-569; Bain Luxury Report 2024; Society for Consumer Psychology, “Anat Keinan Wins 2026 Distinguished Scientific Contribution Award” (2026). Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/keinan-status-segmentation-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  424. Simmons, Nelson, Simonsohn, “False-Positive Psychology,” Psychological Science , vol. 22, no. 11, 2011, pp. 1359–1366. Cited in: Ch 5
  425. Simonson, “Choice Based on Reasons: Attraction and Compromise Effects,” JCR 16, 2 (1989): 158-174 (4-11 pp across cars/batteries/microwaves/cameras/computers); Simonson and Nye, JEP (1992) (accountability doubles to 8-22 pp); Tversky and Simonson, Management Science (1993); Yang and Lynn, JCR meta (2014) (mean 7.6 pp). Synthesis at canonical/simonson-compromise-effect-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  426. Sjoerd Beugelsdijk and Robbert Maseland, Culture in International Business , 2nd ed. (Cambridge University Press, 2022/2023; 1st ed. 2011). The contemporary canonical textbook synthesizing Hofstede, GLOBE, Schwartz, Hall, and Inglehart-Welzel World Values Survey for international-business research, with the recommendation that researchers and managers report dimension-specific gaps rather than aggregate scalar distance scores. Methodological recommendation in Sjoerd Beugelsdijk, Robbert Maseland, and Andre van Hoorn (with M. Onrust), “The Kogut and Singh National Cultural Distance Index: Time to Start Using It as a Springboard Rather than a Crutch,” Journal of International Business Studies 49 (2018), DOI 10.1057/s41267-018-0150-z; companion empirical anchor already cited at ref-36 (Beugelsdijk, Kostova, Kunst, Spadafora, and van Essen 2018 meta-analysis). 2024 JIBS perspectives essay argues for reorientation around the WVS for consumer-level cultural change. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/beugelsdijk-maseland-culture-in-international-business-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  427. Sjoerd Beugelsdijk et al., “Cultural Distance and Firm Internationalization: A Meta-Analytical Review,” J. Management 44, no. 1 (2018): 89-130 (156 studies, 437 effect sizes; mean r ~ -.03 on subsidiary performance, negligible at parent level). Cited in: Ch 15
  428. Skift, “Marriott Bonvoy Elite Recovery Protocols Update,” June 18, 2024; Cornell Hospitality Quarterly, “Empowerment and Service Recovery in the Age of Agentic AI,” September 2023; HBR , November 2023. Hyatt Empower (2023) and Rosewood Calling Card (1998). Cited in: Ch 9
  429. Sleep, S., Dixon, A. L., DeCarlo, T. E., & Lam, S. K. (2020). The business-to-business inside sales force: roles, configurations and research agenda. European Journal of Marketing , 54(5), 1025-1060, DOI 10.1108/EJM-06-2018-0416. Two-study qualitative inquiry establishing the four-configuration partition (Inside-Lead-Generation, Inside-Account-Management, Inside-Hybrid, Inside-Independent) as the structural taxonomy of the B2B inside salesforce; the configuration choice drives SLA structure, comp mix, and lead-quality definitions. Ahearne, M., Pourmasoudi, M., & Habel, J. (2025). Inside sales structures and firm performance. Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science , 53(5), DOI 10.1007/s11747-025-01085-6 (multi-method panel of 194 firms; higher inside-rep dependence raises interaction volume and lowers per-interaction quality, with net firm-performance effect contingent on customer-exchange demand and managerial observability). Pavilion. (2025). State of the SDR 2025 . SaaStr (2025) practitioner survey: ~36% of B2B firms cut SDR/BDR headcount and ~28% expanded AE headcount over the 2024-2025 window, with displaced work moving to AI-augmented inside-sales tooling and an emerging GTM-Operator role at the RevOps and inside-sales seam. Cited in: Ch 23
  430. Smith, Getting Price Right (Columbia, 2021), Chs 1-3 (strategy-vs-orientation distinction; four cardinal orientations mapped to finance/marketing/pricing science/product management; decision-influence diagnostic). Anderson and Oliver, “Behavior- vs Outcome-Based Salesforce Control,” JM 51, 4 (1987): 76-88. Synthesis at canonical/smith-getting-price-right-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  431. Solove, "A Taxonomy of Privacy," 154 U. Pa. L. Rev. 477 (2006); Understanding Privacy (Harvard UP, 2008); "Artificial Intelligence and Privacy," 77 Fla. L. Rev. 1 (2024); "A Regulatory Roadmap to AI and Privacy," SSRN 4825146 (2024) (200+ FTC §5 cases since 2000). Cited in: Ch 26
  432. Sonia Thompson, “The Five Cs of Inclusive Marketing,” Harvard Business Review , Aug. 2023, and follow-up essays at Inclusion & Marketing , 2023-2026 (customers, consumers, communications, content, culture as the audit dimensions); Pepper Miller and Herb Kemp, What's Black About It? Insights to Increase Your Share of a Changing African American Market (Paramount Market, 2005), and Pepper Miller, The New Black Consumer (2008), the canonical sources on Black-American consumer segmentation; Cassi Pittman Claytor, Black Privilege: Modern Middle-Class Blacks with Credentials and Cash to Spend (Stanford, 2020), and Nielsen, “Black Consumers: Spending Power & Influence,” multiple reports 2018-2025; mirror-window-door framing adapted from Rudine Sims Bishop, “Mirrors, Windows, and Sliding Glass Doors,” Perspectives 6, no. 3 (1990): ix-xi. Levi Strauss & Co. + Lalaland.ai partnership announcement, March 2023, and subsequent backlash documented in Business of Fashion , The Guardian , and Vogue Business ; Mango synthetic-model campaign 2024 in The Guardian , May 2024. Cited in: Ch 15
  433. Spann, Bertini, Koenigsberg, Zeithammer et al. (13 authors), “Algorithmic Pricing: Implications for Marketing Strategy and Regulation,” NBER WP 32540 (Jun 2024, rev. Jun 2025); IJRM (2025). 13-author field-consensus paper: operating definition, four-row taxonomy (traditional/dynamic/personalized/participative), input-rules-output spine, 71-manager EPP survey (25K+ pros), 225-store ESL case, US-EU regulatory map. Synthesis at frontier/spann-bertini-algorithmic-pricing-megapaper-2024-2025-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  434. Sridhar, Naik, and Kelkar, “Modeling the Long-Term Impact of Content Marketing,” Journal of Marketing , 2024; Pauwels et al., observational-versus-experimental marketing-attribution methods, 2018-2025. Cited in: Ch 17
  435. Stefano Puntoni, “AI Is Upending Marketing on Two Fronts,” Harvard Business Review , February 23, 2026 (HBR product code H0931I); London Business School and UCLA study cited within (~20 percent search-volume drop after individual ChatGPT adoption, with information-based sites suffering most). Companion peer-reviewed work: Erik Hermann and Stefano Puntoni, “Generative AI in Marketing and Principles for Ethical Design and Deployment,” Journal of Public Policy & Marketing 44, no. 3 (2025): 332-349 (the ASSURANCE rubric: Autonomy, Security, Sustainability, Representativeness, Accountability, Nonbiasedness, Crediting, Empowerment); Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel, “Conversational AI: The Next Frontier of Digital Platform Monetization,” SSRN 5634270 (October 8, 2025); Hermann, Puntoni, and Schweidel, “Trusting AI Agents: Uncertainty Reduction by Design,” SSRN 6103206 (January 12, 2026). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/frontier/puntoni-ai-upending-marketing-2026-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  436. Stephen J. Hoch, Xavier Drèze, and Mary E. Purk, “EDLP, Hi-Lo, and Margin Arithmetic,” Journal of Marketing 58, no. 4 (1994): 16-27; Hoch, Kim, Montgomery, and Rossi, “Determinants of Store-Level Price Elasticity,” JMR 32, no. 1 (1995): 17-29. Canonical reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hoch-edlp-hilo-ch12.md . Cited in: Ch 12
  437. Stephen King, JWT Planning Guide (J. Walter Thompson London, March 1974), 34-page mimeograph; Judie Lannon and Merry Baskin (eds.), A Master Class in Brand Planning: The Timeless Works of Stephen King (Wiley, 2007), 318pp, ISBN 978-0-470-51791-8 collecting twenty King articles 1967-2002. James Webb Young, A Technique for Producing Ideas (Advertising Publications, 1940; McGraw-Hill Advertising Age Classics Library reissue 2003 with forewords by Bill Bernbach and Keith Reinhard), the five-step ideation framework (gather, work over, drop, surface, shape) and the canonical line "an idea is nothing more or less than a new combination of old elements." Course-internal syntheses: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/king-jwt-planning-ch16.md ; core-course/wiki/external/canonical/webb-young-technique-producing-ideas-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  438. Stephen L. Vargo and Robert F. Lusch, “Evolving to a New Dominant Logic for Marketing,” Journal of Marketing 68, no. 1 (2004): 1-17 (the foundational service-dominant-logic paper); “Service-Dominant Logic: Continuing the Evolution,” Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science 36, no. 1 (2008): 1-10; Lusch and Vargo, Service-Dominant Logic: Premises, Perspectives, Possibilities (Cambridge, 2014). Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/vargo-lusch-sd-logic-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  439. Stephen L. Vargo and Robert F. Lusch, “Evolving to a New Dominant Logic for Marketing,” Journal of Marketing 68, no. 1 (January 2004): 1-17. The service-dominant-logic framing in which goods are vehicles for service delivery and value is co-created with the customer; cited as background for the modern services-marketing premise that the buyer is part of the production. Cited in: Ch 9
  440. Stigler, G. J., “The Economics of Information,” Journal of Political Economy 69, 3 (1961): 213-225. The foundational paper on search-cost theory: buyers facing a positive marginal cost of one more price quote stop searching when the expected marginal saving from the next quote equals the cost of gathering it, with the equilibrium consequence that price dispersion across sellers is a manifestation and the measure of buyer ignorance in the market, and the one-price equilibrium obtains only when the marginal cost of information is zero. Diamond, P. A., “A Model of Price Adjustment,” Journal of Economic Theory 3, 2 (1971): 156-168 identified the knife-edge result (the Diamond paradox) under which strict positive search costs collapse the Stigler distribution to the monopoly price; Burdett, K., and Judd, K. L., “Equilibrium Price Dispersion,” Econometrica 51, 4 (1983): 955-969 and Reinganum, J. F., “A Simple Model of Equilibrium Price Dispersion,” Journal of Political Economy 87, 4 (1979): 851-858 resolved the paradox by admitting heterogeneous buyer types (some receiving multiple quotes, some receiving one) and recovered the non-degenerate Stigler distribution that the empirical record consistently confirms. Pratt, J. W., Wise, D. A., and Zeckhauser, R., “Price Differences in Almost Competitive Markets,” QJE 93, 2 (1979): 189-211 (Boston-area retail price-dispersion field study, 50 to 100 percent ranges for identical consumer-grocery items); Sorensen, A. T., “Equilibrium Price Dispersion in Retail Markets for Prescription Drugs,” JPE 108, 4 (2000): 833-850 (Suffolk County, NY pharmacy data, larger dispersion for acute-use drugs than chronic-use drugs because acute buyers cannot search); Brown, J. R., and Goolsbee, A., “Does the Internet Make Markets More Competitive? Evidence from the Life Insurance Industry,” JPE 110, 3 (2002): 481-507 (natural experiment in late-1990s comparison-platform spread; 8 to 15 percent price decline and sharp dispersion compression in covered categories, no effect in uncovered categories); Baye, M. R., Morgan, J., and Scholten, P., “Information, Search, and Price Dispersion,” in Handbook of Economics and Information Systems (Elsevier, 2006), Vol. 1: 323-375 (online-market price-dispersion synthesis: 10 to 20 percent dispersions persist across commodity online categories because sellers compete on non-price dimensions when price becomes transparent); Hortacsu, A., and Syverson, C., “Product Differentiation, Search Costs, and Competition in the Mutual Fund Industry,” QJE 119, 2 (2004): 403-456 (S&P 500 index-fund expense-ratio dispersion despite identical underlying portfolios). The search-cost framework is the upstream foundation under EDLP-vs-Hi-Lo channel positioning (ref-49), personalized pricing (ref-36, ref-50), yield management and Uber surge (ref-13, ref-15, ref-27), and the algorithmic-collusion regulatory concern (ref-41), each of which measures Stigler's mechanism at a different resolution. Synthesis at canonical/stigler-economics-information-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  441. Stuart Russell, Human Compatible: Artificial Intelligence and the Problem of Control (Viking, October 8, 2019; ISBN 978-0-525-55861-3; 352 pp.). The standard-model critique, the King Midas analogy, and the three principles of beneficial AI (machine's only objective is to maximize the realization of human preferences; machine is initially uncertain about what those preferences are; ultimate source of information about human preferences is human behavior). Off-switch theorem in formal form: Russell, "Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence," in Human-Like Machine Intelligence , ed. Stephen Muggleton and Nicholas Charter (Oxford UP, 2021), Ch. 1; preprint at people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~russell/papers/mi19book-hcai.pdf. Russell directs the Center for Human-Compatible AI (CHAI) at UC Berkeley (humancompatible.ai/); the recommender-system / engagement-maximization argument runs through the second half of the book and is the marketing-side bridge to §26.5. Publisher record at penguinrandomhouse.com/books/566677/human-compatible-by-stuart-russell/; bibliographic anchor en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human_Compatible. Reader: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/russell-human-compatible-ch26.md . Cited in: Ch 26
  442. Sunaina Banerjee and Oleg Urminsky, "The Language That Drives Engagement: A Systematic Large-scale Analysis of Headline Experiments," Marketing Science 44(3), 2024, INFORMS (pubsonline.informs.org/doi/10.1287/mksc.2021.0018; open-access preprint home.uchicago.edu/ourminsky/Banerjee_Urminsky_Headlines.pdf). Systematic large-scale text-feature analysis of 8,977 A/B-tested headline pairs drawn from the Upworthy Research Archive across cognitive, motivational, affective, and grammatical dimensions; principal finding is a curvilinear (inverted-U) relationship between concreteness and click-through, with the optimum in a middle band that names the domain and stakes without resolution disclosure. Replication of the curvilinear shape: Sirola, Rusko, Kakkonen, and colleagues, "When curiosity gaps backfire: effects of headline concreteness on information selection decisions," Scientific Reports 14, Nature Portfolio, 2024 (nature.com/articles/s41598-024-81575-9). Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/ai-headline-craft-empirical-2024-2026-ch16.md . Cited in: Ch 16
  443. Sunstein, "Sludge Audits," Behavioural Public Policy 6, no. 4 (2022): 654-673. Sunstein, Sludge (MIT Press, 2022). Empirical application: Rockwell, Tabriz, Crane et al., "A Sludge Audit for Health System Colorectal Cancer Screening," Annals of Family Medicine (2023). Cited in: Ch 26
  444. Susan Fournier, “Consumers and Their Brands,” Journal of Consumer Research 24, no. 4 (1998): 343-373. Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/fournier-ch02.md . Cited in: Ch 2
  445. Susan P. Douglas and Yoram Wind, “The Myth of Globalization,” Columbia Journal of World Business 22, no. 4 (1987): 19-29; Roland Robertson, “Glocalization: Time-Space and Homogeneity-Heterogeneity,” in Featherstone, Lash, and Robertson, eds., Global Modernities (Sage, 1995). Course-internal synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/canonical/douglas-wind-myth-globalization-ch15.md . Cited in: Ch 15
  446. Sylvia Hristakeva, Jūra Liaukonytė, and Leo Feler, "How GLP-1 Adoption Reshapes Household Grocery Purchasing," Journal of Marketing Research , December 2025, doi:10.1177/00222437251412834, https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/00222437251412834 (SSRN preprint abstract_id=5073929; difference-in-differences household-panel design; 5.3% grocery decline within six months of GLP-1 adoption, 10.1% in savory snacks, 8.0% at fast-food / coffee / limited-service restaurants, 8.2% grocery decline in higher-income adopting households). Companion sources: Numerator GLP-1 Trends Hub (~30,000 panelists per quarter; ~23% of U.S. households containing a current or recent GLP-1 user by late 2025; 16% YoY new-prescription lift), https://www.numerator.com/press/numerator-glp-1-hub-reveals-purchase-trends-from-30k-consumers/ Cornell Chronicle, December 2025, https://news.cornell.edu/stories/2025/12/ozempic-changing-foods-americans-buy (food-noise construct as interoceptive cue layer underneath cue-driven purchase); Hopp et al., "How GLP-1 Medications Are Changing Consumer Behavior," HBR , October 2025, https://hbr.org/2025/10/how-glp-1-medications-are-changing-consumer-behavior (four buyer-behavior pivots: smaller basket sizes, protein and fiber growth, planned-vs-impulse asymmetry, brand-loyalty erosion in calorie-dense categories); Mondelez North America Q1 2026 earnings disclosure (4.1% net revenue decline, CEO-attributed to staples shift); Walmart Q3 2025 disclosure (GLP-1 Nutrition Zones in 1,200 stores) and April 16, 2026 Walmart corporate announcement of dedicated weight-management platform, https://corporate.walmart.com/news/2026/04/16/walmart-expands-access-to-weight-management-support-services-for-customers-on-or-exploring-glp-1s Course reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/glp1-buyer-behavior-shift-2025-2026-ch04.md . Cited in: Ch 4
  447. Teece, Pisano, and Shuen, "Dynamic Capabilities and Strategic Management," SMJ 18, no. 7 (1997); Teece, "Explicating Dynamic Capabilities," SMJ 28, no. 13 (2007), the sensing/seizing/transforming triad. Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/teece-dynamic-capabilities-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  448. Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991, 47 U.S.C. § 227; FCC ruling, “In the Matter of Targeting and Eliminating Unlawful Text Messages,” CG Docket No. 21-402, March 2023; relevant case law including Facebook v. Duguid (Supreme Court, 2021) on auto-dialer scope. Cited in: Ch 21
  449. Tellis, "The Price Elasticity of Selective Demand: A Meta-Analysis of Econometric Models of Sales," JMR 25, 4 (1988): 331-341 (367 elasticities; price ~8x more elastic than advertising); Bijmolt, van Heerde, and Pieters, "New Empirical Generalizations on the Determinants of Price Elasticity," JMR 42, 2 (2005): 141-156 (1,851 elasticities, 81 studies, mean -2.62); Hanssens (ed.), Empirical Generalizations about Marketing Impact , 2nd ed. (MSI, 2015). Synthesis at canonical/tellis-bijmolt-elasticity-meta-ch10.md . Cited in: Ch 10
  450. Thaler, "Mental Accounting and Consumer Choice," Marketing Science , 4(3), Summer 1985, 199-214. Cited in: Ch 4
  451. The deep-learning lineage. David E. Rumelhart, Geoffrey E. Hinton, and Ronald J. Williams, “Learning representations by back-propagating errors,” Nature 323, no. 6088 (October 9, 1986): 533–536, doi:10.1038/323533a0. Yann LeCun, Léon Bottou, Yoshua Bengio, and Patrick Haffner, “Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition,” Proceedings of the IEEE 86, no. 11 (November 1998): 2278–2324, doi:10.1109/5.726791. Geoffrey E. Hinton, Simon Osindero, and Yee-Whye Teh, “A fast learning algorithm for deep belief nets,” Neural Computation 18, no. 7 (July 2006): 1527–1554, doi:10.1162/neco.2006.18.7.1527. Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey E. Hinton, “ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks,” NIPS 2012 (December 2012). Yann LeCun, Yoshua Bengio, and Geoffrey Hinton, “Deep Learning,” Nature 521, no. 7553 (May 28, 2015): 436–444, doi:10.1038/nature14539. Association for Computing Machinery, “2018 ACM A.M. Turing Award” announcement, March 27, 2019, awards.acm.org/about/2018-turing . Synthesis at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/hinton-lecun-bengio-deep-learning-ch01.md . Cited in: Ch 1
  452. The open-source collaboration primitives are codified in GitHub's documentation: issues ( docs.github.com/issues ), pull requests ( docs.github.com/pull-requests ), and forks ( docs.github.com/get-started/quickstart/fork-a-repo ). The five public repositories named in the Ch 0 walkthrough are github.com/anthropics/skills , github.com/anthropics/anthropic-cookbook , github.com/modelcontextprotocol/servers , github.com/awesome-selfhosted/awesome-selfhosted , and github.com/anthropics/claude-code . The awesome-* convention originates with Sindre Sorhus's awesome meta-list (2014) and now spans thousands of curated topic indexes. Cited in: Ch 1
  453. Theodore Levitt, “Marketing Intangible Products and Product Intangibles,” Harvard Business Review , May-June 1981, pp. 94-102. The original framing of the “tangibilize the intangible” principle that became the physical-evidence P in services-marketing literature. Cited in: Ch 9
  454. Theodore Levitt, “Marketing Myopia,” Harvard Business Review , July-August 1960 (reprinted Sept-Oct 1975 with retrospective commentary). Cited in: Ch 11
  455. Theodore Levitt, “Marketing Myopia,” Harvard Business Review , July-August 1960, pp. 45-56. Cited in: Ch 2
  456. Theodore Levitt, “The Globalization of Markets,” HBR , May-June 1983 (convergence-and-standardization argument); Susan P. Douglas and Yoram Wind, “The Myth of Globalization,” Columbia Journal of World Business 22, no. 4 (1987): 19-29 (localization reply). Cited in: Ch 15
  457. Thomas and Morwitz, "Penny Wise and Pound Foolish," JCR 32, 1 (2005): 54-64; Prelec and Loewenstein, "The Red and the Black," Marketing Science 17, 1 (1998): 4-28. Cited in: Ch 10
  458. TikTok Engineering Blog, “How the For You Algorithm Works,” 2022; upd 2024-25. Architecture: Liu et al., “Monolith: Real Time Recommendation System with Collisionless Embedding Table,” arXiv 2209.07663 (RecSys 2022); collisionless embedding tables, real-time parameter-server training, reliability-vs-real-time tradeoff. UW 120-day instrumented panel: average daily time 29 to 50 min day 1 to day 120, with 30-50% of the first 1,000 videos already personalized to the user's predicted preferences. Algorithmic-persistence audits (arXiv 2410.13124, 2504.13895; Lim/Han et al. AAAI ICWSM 2024): explicit user resets via Not Interested have minimal durable effect, the recommender's classification is sticky. Synthesis: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/tiktok-algorithm-monolith-ch19.md . Cited in: Ch 19
  459. Toubia et al. (Twin-2K-500), Marketing Science (2025); Peng, Gui, Merlau, Toubia, Netzer et al., “Digital Twins as Funhouse Mirrors,” arXiv 2509.19088 (2025); Brand, Israeli, and Ngwe, HBS WP 23-062 (2023, rev. 2025); Korst, Puntoni, and Toubia, HBR , May-June 2025. Frontier reader: core-course/wiki/external/frontier/synthetic-respondents-segmentation-ch06.md . Cited in: Ch 6
  460. Toubia, Gui, Peng, Merlau, Li, Chen, “Twin-2K-500,” Marketing Science 44(6), 2025, 1446-1455 (arXiv 2505.17479); 2,058-person four-wave Prolific panel, 500 questions, Wave 4 test-retest floor; on Hugging Face as the digital-twin benchmark. Cited in: Ch 24
  461. Tushman and Anderson, "Technological Discontinuities and Organizational Environments," ASQ 31, no. 3 (1986); Anderson and Tushman, ASQ 35, no. 4 (1990); Tushman and O'Reilly, Winning Through Innovation (HBSP, 2002). Canonical reader at core-course/wiki/external/canonical/tushman-anderson-technological-discontinuities-ch03.md . Cited in: Ch 3
  462. U.S. automotive franchise law (DOJ Antitrust Division; Mercatus Center); Tesla and Ford direct-sales litigation ( JEP 24, no. 3); Nike Consumer Direct Offense and post-2023 wholesale walkback in RetailWire coverage. Cited in: Ch 11
  463. U.S. Census Bureau, Quarterly Retail E-commerce Sales reports, 2024-2026 series; e-commerce share of total retail of approximately 16 percent in early 2026. Cited in: Ch 11
  464. Valarie A. Zeithaml, Leonard L. Berry, and A. Parasuraman, “The Behavioral Consequences of Service Quality,” Journal of Marketing 60, no. 2 (April 1996): 31-46, on the linkage between zone-of-tolerance position and customer behavioral outcomes (loyalty, switching, complaint, word-of-mouth). Cited in: Ch 9
  465.
